opts.text: Apply soft wrist and ankle restraints to prevent injury during convulsions
opts.rat: Restraining a seizing patient causes more harm than benefit (fracture, dislocation, and worsens autonomic stress). Pad rails, move furniture, and let the seizure run its course while you treat it pharmacologically. Restraints have no role here.

opts.text: Administer IV phenytoin 1 g rapid push over 5 minutes for fastest control
opts.rat: Maximum phenytoin infusion rate is 50 mg/min (25 mg/min elderly/cardiac). Faster causes hypotension, arrhythmia, asystole. If using phenytoin (vs fosphenytoin), it must run slowly with cardiac monitoring.

opts.text: Order an LP immediately to rule out meningitis
opts.rat: LP is contraindicated in actively seizing patient and only considered after stabilization, imaging, and clinical suspicion of CNS infection (fever, meningismus). Not part of the first 20-minute algorithm.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Position patient (left lateral decubitus or head elevated as feasible), suction airway, ensure BVM with 100% O2, attach continuous cardiac/SpO2/capnography monitoring; verify IV patency, check fingerstick glucose
opts.rat: FIRST - airway protection and rapid metabolic assessment. The fingerstick glucose takes 15 seconds and rules out the easiest reversible cause. Cannot give meds reliably without secured IV.

opts.text: Administer IV lorazepam 4 mg now; document time; if seizure persists at 5 min, repeat lorazepam 4 mg once
opts.rat: SECOND - first-line pharmacologic therapy. Time-stamped. Repeat dose is allowed once before escalating to second-line.

opts.text: While benzodiazepine is taking effect, prepare and administer second-line agent: fosphenytoin 20 PE/kg IV (or levetiracetam 60 mg/kg) loading dose; continue cardiac monitoring during fosphenytoin infusion (watch for hypotension/arrhythmia)
opts.rat: THIRD - second-line load. If seizure broke with benzo, the second-line agent is still given to prevent recurrence and to provide bridge therapy. If seizure continues, this is the next-step active treatment.

opts.text: If still seizing after second-line agent: prepare for RSI and intubation; initiate anesthetic infusion (propofol 1-2 mg/kg bolus then 30-200 mcg/kg/min, OR midazolam 0.2 mg/kg bolus then 0.05-2 mg/kg/hr); transfer to ICU; place on continuous EEG
opts.rat: FOURTH - refractory SE management. Requires intubation because anesthetic doses suppress respiratory drive. Continuous EEG is essential because subclinical/non-convulsive status can persist after motor activity stops.

opts.text: Order CT head, draw full labs (CMP, Mg, Ca, phosphorus, AED levels, troponin, urine drug screen), insert Foley to monitor UO (rhabdo concern), update wife/family on diagnosis and ICU plan, restart home AED with bridge plan
opts.rat: LAST in immediate sequence - workup and stabilization care. Imaging needed to rule out structural cause. Foley + IV fluids + UA for myoglobin address rhabdomyolysis (CK 1,840 already concerning - aggressive hydration prevents AKI). Family communication and AED bridging prevent recurrence.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After lorazepam x2, fosphenytoin loading, and ICU transfer, classify each finding over the next several hours.

opts.text: Convulsive activity stopped within 3 minutes of second lorazepam dose; SpO2 98% on 4L NC; HR 88; pH normalized to 7.36; lactate cleared from 8.4 to 1.6 over 4 hours
opts.rat: Successful first-line response. Resolution of acidosis and lactate clearance confirm cessation of muscular hyperactivity. Excellent outcome - patient avoided refractory status.

opts.text: Patient awake, oriented x3 within 90 minutes of seizure cessation, conversational, asking for water; able to give history of running out of medications; no focal deficits on neuro exam; CT head negative
opts.rat: Rapid post-ictal recovery is favorable. Negative CT rules out the major structural causes. Focus shifts to AED bridging, education, and prior auth resolution to prevent recurrence.

opts.text: CK trending up to 4,200; UO clear yellow at 80 mL/hr with aggressive IV fluids; urine myoglobin negative; creatinine stable at 1.1
opts.rat: Rhabdomyolysis is being managed successfully. CK rise is expected (peaks 24-72 hr after muscular event); the goal is preserved renal function via aggressive IV hydration. Negative myoglobin and good UO confirm prevention of pigment-induced AKI.

opts.text: Continuous EEG shows ongoing electrographic seizures despite cessation of motor activity; patient unresponsive 60 minutes after last visible convulsion
opts.rat: NON-CONVULSIVE STATUS EPILEPTICUS (NCSE). The brain is still seizing without visible motor signs. Common after refractory convulsive SE - up to 48% have ongoing electrographic seizures. Requires continued/escalated antiepileptic therapy guided by EEG. Easy to miss without continuous EEG.

opts.text: After 36 hours on midazolam infusion: BP 78/42 requiring norepinephrine; new-onset metabolic acidosis with elevated triglycerides; lactate rising; CK 28,000
opts.rat: PROPOFOL INFUSION SYNDROME (PRIS) if on propofol, or sedation-related complications generally. PRIS triad: metabolic acidosis + rhabdomyolysis + cardiac/hemodynamic collapse. Switch agents, ICU escalation, supportive care. Mortality up to 80% if not recognized early. Monitor lipids/lactate/CK on prolonged anesthetic drips.

opts.text: Repeat creatinine 2.4, oliguric, dark cola-colored urine, urine myoglobin strongly positive
opts.rat: PIGMENT-INDUCED AKI from rhabdomyolysis. Treatment: continue aggressive IV fluids (target UO 200-300 mL/hr), consider sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine (controversial), may require dialysis. Avoid nephrotoxins.

opts.text: Wife verbalizes understanding of importance of medication adherence; case manager working on emergency 30-day supply through pharmacy assistance program; epilepsy nurse navigator scheduled outpatient follow-up; patient engaged with safety planning (driving restrictions, swimming, working with heights)
opts.rat: Critical psychosocial outcome. Recurrence prevention requires solving the medication access problem AND education on triggers. Driving laws vary by state but most require seizure-free interval before resuming. Status epilepticus increases lifetime mortality - prevention is essential.

opts.text: Mild persistent headache, fatigue, body aches, slight word-finding difficulty resolving over 24-48 hours
opts.rat: Expected post-ictal symptoms. Generalized seizures cause widespread neuronal disturbance plus muscle soreness from convulsions. As long as symptoms are improving on their own (not worsening), this is normal recovery, not deterioration. Reassure patient and family.

title: Acute Limb Ischemia (Embolic)
subtitle: 67 y/o female with atrial fibrillation, sudden severe right leg pain
nursesNote: 1115 - 67 y/o female arrived in ED via private vehicle with sudden onset severe right leg pain that began approximately 4 hours ago while watching TV. PMH significant for atrial fibrillation (diagnosed 3 years ago), CHF (EF 38%), HTN, T2DM. Patient stopped taking apixaban 8 days ago because 'it was too expensive this month and I felt fine.' Pain described as constant, severe (10/10), starting in the right thigh and now involving the entire leg below the mid-thigh; not relieved by repositioning. On exam: right leg pale and mottled below mid-thigh, cool to touch (markedly cooler than left), no palpable femoral, popliteal, posterior tibial, or dorsalis pedis pulse on right; Doppler reveals faint femoral signal but no popliteal or distal signals. Left leg warm with intact pulses throughout. Patient reports numbness and tingling in right foot and toes; cannot dorsiflex or plantarflex right foot when asked. Calf soft on palpation but exquisitely tender. Vascular surgery paged emergently; OR being notified; heparin drip preparation underway.

labs.v: Atrial fibrillation rate 118, no acute ischemic changes

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish acute limb ischemia with threatened limb viability.

opts.text: Sudden severe pain with pallor, mottling, cool extremity, and absent palpable pulses (no femoral, popliteal, PT, or DP on right) - 4 of the 6 P's present
opts.rat: The 6 P's of acute limb ischemia: Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Poikilothermia (coolness). Sudden onset distinguishes acute (hours) from chronic ischemia (months). Multiple P's plus absent Doppler signals = surgical emergency. Rutherford classification IIb (immediately threatened) requires emergent revascularization within hours.

opts.text: Paresthesia (numbness/tingling) and paralysis (inability to dorsiflex/plantarflex right foot) - the 5th and 6th P's signaling motor/sensory nerve involvement
opts.rat: These are the LATE P's and indicate Rutherford IIb (immediately threatened) or III (irreversible) limb. Motor nerves die before muscle - loss of motor function signals critical time window. Once paralysis is established, the clock is running out. Mortality and amputation rates climb sharply after 6 hours of ischemia.

opts.text: Likely embolic source: atrial fibrillation with 8 days off apixaban anticoagulation, sudden onset, no prior claudication history
opts.rat: AFib with anticoagulation lapse is the most common embolic source for acute limb ischemia. Sudden onset (vs gradual claudication progression) and no prior PAD history distinguish embolism from in-situ thrombosis. The clot likely originated in the left atrial appendage. Treatment urgency and approach depend on this distinction.

opts.text: Heart rate 118 irregular
opts.rat: Confirms the underlying AFib (the embolic source) but is a chronic finding, not a cue establishing limb ischemia itself. Important context, not diagnostic of ALI.

opts.text: Lactate 3.8 mmol/L
opts.rat: Reflects tissue hypoperfusion - supports severity but is a downstream marker, not a primary diagnostic cue. The clinical exam (6 P's) establishes ALI before any lab returns.

opts.text: Blood pressure 162/94
opts.rat: Reflects sympathetic response to severe pain and underlying HTN - not specific to ALI. Many conditions produce this BP.

opts.text: Glucose 184 mg/dL
opts.rat: Stress hyperglycemia in a diabetic - background data, not an ALI cue.

opts.text: CK 486 (rising)
opts.rat: Early rhabdomyolysis from muscle ischemia - a CONSEQUENCE of ALI, not what establishes the diagnosis. Important to track but follows the clinical diagnosis.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to underlying pathophysiology and management.

opts.text: Skeletal muscle tolerates ischemia for approximately 4-6 hours before irreversible necrosis begins; nerves are even more sensitive (paralysis precedes muscle death); the 6-hour window from symptom onset is the critical determinant of limb salvage
opts.rat: The 'golden 6 hours' concept. Patient is at 4 hours - within window but closing fast. Every minute of delay reduces salvage chance. Why ALI is a 'time is tissue' emergency comparable to stroke or STEMI. Lewis Med-Surg 11e p. 805.

opts.text: Immediate IV heparin anticoagulation (typically 80 units/kg bolus then 18 units/kg/hr drip) prevents clot propagation and protects collateral circulation while definitive revascularization is arranged - it does NOT dissolve the existing clot
opts.rat: Heparin is the first medical step in essentially every ALI case (unless contraindicated). Prevents the clot from extending proximally and distally, preserving any collateral flow. Definitive treatment is surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed thrombolysis. Goal aPTT 60-80 seconds.

opts.text: After revascularization, REPERFUSION INJURY can cause sudden severe hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis with pigment-induced AKI, and compartment syndrome - the patient is at highest risk in the first few hours post-revascularization
opts.rat: Critical post-op nursing concept. The same toxic metabolites that accumulated during ischemia now flood the circulation. Pre-emptive monitoring of K+, pH, CK, urine color, and compartment pressures is the standard. Death from sudden hyperkalemia during reperfusion is a documented complication.

opts.text: The affected limb should be elevated above heart level to reduce edema and improve perfusion
opts.rat: BACKWARDS for ALI. Elevation REDUCES arterial perfusion to an already ischemic limb. Position the limb DEPENDENT (lower than heart) to improve gravity-assisted arterial inflow until revascularization. Elevation is for venous problems (DVT, venous stasis), not arterial.

opts.text: Warming pads should be applied to the cool limb to restore temperature and improve circulation
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. External warming of an ischemic limb increases metabolic demand without improving supply, accelerating tissue death. Keep the limb at room temperature; do NOT apply heat. Also do not apply ice (further vasoconstriction). The limb is cool because there's no blood flow - fixing the blood flow is the only treatment.

opts.text: Aspirin should replace heparin as the anticoagulant of choice in acute embolic limb ischemia
opts.rat: WRONG. Aspirin is antiplatelet (chronic prevention) - inadequate for acute embolic ischemia. Heparin is therapeutic anticoagulation and the standard. Aspirin has a role in long-term secondary prevention after revascularization, not in acute management.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the most likely causes from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Cardiogenic embolus from atrial fibrillation (left atrial appendage thrombus) after anticoagulation lapse
opts.rat: Classic clinical picture: AFib + missed anticoagulation + sudden onset + no PAD history + single-limb involvement. Embolic source is the working diagnosis.

opts.text: In-situ thrombosis of pre-existing atherosclerotic peripheral artery disease
opts.rat: Always on the differential. Less likely without prior claudication and gradual symptoms, but T2DM and HTN are risk factors. CT angiography would distinguish.

opts.text: Aortic dissection with extension into iliac/femoral artery
opts.rat: Sudden severe limb ischemia with HTN - dissection must be considered. No back/chest pain reported, but extension can produce limb ischemia as a presenting feature. CT angio would diagnose.

opts.text: Paradoxical embolus through a patent foramen ovale (PFO)
opts.rat: Less common but real - venous thrombus crosses into arterial circulation through PFO. Less likely without DVT history but echocardiogram during workup would assess.

opts.text: Trauma-related arterial injury
opts.rat: No history of trauma reported. Lowest on the differential but always worth screening for.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions in the next 30-60 minutes.

opts.text: Administer IV heparin bolus 80 units/kg, then continuous drip at 18 units/kg/hr; check aPTT in 6 hours; document time of administration prominently
opts.rat: First-line medical therapy. Prevents propagation while OR is mobilized. Weight-based dosing is standard. Document timing because all subsequent decisions (lytic therapy, surgery) interact with anticoagulation status.

opts.text: Activate vascular surgery for emergent revascularization (surgical embolectomy with Fogarty catheter OR catheter-directed thrombolysis); coordinate OR/IR and anesthesia; obtain CT angiography if it will not delay definitive treatment
opts.rat: Time is tissue. Rutherford IIb (this patient - paralysis present) needs revascularization within hours. Embolectomy is preferred for embolic source; catheter-directed thrombolysis better for in-situ thrombosis. CT angio is preferred but not at the cost of delay.

opts.text: Keep limb at room temperature (no heat, no ice), in dependent position; provide aggressive IV opioid analgesia; mark pulses with skin pen at frequent intervals; ensure IV access and pre-op labs/consent in process
opts.rat: Supportive measures and OR readiness. Dependent positioning maximizes arterial inflow. Pain management is essential and humane. Pulse marking allows serial reassessment of progression. Pre-op preparation runs in parallel.

opts.text: Elevate the affected leg on 2 pillows to reduce edema
opts.rat: WORSENS arterial perfusion. Elevation reduces inflow to an already ischemic limb. Position dependent for ALI. NCLEX trap (elevation works for venous problems, opposite for arterial).

opts.text: Apply warm compresses to the affected leg to restore temperature
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. Increases metabolic demand of dying tissue without improving supply. Room temperature only. No heat or cold.

opts.text: Administer IV cardizem (diltiazem) to slow the AFib rate, then await heparin therapeutic range
opts.rat: Rate control is reasonable for AFib management but is NOT a time-critical priority in ALI. The limb is the immediate emergency; AFib management is concurrent but secondary. Avoid actions that delay revascularization.

opts.text: Order tPA IV bolus for systemic thrombolysis
opts.rat: Systemic tPA is NOT first-line for ALI - bleeding risk exceeds benefit. Catheter-directed thrombolysis (delivered locally via IR catheter) is the lytic option when chosen. Surgical embolectomy is preferred for embolic sources.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Continuous cardiac, SpO2, and BP monitoring; ensure two large-bore IVs; place affected limb in dependent position at room temperature; mark Doppler pulse locations with skin pen for serial reassessment
opts.rat: FIRST - immediate stabilization and positioning. Pulse marking allows next nurse to quickly find the Doppler signal locations. Free, immediate, no orders needed.

opts.text: Administer IV heparin 80 units/kg bolus then 18 units/kg/hr drip per protocol; verify pre-existing anticoagulation status with patient/family
opts.rat: SECOND - the immediate medical therapy. Prevents clot propagation while OR mobilized. Document time precisely; affects subsequent dosing decisions and surgery timing.

opts.text: Administer IV opioid analgesia (e.g., fentanyl or morphine titrated for severe ischemic pain); obtain pre-op labs (type and screen, coags, CBC, BMP, CK), ECG, consent if patient able
opts.rat: THIRD - parallel pre-op preparation and pain management. Severe ischemic pain is among the worst pains in medicine; adequate analgesia is humane and reduces sympathetic response.

opts.text: Coordinate emergent OR or IR suite with vascular surgery; complete CT angiography if it does not delay definitive treatment; notify family of severity and impending procedure
opts.rat: FOURTH - escalation and definitive care logistics. Imaging guides decision but should not delay revascularization in Rutherford IIb. Family communication is essential given potential amputation risk.

opts.text: Anticipate post-revascularization needs: ICU bed, K+ replacement readiness, mannitol/bicarb for rhabdo, compartment pressure monitoring; brief next-shift nurse on reperfusion injury signs
opts.rat: LAST in immediate sequence - anticipatory planning. The most dangerous period is actually AFTER revascularization (reperfusion injury). Setting up the receiving environment prevents complications from being missed.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After heparin, surgical embolectomy, and ICU admission, classify each finding over the next 24-48 hours.

opts.text: Post-op: palpable popliteal and PT pulses on right, leg pink and warm, capillary refill 2 sec, patient able to dorsiflex foot weakly
opts.rat: Successful revascularization. Return of pulses, color, warmth, and motor function are the strongest improvement markers. Continue monitoring for reperfusion injury despite excellent technical result.

opts.text: Pain 2/10 with PCA analgesia, no fasciotomy required, ambulating with PT on POD#2, tolerating PO apixaban restart with bridge
opts.rat: Excellent functional recovery with limb salvage. Anticoagulation restart with bridging plan addresses the underlying embolic source. Patient education on adherence is the most important discharge intervention to prevent recurrence.

opts.text: Lactate cleared from 3.8 to 1.4 mmol/L; CK peaked at 4,200 then trending down to 2,100; UO clear yellow at 70 mL/hr; potassium stable at 4.2
opts.rat: Reperfusion injury managed successfully - lactate clearance, CK trending down, preserved renal function, normal K+. Aggressive fluid resuscitation prevented pigment-induced AKI.

opts.text: Sudden K+ rise from 4.6 to 6.8 within 30 min of revascularization, with peaked T waves and widened QRS on monitor
opts.rat: REPERFUSION HYPERKALEMIA - intracellular K+ accumulated during ischemia floods circulation with revascularization. Life-threatening within minutes. Treatment: IV calcium gluconate for membrane stabilization, then insulin/dextrose, beta-agonist, possibly emergent dialysis. Anticipated complication.

opts.text: POD#1: severe right calf pain on passive stretch, tense/firm compartments, paresthesia returning, paralysis recurring; intracompartmental pressure 42 mmHg
opts.rat: COMPARTMENT SYNDROME from reperfusion edema. Pressure >30 mmHg with clinical signs = emergent fasciotomy. The classic 'pain out of proportion' returns after initial improvement. Common post-revascularization complication; failure to recognize causes preventable limb loss.

opts.text: UO dropping to <20 mL/hr; urine cola-colored; CK rising to 22,000; creatinine 1.1 -> 2.1 -> 2.8
opts.rat: PIGMENT-INDUCED AKI from rhabdomyolysis. Aggressive isotonic fluid resuscitation (target UO 200-300 mL/hr), consider sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine, monitor K+ closely. May require CRRT if oliguria sustained. The kidney pays for the limb if not protected.

opts.text: Patient and family verbalize understanding of strict apixaban adherence going forward; case manager arranges 90-day refill with copay assistance program; outpatient anticoagulation clinic follow-up scheduled
opts.rat: Critical secondary prevention. The original cause was missed anticoagulation due to cost; failure to address this guarantees recurrence. Social determinants intervention is the most impactful nursing-led action for long-term outcome.

opts.text: Persistent intermittent AFib with rate 88-104, well-controlled on metoprolol, anticoagulated on apixaban with INR not needed for DOAC
opts.rat: Chronic AFib continues - it doesn't 'cure' from this event. The goal is rate control and anticoagulation to prevent the NEXT embolus. Stable maintenance is the expected baseline. Long-term cardiology follow-up is part of discharge planning.

title: Pediatric Diabetic Ketoacidosis
subtitle: 11 y/o male, new-onset T1DM, severe DKA with cerebral edema risk
nursesNote: 0530 - 11 y/o male, weight 38 kg, brought to ED by parents for 'breathing funny' and lethargy. Per mother, 2-week history of polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia, 8-pound weight loss, fatigue; pediatrician appointment was scheduled for tomorrow. Yesterday he developed nausea/vomiting and abdominal pain; this morning he was hard to wake and breathing rapidly. No prior history of diabetes; no family history. On arrival: lethargic but arousable, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6), oriented to person only, Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath odor, dry mucous membranes, skin tenting, sunken eyes, cool extremities with delayed capillary refill 4 sec. Bedside glucose 'HI'; bedside ketones large. Two IVs placed. Mother is terrified and crying; father with younger sibling in waiting room. PICU bed assigned. Endocrinology fellow on the way.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish SEVERE pediatric DKA with high cerebral edema risk.

opts.text: pH 7.08 with HCO3 6 - meets ISPAD severe DKA criteria (pH <7.1 OR HCO3 <5); severity drives fluid rate, monitoring intensity, and cerebral edema risk
opts.rat: ISPAD (International Society for Pediatric and Adolescent Diabetes) classifies DKA: mild (pH 7.20-7.30), moderate (7.10-7.20), severe (<7.10). Severe DKA has the highest cerebral edema risk - the leading cause of mortality in pediatric DKA. Severity dictates the cautious fluid approach.

opts.text: Age 11 with new-onset T1DM presentation (2-week prodrome of polyuria/polydipsia/polyphagia/weight loss), severe acidosis on arrival, elevated BUN/Cr indicating dehydration
opts.rat: All major risk factors for cerebral edema present: young age (<5 highest, but <12 still elevated), new-onset diabetes (first DKA presentation), severe acidosis, elevated BUN. Glaser/Marcin risk stratification - this patient carries multiple risk factors stacked.

opts.text: GCS 13 with altered mentation at presentation (oriented only to person, lethargic), in a child with severe metabolic derangement
opts.rat: Baseline AMS before treatment establishes neuro starting point - critical because cerebral edema typically develops 4-12 hours INTO treatment, manifesting as worsening mentation from this baseline. Q1h neuro checks during treatment are mandated.

opts.text: Heart rate 138
opts.rat: Reflects volume depletion + acidotic state - expected in DKA. Not specific to pediatric severity.

opts.text: Glucose 682 mg/dL
opts.rat: Confirms hyperglycemia but the ABSOLUTE glucose number doesn't correlate well with severity in DKA. Pediatric DKA can occur with glucose 200-300 (euglycemic DKA in SGLT2 inhibitor users, though rare in kids). pH/HCO3 drive severity classification.

opts.text: HbA1c 12.4%
opts.rat: Confirms chronic hyperglycemia leading to diagnosis - useful for new-onset T1DM workup but a historical marker, not an acute severity cue.

opts.text: Kussmaul respirations and fruity breath
opts.rat: Classic findings of severe acidosis with ketone elimination - confirm DKA but not unique to PEDIATRIC severity. Adults with severe DKA show the same.

opts.text: Mother crying and terrified
opts.rat: Important psychosocial context but not a clinical cue establishing severity.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link cues to PEDIATRIC DKA pathophysiology and management - emphasizing differences from adult DKA.

opts.text: Cerebral edema occurs in 0.5-1% of pediatric DKA cases but carries 20-25% mortality and 25% morbidity in survivors; the mechanism is multifactorial (osmotic shifts, vasogenic edema, ischemia-reperfusion); RAPID fluid administration or rapid osmotic correction increases risk
opts.rat: The defining concern of pediatric DKA management. Adult DKA cerebral edema is rare; pediatric cerebral edema is the LEADING cause of DKA mortality in children. All pediatric DKA protocols are built around its prevention - especially controlled fluid rates and gradual glucose lowering.

opts.text: Fluid resuscitation in pediatric DKA is more conservative than adult DKA: typically 10-20 mL/kg 0.9% NS over 1-2 hours for resuscitation (not boluses), then maintenance + replacement of deficit (assume 5-10% dehydration) evenly over 24-48 hours - NOT rapid front-loading
opts.rat: Critical pediatric-specific protocol. PECARN FLUID trial (2018) showed faster vs slower rates don't differ in cerebral edema rates - but standard remains controlled. Avoid 'wide-open' boluses unless frank shock. Replace deficit over 24-48 hr, NOT 24 hr.

opts.text: Insulin infusion is started at 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr AFTER initial fluid resuscitation has begun AND potassium is verified >=3.3 mEq/L; NEVER given as a bolus in pediatric DKA; D5 is added to IVF when glucose reaches ~250-300 to prevent rapid drop
opts.rat: Pediatric insulin protocol is more conservative than adult - NO bolus dose (was historical practice, now contraindicated in peds). Lower starting dose (0.05-0.1 vs 0.1-0.14 adult). Add dextrose proactively when glucose hits 250-300 to allow continued insulin without hypoglycemia.

opts.text: Sodium bicarbonate should be given for any pH below 7.10 to rapidly correct acidosis and prevent arrest
opts.rat: DANGEROUS in pediatric DKA. Bicarbonate is associated with INCREASED cerebral edema risk in children and prolonged hospitalization. Reserved for life-threatening hyperkalemia or pH <6.9 with hemodynamic instability. ISPAD specifically discourages routine bicarb in peds DKA - a key difference from older adult practice.

opts.text: Pediatric DKA patients should be made strictly NPO with no oral fluids until DKA is resolved
opts.rat: OUTDATED. Once mental status improves and DKA is resolving, oral fluids supplement IV resuscitation. NPO status is for airway protection in obtunded patients, not arbitrary protocol. The key is hourly neuro reassessment.

opts.text: Aggressive correction of glucose to under 100 mg/dL within 4 hours of presentation is the primary goal
opts.rat: DANGEROUS. Rapid glucose drops (>100 mg/dL/hr) increase cerebral edema risk in children. Target: 50-75 mg/dL/hr decrease. Add dextrose at 250-300 to keep insulin running while preventing rapid drop. The goal is correcting acidosis, not glucose - glucose normalizes incidentally as insulin clears ketones.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Drag to rank the most likely treatment complications/threats from MOST concerning (1) to LEAST concerning (5).

opts.text: Cerebral edema developing 4-12 hours into treatment (the leading cause of pediatric DKA mortality)
opts.rat: All risk factors present in this patient. Watching for it is the single most important monitoring task during treatment. Headache, bradycardia, incontinence, declining GCS, or abnormal motor/verbal response are red flags.

opts.text: Hypokalemia from insulin-driven cellular shift (the most common AVOIDABLE complication)
opts.rat: Total body K+ is depleted even when serum K+ is elevated. Once insulin starts, K+ shifts intracellularly and can drop dangerously fast. Hourly K+ checks and proactive replacement when K+ <5.5.

opts.text: Hypoglycemia from inadequate dextrose addition as glucose corrects
opts.rat: Easy to prevent (add D5 to IVF at glucose 250-300) but common error. Causes rebound ketosis and can mimic cerebral edema with AMS.

opts.text: Aspiration in obtunded patient who is vomiting
opts.rat: Real risk in any obtunded patient. Position appropriately, suction available, consider gastric tube if persistent vomiting.

opts.text: Acute kidney injury from severe dehydration
opts.rat: Pre-renal AKI in DKA is common but usually reverses with hydration. Monitor UO and creatinine; AKI from DKA itself rarely requires intervention beyond fluid restoration.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions in the first hour of pediatric DKA management.

opts.text: Initiate 0.9% NS 10-20 mL/kg over 1-2 hours (here ~380-760 mL); recalculate ongoing rate to replace deficit over 24-48 hr PLUS maintenance; titrate based on perfusion (cap refill, HR, UO) rather than rapid bolusing
opts.rat: Cautious controlled rehydration is the foundation. PECARN-supported approach. Avoid the adult instinct of rapid resuscitation. Frank shock (rare in DKA) is the only reason to bolus 20 mL/kg rapidly.

opts.text: Place on continuous cardiac monitoring, q1h neuro checks (GCS, pupils, motor exam, ask about headache), strict hourly intake/output, q1h glucose, q1-2h electrolytes; document neuro baseline carefully
opts.rat: Pediatric DKA monitoring is INTENSIVE because cerebral edema can appear suddenly. The neuro baseline at admission is the reference for detecting subtle decline. Q1h checks aren't optional.

opts.text: After fluid resuscitation begins (1-2 hours in) and K+ confirmed >=3.3 mEq/L, start IV regular insulin at 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr (NO bolus); target glucose decrease 50-75 mg/dL/hr; add D5 to IVF when glucose reaches 250-300
opts.rat: Insulin protocol is pediatric-specific: no bolus, lower rate than adult, dextrose added earlier. The insulin drives ketone clearance and acidosis correction - the actual treatment goal.

opts.text: Administer IV regular insulin 0.1 unit/kg bolus immediately to rapidly drop glucose
opts.rat: CONTRAINDICATED in pediatric DKA. Insulin bolus increases rapid osmotic shifts and cerebral edema risk. Was historical practice in adults; now avoided in peds entirely.

opts.text: Administer sodium bicarbonate 1 mEq/kg IV push to correct severe acidosis
opts.rat: INCREASED cerebral edema risk in children. ISPAD recommends against routine bicarb in pediatric DKA. Reserved for life-threatening hyperkalemia or pH <6.9 with hemodynamic compromise - very rarely indicated.

opts.text: Bolus 20 mL/kg 0.9% NS rapidly, then repeat boluses every 30 minutes until perfusion is restored
opts.rat: AGGRESSIVE bolusing increases cerebral edema risk. Pediatric DKA uses ONE conservative bolus only if true shock is present; otherwise controlled rehydration over hours. Major peds-vs-adult difference.

opts.text: Administer mannitol 1 g/kg IV prophylactically to prevent cerebral edema
opts.rat: Prophylactic mannitol has no role - reserved for TREATING established cerebral edema. Routine use would expose patients to side effects without benefit.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Continuous cardiac/SpO2 monitoring, q1h neuro check baseline documented (GCS, pupils, motor, headache assessment), two IVs confirmed patent, Foley placed for strict hourly UO, head of bed at 30 degrees
opts.rat: FIRST - monitoring setup and baseline documentation. The neuro baseline is the reference point for all subsequent assessments. HOB at 30 is part of standard cerebral edema prevention positioning.

opts.text: Initiate 0.9% NS at 10-15 mL/kg over the first hour (here ~380-570 mL); send full labs (CBC, BMP, ABG, beta-hydroxybutyrate, magnesium, phosphorus, urinalysis); place patient on PICU pathway
opts.rat: SECOND - controlled fluid resuscitation. Labs guide further management. PICU pathway invokes pediatric-specific protocols and intensity of monitoring.

opts.text: After 1-2 hours of fluid AND verification K+ >=3.3, start IV regular insulin infusion at 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr (no bolus); recheck glucose hourly
opts.rat: THIRD - insulin starts after volume restored and K+ confirmed safe. Slow conservative approach. The wait is intentional - matches pediatric protocol.

opts.text: Recalculate fluid: deficit (assume 7-10% dehydration = 2,660-3,800 mL) PLUS maintenance (1,710 mL/day by Holliday-Segar for 38 kg) replaced evenly over 24-48 hours; add KCl 20-40 mEq/L to IVF once UO established and K+ <5.5; add D5 to IVF when glucose <300
opts.rat: FOURTH - longer-term fluid plan. Even distribution over 24-48 hours protects against cerebral edema. K+ replacement is essential. Dextrose addition prevents rapid drop while continuing insulin.

opts.text: Coordinate PICU transfer, endocrinology consult for new-onset T1DM education, social work, and diabetes nurse educator; update parents on diagnosis, prognosis, and the lifelong commitment of T1DM management; offer family room and chaplaincy
opts.rat: LAST in immediate sequence - psychosocial and educational support. New-onset T1DM is a family-altering diagnosis. Initial education during inpatient stay strongly influences long-term adherence. Parents need processing time and concrete support.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After 6-12 hours of conservative fluid resuscitation, insulin infusion, and PICU monitoring, classify each finding.

opts.text: Glucose 248 mg/dL, pH 7.28, HCO3 16, anion gap closing to 18, beta-hydroxybutyrate 2.1
opts.rat: Controlled correction at the target rate. Anion gap closure is the marker of DKA resolution - more important than glucose. Continue insulin until anion gap normalizes; add dextrose to maintain glucose 150-200.

opts.text: GCS improved from 13 to 15, alert and oriented, asking for mom, no headache, normal motor exam
opts.rat: Neurologic recovery confirms no cerebral edema developed. Continue hourly neuro checks until DKA fully resolved - cerebral edema can occur up to 24 hours into treatment.

opts.text: Parents engaging with diabetes nurse educator, asking questions about insulin injection technique, reviewing carb counting basics with hospital dietitian
opts.rat: Family education engagement is the strongest predictor of long-term outcomes in new-onset T1DM. Hospital admission is the foundational education opportunity. Insulin pump or MDI decision, glucagon training, and sick-day rules are part of discharge planning.

opts.text: 8 hours into treatment: new severe headache, then sudden bradycardia (HR 58), confusion, vomiting, and pupil asymmetry
opts.rat: CEREBRAL EDEMA - the feared complication. Mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg IV OR hypertonic saline 3% 2.5-5 mL/kg, head of bed at 30, reduce IVF rate by 1/3, intubate if GCS drops, stat CT head. Bradycardia (Cushing's reflex) + new severe headache + AMS = treat empirically without waiting for imaging.

opts.text: K+ dropped from 5.4 to 2.6 with new ST flattening and U waves on monitor
opts.rat: Insulin-driven hypokalemia - the most common avoidable complication. Hold insulin temporarily, give IV KCl, continuous cardiac monitoring. Often happens when K+ replacement wasn't added to IVF or was inadequate.

opts.text: Glucose dropped from 682 to 220 mg/dL in 3 hours; no dextrose added to IVF yet; child becoming sleepy
opts.rat: Approaching iatrogenic hypoglycemia. Add D5 to IVF NOW; do not stop insulin (we need to continue ketone clearance). Common error - failing to add dextrose at the 250-300 threshold.

opts.text: Heart rate 96, BP 110/68, capillary refill 2 sec, UO 50 mL/hr (>1 mL/kg/hr), mucous membranes moist
opts.rat: Perfusion fully restored without overshooting into fluid overload. Controlled rehydration succeeded. Continue current rate and reassess at deficit replacement halfway point.

opts.text: Mild residual abdominal pain and nausea, glucose 200, on continued insulin infusion with D5 in IVF, mentation normal
opts.rat: Expected late-DKA findings. Abdominal pain often persists even as acidosis resolves. As long as not worsening and trajectory is favorable, this is normal recovery.

title: C. difficile Colitis with Infection Control
subtitle: 74 y/o female, hospital day 8 on broad-spectrum antibiotics, profuse watery diarrhea
nursesNote: 1420 - 74 y/o female on hospital day 8, originally admitted for community-acquired pneumonia. Has received ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily + levofloxacin 750 mg IV daily x 7 days; pneumonia clinically improved (afebrile x 3 days, weaned to room air). Day 7 (yesterday) noted onset of loose stools (3 episodes), attributed initially to antibiotic-associated diarrhea. Overnight and today: watery foul-smelling green-brown stool x 9 episodes in 18 hours, with diffuse cramping abdominal pain rated 6/10, new fever 38.6 C (was afebrile yesterday), and decreased oral intake. PMH: HTN, GERD on omeprazole 20 mg daily x 4 years, osteoarthritis. Skin tents on forehead, mucous membranes dry, mild orthostatic drop in BP on sitting. Abdomen: soft, mildly distended, diffusely tender without rebound or guarding, hyperactive bowel sounds in all quadrants. No blood per rectum reported. Stool C. diff NAAT and toxin EIA sent STAT; CBC, CMP, lactate drawn. Patient moved to private room and contact-plus precautions initiated pending results.

labs.n: Stool C. difficile NAAT (toxin gene PCR)

labs.v: Mildly distended colon, no free air, no megacolon (transverse colon 4.5 cm)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish severe Clostridioides difficile infection requiring isolation and aggressive treatment.

opts.text: Profuse watery foul-smelling diarrhea (>=3 unformed stools in 24 hours, here 9 in 18 hours) with cramping abdominal pain and fever in a patient on day 8 of broad-spectrum antibiotics (ceftriaxone + levofloxacin) - classic CDI presentation; fluoroquinolones and cephalosporins are among the highest-risk antibiotic classes
opts.rat: CDI is defined clinically by >=3 unformed stools/24h plus laboratory confirmation. The antibiotic exposure is the dominant risk factor. Highest-risk classes: fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins (especially 3rd/4th gen), clindamycin, and broad-spectrum penicillins. PPI use (this patient on omeprazole 4 years) and age >65 compound the risk.

opts.text: Severity criteria met per IDSA 2021: WBC 24,200 (>=15,000) AND new AKI with Cr 1.8 (>=1.5 mg/dL or 1.5x baseline 0.9) - meets SEVERE CDI definition (either criterion alone is sufficient); guides treatment intensity and risk of progression to fulminant disease
opts.rat: IDSA 2021 severity: non-severe = WBC <15K AND Cr <1.5; severe = WBC >=15K OR Cr >=1.5; fulminant = hypotension/shock, ileus, or megacolon. Severity dictates treatment regimen and monitoring intensity. This patient has BOTH severe markers, raising fulminant progression risk.

opts.text: Stool NAAT POSITIVE for toxin gene AND stool toxin A/B EIA POSITIVE - confirms TOXIN-PRODUCING active infection, distinguishing true CDI from asymptomatic carriage; the two-step algorithm reduces false positives from colonization
opts.rat: NAAT alone detects the toxin GENE (high sensitivity but can be positive in asymptomatic carriers). Toxin EIA detects actively produced TOXIN (less sensitive but more specific for active disease). When both are positive plus clinical syndrome, true CDI is confirmed. Toxin-negative/NAAT-positive cases are debated and clinical judgment guides treatment.

opts.text: PPI use (omeprazole) for 4 years
opts.rat: Risk factor for CDI (gastric acid suppression allows spore survival to colon) but not a diagnostic cue. Worth deprescribing if no strong indication, but does not establish the current infection.

opts.text: Age 74 years
opts.rat: Risk factor (immunosenescence and altered microbiome) but not a diagnostic cue. Patients >=65 have markedly higher CDI incidence and recurrence rates.

opts.text: Hospital day 8
opts.rat: Defines this as healthcare-onset CDI (>3 days after admission) versus community-onset, which has reporting and infection-control implications, but is not itself a diagnostic cue establishing the severity of infection.

opts.text: Albumin 2.6 g/dL
opts.rat: Hypoalbuminemia is a CDI severity/prognostic marker (older Zar criteria included albumin <2.5) but is NOT in the current IDSA 2021 severity definition. Reflects chronic illness and protein loss but doesn't drive treatment selection.

opts.text: Hyperactive bowel sounds
opts.rat: Consistent with active diarrheal illness but non-specific. The CONCERNING finding to watch for is the OPPOSITE - decreased or absent bowel sounds suggesting ileus, which would mark progression to fulminant CDI / toxic megacolon.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to underlying pathophysiology and management.

opts.text: First-line treatment for initial severe CDI per IDSA 2021: ORAL fidaxomicin 200 mg BID x 10 days (preferred - lower recurrence rate vs vancomycin) OR ORAL vancomycin 125 mg every 6 hours x 10 days; NEVER use IV vancomycin for CDI - it does not penetrate the colon lumen where the organism lives
opts.rat: The IV-vs-oral vancomycin distinction is one of the highest-yield NCLEX traps. Vancomycin's mechanism for CDI requires direct luminal contact - IV administration delivers it systemically but not into the gut where it's needed. Fidaxomicin became preferred in IDSA 2018/2021 updates due to lower recurrence (narrower spectrum spares the protective microbiome). Metronidazole is no longer first-line.

opts.text: Contact-PLUS precautions: gown + gloves on entry, private room, DEDICATED equipment (stethoscope, thermometer, BP cuff stay in room), HAND HYGIENE WITH SOAP AND WATER (mechanical removal of spores - alcohol-based hand sanitizer does NOT kill C. difficile spores), bleach-based environmental cleaning (sodium hypochlorite); continue precautions until 48 hours after diarrhea resolves
opts.rat: C. diff produces hardy spores that survive alcohol, most disinfectants, and dry surfaces for months. Soap-and-water works mechanically (friction + rinse-off). Sodium hypochlorite (1:10 bleach) is the only widely available agent that kills spores. PPE plus dedicated equipment plus environmental cleaning together break the transmission chain. The 48-hour-post-resolution rule prevents premature de-isolation.

opts.text: Antibiotic stewardship is part of treatment: discontinue or narrow the offending antibiotics if clinically possible (here ceftriaxone and levofloxacin can likely be stopped now that pneumonia has resolved); avoid antimotility agents (loperamide, diphenoxylate-atropine) - they retain toxin in the colon and can precipitate toxic megacolon
opts.rat: Removing the antibiotic insult often allows microbiome recovery alongside CDI-specific treatment. The antimotility-agent prohibition is high-yield NCLEX: in many diarrhea types loperamide is appropriate, but in CDI it traps the toxin and accelerates progression to fulminant disease.

opts.text: IV metronidazole 500 mg every 8 hours is the preferred first-line therapy for all CDI severity levels
opts.rat: OUTDATED. IDSA 2018 update downgraded metronidazole. It is now reserved for: (1) FULMINANT CDI as part of combination therapy with oral/PR vancomycin, or (2) when oral therapy cannot be administered. Metronidazole alone has higher failure and recurrence rates than vancomycin or fidaxomicin.

opts.text: Loperamide 4 mg after each loose stool is appropriate symptom management to reduce dehydration and electrolyte loss
opts.rat: DANGEROUS in CDI. Antimotility agents retain toxin in the colon, worsen mucosal damage, and can precipitate toxic megacolon. Diarrhea is how the body clears toxin - suppressing it backfires. Manage volume losses with IV fluids and electrolyte replacement instead.

opts.text: Alcohol-based hand sanitizer used between every patient contact is sufficient hand hygiene to prevent CDI transmission
opts.rat: FALSE and a high-frequency NCLEX trap. Alcohol does NOT kill C. diff spores. Soap-and-water hand washing is required when caring for patients with confirmed or suspected CDI - the friction and rinsing physically remove spores from skin. Alcohol sanitizer is acceptable for most other organisms but fails for C. diff spores, norovirus, and Cryptosporidium.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Most likely cause of the diarrhea
steps.inst: Drag to rank causes of the diarrhea from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: C. difficile infection - confirmed by NAAT + toxin EIA, with classic risk factors (broad-spectrum antibiotics, age, PPI, hospitalization) and meets severe criteria
opts.rat: Confirmed by laboratory and clinical syndrome. Single most likely cause given risk factor profile and positive testing.

opts.text: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea (non-CDI) - alteration of normal flora causing osmotic/secretory diarrhea without C. diff infection
opts.rat: Common in hospitalized patients on antibiotics but a diagnosis of exclusion. Toxin-positive testing rules this out as the dominant cause here, though it can co-exist.

opts.text: Healthcare-associated viral gastroenteritis (norovirus, rotavirus) - explosive watery diarrhea, often with vomiting, in hospital outbreaks
opts.rat: Worth considering in hospital outbreaks, especially if other patients/staff also affected. Norovirus is also resistant to alcohol sanitizer (requires soap-and-water). Less likely here given confirmed C. diff testing and absence of vomiting/outbreak context.

opts.text: Tube feeding intolerance or osmotic diarrhea from medications/supplements
opts.rat: Common in inpatients but not applicable here without tube feeds or new osmotic agents. Worth checking medication list for sorbitol-containing liquids or magnesium-containing antacids.

opts.text: Inflammatory bowel disease flare or microscopic colitis
opts.rat: Less likely without prior history. Patients with IBD do have higher CDI rates and CDI can mimic or trigger flares - colonoscopy may be considered if symptoms persist after CDI treatment.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions in the next several hours.

opts.text: Implement contact-PLUS precautions IMMEDIATELY: private room, gown + gloves on entry, dedicated equipment, signage on door, soap-and-water hand hygiene (NOT alcohol sanitizer), bleach-based room cleaning; educate all staff and family entering the room; do not delay isolation pending laboratory confirmation if clinical suspicion is high
opts.rat: Empiric isolation pending labs is the standard of care - waiting for results allows ongoing transmission. Spore shedding starts before symptoms peak and continues during treatment. Cohorting with other CDI patients is acceptable if private room unavailable.

opts.text: Initiate ORAL fidaxomicin 200 mg BID x 10 days (preferred per IDSA 2021) OR oral vancomycin 125 mg q6h x 10 days; do NOT order IV vancomycin (does not reach colon lumen); coordinate with infectious disease and antibiotic stewardship to discontinue or narrow offending antibiotics (ceftriaxone/levofloxacin)
opts.rat: Empiric treatment for high-suspicion severe CDI may be started while awaiting confirmatory testing in some institutions. The oral route is non-negotiable - this is the highest-yield NCLEX point in CDI management.

opts.text: Aggressive volume resuscitation with isotonic IV crystalloid (lactated Ringer's preferred) for AKI from dehydration and orthostatic hypotension; replace potassium (K 3.1) and other electrolytes; daily basic metabolic panel; strict intake/output monitoring; daily abdominal exam for distention or decreased bowel sounds suggesting ileus
opts.rat: Volume losses in CDI can be massive (liters per day) and cause AKI, electrolyte derangement, and shock. The Cr 1.8 from baseline 0.9 reflects pre-renal AKI that should improve with rehydration. Daily abdominal exam screens for the ileus that heralds fulminant progression.

opts.text: Initiate IV vancomycin 1 g every 12 hours for systemic coverage of the colitis
opts.rat: FUNDAMENTAL ERROR. IV vancomycin does NOT reach the colon lumen and is INEFFECTIVE for CDI. The drug must be in the gut to work. Route matters more than dose.

opts.text: Loperamide 4 mg now and after each loose stool to reduce diarrhea frequency and prevent dehydration
opts.rat: CONTRAINDICATED in CDI. Antimotility agents retain toxin in the colon and can precipitate toxic megacolon. Address dehydration with IV fluids.

opts.text: Strict use of alcohol-based hand sanitizer between every patient encounter
opts.rat: INADEQUATE for C. diff. Alcohol does not kill spores. Hand washing with soap and water is required for every CDI patient encounter - the mechanical action of friction and rinsing removes spores from skin.

opts.text: Stop ALL antibiotics immediately, including any active treatment for the original pneumonia or other infections
opts.rat: Oversimplified. Antibiotic stewardship means narrow or stop OFFENDING antibiotics WHEN CLINICALLY SAFE. If the pneumonia is resolved, ceftriaxone/levofloxacin can stop. If another active infection requires treatment, choose the narrowest effective agent. Stopping needed antibiotics blindly causes other harm.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Implement contact-PLUS precautions empirically: move to private room (or cohort with confirmed CDI patients), post signage, gown and gloves on entry, dedicated equipment, soap-and-water hand hygiene, bleach-based environmental cleaning; staff and family education
opts.rat: FIRST - empiric isolation prevents ongoing transmission while awaiting confirmation. Every encounter without proper precautions is a chance to spread spores throughout the unit.

opts.text: Send stool C. difficile NAAT and toxin EIA, CBC with differential, CMP including creatinine and electrolytes, lactate, albumin; assess severity (WBC, Cr, hemodynamics, mental status, abdominal exam); plain abdominal X-ray to assess for ileus or megacolon
opts.rat: SECOND - confirmatory testing and severity stratification. Severity drives treatment intensity and disposition. Imaging baseline allows comparison if patient deteriorates.

opts.text: Initiate oral fidaxomicin 200 mg BID (preferred) OR oral vancomycin 125 mg q6h x 10 days; do NOT use IV vancomycin; coordinate antibiotic stewardship to discontinue/narrow ceftriaxone and levofloxacin if pneumonia resolved
opts.rat: THIRD - definitive antimicrobial therapy. Started promptly even before final laboratory return when clinical suspicion is high and severity criteria met.

opts.text: IV crystalloid resuscitation (lactated Ringer's) for AKI and orthostasis; potassium and electrolyte replacement; strict I&O monitoring, daily BMP, daily abdominal exam; avoid antimotility agents (no loperamide)
opts.rat: FOURTH - supportive care for the volume and electrolyte consequences. Ongoing assessment for progression is built in here - the abdominal exam each shift detects developing ileus or megacolon.

opts.text: Education and prevention: reinforce soap-and-water hand hygiene with patient, family, and visitors; counsel on warning signs of recurrence (15-25% recur within 8 weeks); plan post-discharge primary care follow-up; consider PPI deprescription if no strong indication; document recurrence-risk factors in chart
opts.rat: LAST in immediate sequence - prevention of recurrence and transmission to family. Discharge planning starts early. Recurrence is common (15-25% after first episode, 40%+ after second) and outpatient identification of warning signs determines outcome.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After isolation, oral vancomycin/fidaxomicin, IV fluids, and antibiotic narrowing, classify each finding over the next several days.

opts.text: Day 4 of oral vancomycin: stool frequency decreased from 9/day to 3 semi-formed BMs/day, abdominal cramping resolved, WBC down to 12.8 from 24.2, creatinine improved to 1.1 from 1.8, eating soft diet, ambulating in room
opts.rat: Excellent treatment response. Most patients see clinical improvement within 48-72 hours of starting oral vanc/fidaxomicin. Recovery of WBC and Cr indicates resolution of systemic and renal stress. Continue full 10-day course - shortening risks recurrence.

opts.text: Completion of 10-day oral vancomycin course: diarrhea fully resolved x 48 hours, formed stools, WBC normalized, Cr at baseline, contact-plus precautions discontinued (48 hours post-diarrhea-resolution rule), transferring to subacute rehabilitation
opts.rat: Successful resolution of acute episode. The 48-hour-post-resolution rule for stopping isolation prevents premature de-isolation while spore shedding continues. Recurrence risk persists - patient remains a candidate for close outpatient monitoring x 8 weeks.

opts.text: Family demonstrates correct hand-washing technique with soap and water; verbalizes understanding that diarrhea returning within 8 weeks should prompt immediate medical contact; PPI deprescribed; primary care follow-up scheduled
opts.rat: Critical secondary prevention. Family teaching matters because recurrent CDI usually presents to outpatient setting first. PPI deprescribing reduces recurrence risk. Continued vigilance for 8 weeks is the standard surveillance window.

opts.text: Day 3 of treatment: increasing abdominal distention with decreased then absent bowel sounds, lactate rising to 4.2, BP 88/52 unresponsive to 2 L LR bolus, requiring norepinephrine support; new altered mental status
opts.rat: FULMINANT CDI (hypotension/shock + ileus). Mortality ~30%. Treatment escalation: oral vancomycin DOSE INCREASE to 500 mg q6h, ADD IV metronidazole 500 mg q8h, consider rectal vancomycin instillation if ileus blocks oral delivery, urgent surgical consult, ICU transfer, abdominal imaging for megacolon.

opts.text: CT abdomen showing colonic dilation 8 cm with thumbprinting (mucosal edema), pneumatosis (air in bowel wall), and surrounding fluid; abdomen rigid with rebound tenderness
opts.rat: TOXIC MEGACOLON with possible early perforation. Colon dilation >6 cm + systemic toxicity defines megacolon. Pneumatosis suggests transmural injury; rebound suggests peritonitis from microperforation. Emergent surgical consult for subtotal colectomy with end ileostomy - mortality 30-50% if delayed.

opts.text: Two weeks after completing initial 10-day course: return of watery diarrhea 5-6 episodes/day with cramping; stool re-tested and POSITIVE for C. difficile NAAT and toxin
opts.rat: RECURRENT CDI (15-25% after first episode). Treatment for first recurrence: fidaxomicin (if not used initially) or extended-pulsed-tapered vancomycin regimen. For multiple recurrences, consider bezlotoxumab (monoclonal anti-toxin B) as adjunct or fecal microbiota transplant (FMT). Each recurrence increases the risk of further recurrences.

opts.text: Mild persistent fatigue, decreased appetite, and intermittent loose stools (1-2 formed-to-soft per day) for 1-2 weeks post-treatment, gradually improving
opts.rat: Expected post-CDI recovery course. Microbiome takes weeks to months to fully recover. Mild residual GI symptoms without return of severe diarrhea or systemic features are normal. Counsel patience and outpatient follow-up. Probiotics in this phase are debated - no strong evidence of benefit and potential harm in immunocompromised.

opts.text: Mild dryness and chapping of hands from frequent soap-and-water washing; resolution with hand moisturizer between washings
opts.rat: Expected occupational/care effect. Hand hygiene fatigue and skin breakdown are common during CDI care. Moisturizer use between (not instead of) washing maintains skin integrity without compromising infection control.

title: Cardiac Tamponade
subtitle: 64 y/o female with metastatic NSCLC, progressive dyspnea now hemodynamically compromised
nursesNote: 1530 - 64 y/o female with stage IV non-small cell lung cancer (adenocarcinoma, on second-line carboplatin/pemetrexed) presenting with 1 week of progressive dyspnea on exertion now at rest, orthopnea (sleeping in recliner x 3 nights), fatigue, decreased oral intake. This morning had pre-syncopal episode when standing from bed. Spouse reports patient looks 'increasingly puffy and pale' over the past 3 days. Currently: HR 124 sinus tachycardia, BP 86/64 with measured pulsus paradoxus 18 mmHg, RR 26 with mild accessory muscle use, SpO2 92% on 2L NC, T 37.2. Mentation: drowsy, oriented x3 but slow to respond. JVP elevated to 12 cm at 30 degrees. Heart sounds DISTANT and muffled, no friction rub. Lungs clear bilaterally. Hepatomegaly (3 cm below costal margin), 2+ pitting edema to mid-shins. Skin cool, mottled at knees, capillary refill 4 sec, weak peripheral pulses. STAT bedside echo, ECG, CXR, labs ordered. Cardiology and CT surgery paged. Two 18g IVs placed; arterial line being placed; type and screen sent.

patient.name: R. Halverson
patient.code: Full Code (reviewing goals of care given underlying disease)
patient.room: ED Resuscitation Bay 3, transferring to CCU

labs.v: Large circumferential pericardial effusion measuring 2.5 cm; diastolic collapse of right atrium AND right ventricle; IVC dilated 2.4 cm without inspiratory collapse (plethora) - tamponade physiology
labs.r: No effusion, normal chamber filling, IVC <2.1 cm with respiratory variation

labs.v: Sinus tachycardia 124, low-voltage QRS in all leads, electrical alternans (alternating QRS amplitude beat-to-beat) in precordial leads
labs.r: Normal sinus rhythm, normal voltage, no alternans

labs.v: Markedly enlarged 'water bottle' cardiac silhouette; lung fields clear bilaterally; no pulmonary edema

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) cues that BEST establish cardiac tamponade requiring emergent intervention.

opts.text: Beck's triad: hypotension (BP 86/64) + jugular venous distention (JVP 12 cm) + muffled/distant heart sounds - the classic clinical triad of cardiac tamponade
opts.rat: Beck's triad is highly specific but a LATE finding - present in only ~30-50% of tamponade cases. Earlier cases may show only one or two components. Modern diagnosis emphasizes echo confirmation, but presence of all three components in the right context is essentially diagnostic.

opts.text: Pulsus paradoxus 18 mmHg measured at bedside (>10 mmHg = pathologic; >12 mmHg highly suggestive of tamponade) - exaggerated inspiratory drop in systolic BP from impaired left ventricular filling during inspiration as the right ventricle bulges into the LV cavity within the constrained pericardial space
opts.rat: Quantifiable, reproducible bedside finding with high specificity for tamponade. Measured by slowly deflating BP cuff: note SBP at first audible Korotkoff during expiration, continue deflation until heard throughout cycle - the difference is pulsus paradoxus. Differential for pulsus >10: severe asthma/COPD, massive PE, RV infarction, hypovolemia.

opts.text: Bedside transthoracic echocardiography showing large circumferential pericardial effusion (2.5 cm) with diastolic collapse of the right atrium AND right ventricle PLUS dilated non-collapsing inferior vena cava (2.4 cm, plethora) - GOLD STANDARD diagnostic findings for tamponade physiology
opts.rat: Echo is the definitive diagnostic test and guides drainage. RA collapse during systole and RV collapse during diastole indicate pericardial pressure exceeds chamber pressures. IVC plethora (dilated, non-collapsing) reflects elevated right-sided filling pressures. The combination is essentially diagnostic. Can be performed at bedside in minutes.

opts.text: Sinus tachycardia of 124 beats per minute
opts.rat: Compensatory response present in nearly every shock state and many non-shock conditions (pain, fever, hypovolemia, anxiety). Non-specific to tamponade. Notably, beta-blockade of this compensation in tamponade can be lethal.

opts.text: Hepatomegaly with 2+ pitting peripheral edema
opts.rat: Signs of right heart failure / elevated systemic venous pressures. Present in tamponade but also in CHF, cor pulmonale, RV infarction, constrictive pericarditis. Supports the diagnosis but not specific.

opts.text: History of metastatic NSCLC on chemotherapy
opts.rat: Important RISK FACTOR - malignancy is the most common cause of large pericardial effusions in adults (lung, breast, lymphoma, leukemia). Shapes the differential and post-drainage management, but is not itself a diagnostic cue establishing the current event.

opts.text: Cool extremities, mottling at the knees, weak peripheral pulses, capillary refill 4 seconds
opts.rat: Signs of low cardiac output and poor peripheral perfusion - present in any shock state. Non-specific to tamponade physiology.

opts.text: BNP 412 pg/mL
opts.rat: Mildly elevated, suggesting cardiac strain, but BNP is far more elevated in CHF (often >1000) and is non-specific. Modest BNP elevation in setting of tamponade reflects RV strain rather than primary LV dysfunction.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: Obstructive shock physiology and the preload-dependent paradox
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) statements that correctly link the cues to underlying pathophysiology and management.

opts.text: Cardiac tamponade is OBSTRUCTIVE SHOCK with PRELOAD DEPENDENCE: pericardial pressure rises until it equals or exceeds intracardiac filling pressures, the heart cannot fill in diastole, stroke volume drops, cardiac output crashes; treat hypotension with IV fluid bolus to maintain filling pressures while preparing for definitive drainage; AVOID diuretics, nitrates, beta-blockers, and ACE inhibitors - they reduce preload and CAN KILL the patient
opts.rat: The single most important physiologic concept in tamponade. The intracardiac filling pressures equalize (RA = RVEDP = PCWP = pericardial pressure). Any drug that reduces venous return or preload further drops cardiac output. This is the OPPOSITE of CHF management - which is why the trap is so common.

opts.text: Pericardiocentesis (preferably echo-guided subxiphoid approach) is the definitive emergency treatment; even removing 50-100 mL of pericardial fluid can dramatically improve hemodynamics due to the steep pericardial pressure-volume curve; surgical pericardial window is alternative for loculated effusions, post-cardiac-surgery cases, hemorrhagic etiologies (aortic dissection, type A), or recurrent/malignant effusions requiring definitive treatment
opts.rat: The pericardium is non-compliant once stretched - small volume changes cause large pressure changes. Removing even 50-100 mL drops pericardial pressure dramatically and restores filling. Echo guidance reduces complication rates substantially compared to blind technique. Subxiphoid avoids pleura and major vessels.

opts.text: Electrical alternans on 12-lead ECG (alternating QRS amplitude beat-to-beat) reflects the heart physically swinging within a large pericardial effusion changing its electrical axis with each beat; combined with low-voltage QRS and sinus tachycardia, this triad is highly specific for large pericardial effusion with tamponade physiology
opts.rat: Electrical alternans is pathognomonic in the right clinical context. Low voltage occurs because the effusion attenuates electrical signals reaching the surface ECG. Sinus tachycardia is the universal compensation. The triad on ECG plus clinical findings prompts STAT echo.

opts.text: IV furosemide 40 mg should be given immediately to reduce the patient's volume overload and decompress the heart given hepatomegaly and peripheral edema
opts.rat: DEADLY ERROR. Despite the appearance of volume overload (hepatomegaly, edema), the volume is TRAPPED behind the obstructed heart - intravascular preload is actually low. Diuresis drops preload further and crashes cardiac output. The signs of right-sided 'failure' here are from outflow obstruction, not from too much volume.

opts.text: Sublingual nitroglycerin or IV nitroglycerin drip should be initiated to reduce afterload and improve cardiac output
opts.rat: CONTRAINDICATED in tamponade. Nitrates are venodilators that drop preload, which is exactly what cannot be tolerated. Same logic applies to ACE inhibitors and other vasodilators in this physiology.

opts.text: IV beta-blocker (metoprolol 5 mg) should be given to slow the compensatory tachycardia and improve diastolic filling time
opts.rat: DEADLY. Tachycardia is the body's compensation for fixed/limited stroke volume - cardiac output = HR x SV. With SV severely reduced by tamponade, HR is the only compensation available. Blocking it crashes cardiac output. Treat the cause (drain the effusion), not the compensation.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Most likely cause of the effusion
steps.inst: Drag to rank the candidate causes from MOST likely (1) to LEAST likely (5).

opts.text: Malignant pericardial effusion progressing to tamponade - direct or metastatic malignant invasion of pericardium from known stage IV NSCLC
opts.rat: Malignancy is the most common cause of large pericardial effusions in adults. Lung cancer is the leading primary, followed by breast, lymphoma, leukemia, and melanoma. Pericardial fluid cytology will confirm. Recurrence is common - definitive prevention requires pericardial window or sclerotherapy.

opts.text: Chemotherapy- or radiation-associated pericarditis with effusion - several agents (cyclophosphamide, anthracyclines, immune checkpoint inhibitors) and chest radiation cause pericardial inflammation
opts.rat: Becoming more common with newer immunotherapies (immune-related adverse events). Radiation pericarditis can occur acutely or years after thoracic RT. Worth reviewing the chemo history. May be steroid-responsive.

opts.text: Uremic pericarditis - effusion from renal failure (typically when BUN >60 or significant uremia)
opts.rat: Less likely with creatinine 1.3, but still on the differential. Uremic pericarditis is an indication for urgent dialysis. Need to check BUN and renal function trend.

opts.text: Idiopathic or viral pericarditis with effusion progressing to tamponade
opts.rat: Less likely without preceding viral prodrome (URI, fever, pleuritic chest pain), and the size of effusion here is more typical of malignancy. Idiopathic effusions are usually smaller and self-limited.

opts.text: Type A aortic dissection with hemopericardium - catastrophic surgical emergency
opts.rat: Lower likelihood without typical sudden severe chest/back pain, asymmetric pulses, or widened mediastinum on CXR - but cannot be definitively excluded without imaging. CRITICAL to rule out before pericardiocentesis: drainage of hemopericardium from dissection without immediate surgical repair can cause exsanguination. CT angiography after stabilization if any suspicion.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: Select the THREE (3) MOST time-critical interventions in the next 30-60 minutes.

opts.text: STAT bedside echocardiography to confirm tamponade physiology (effusion size, RA/RV diastolic collapse, IVC plethora) and guide pericardiocentesis; emergent cardiology and cardiothoracic surgery consultation; arterial line placement for accurate beat-to-beat BP and pulsus paradoxus quantification
opts.rat: Echo IS the diagnostic test and the procedural guide - cannot be skipped. Cardiology drives pericardiocentesis; CT surgery is on standby for complications or surgical pericardial window. Arterial line allows continuous monitoring of pulsus paradoxus as a real-time response indicator.

opts.text: IV crystalloid bolus 500-1000 mL (lactated Ringer's or normal saline) wide open to support preload while preparing for drainage; can repeat if BP does not improve; norepinephrine via large peripheral or central line if MAP <65 despite fluids; explicitly AVOID diuretics, vasodilators, and beta-blockers
opts.rat: Preload support is the bridge to definitive drainage. The volume bolus often produces a transient but clinically meaningful BP improvement that can buy minutes to hours. Norepinephrine maintains MAP without dropping preload (unlike nitroglycerin or hydralazine).

opts.text: Prepare for emergency pericardiocentesis: subxiphoid approach under echo guidance is preferred (avoids pleura and major vessels); cardiothoracic surgery on standby for surgical pericardial window if percutaneous approach fails or is contraindicated (loculated effusion, post-cardiac surgery, hemorrhagic etiology like dissection); leave pericardial drain in place post-procedure for serial drainage
opts.rat: Definitive treatment cannot be delayed in unstable tamponade. Echo guidance reduces major complications (RV puncture, coronary laceration, pneumothorax, hepatic injury) substantially. The drain stays for 24-72 hours to allow complete drainage and detect re-accumulation.

opts.text: IV furosemide 40 mg to reduce the patient's volume overload (hepatomegaly, peripheral edema) and unload the heart
opts.rat: DEADLY in tamponade - despite appearance of volume overload, intravascular preload is critically dependent and reducing it crashes cardiac output. The 'overload' signs are from outflow obstruction, not true hypervolemia.

opts.text: Sublingual nitroglycerin 0.4 mg or IV nitroglycerin drip for afterload reduction and improved coronary perfusion
opts.rat: CONTRAINDICATED. Drops preload, worsens tamponade, can precipitate hemodynamic collapse. Same applies to all venodilators and aggressive afterload reduction in this physiology.

opts.text: IV metoprolol 5 mg to slow heart rate and improve diastolic filling time
opts.rat: DEADLY. Tachycardia is the only compensation available when stroke volume is fixed by tamponade - blocking it crashes cardiac output.

opts.text: Emergent endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation to support oxygenation given SpO2 92% and respiratory distress
opts.rat: Generally AVOIDED in tamponade if at all possible. Positive pressure ventilation reduces venous return and can precipitate cardiac arrest in tamponade physiology. If intubation is unavoidable, drain the effusion FIRST or have pericardiocentesis equipment ready at bedside; use lowest tidal volumes and minimal PEEP; consider awake intubation.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Drag to rank the actions from FIRST (1) to LAST (5).

opts.text: Stabilize ABCs: continuous cardiac and SpO2 monitoring, two large-bore peripheral IVs, supplemental O2 to maintain SpO2 >92%, head of bed slightly elevated for comfort; AVOID intubation if possible (positive pressure ventilation worsens tamponade by reducing venous return); arterial line for accurate BP and pulsus paradoxus tracking; baseline labs (CBC, CMP, coags, troponin, BNP, lactate, type and screen)
opts.rat: FIRST - airway, access, monitoring, lab baseline. The intubation caution is critical and high-yield - many providers reflexively intubate hypoxic shock patients, which can crash a tamponade patient.

opts.text: STAT bedside echocardiography to confirm tamponade (effusion size, chamber collapse, IVC plethora) and guide drainage; emergent cardiology and cardiothoracic surgery consults
opts.rat: SECOND - imaging confirms the diagnosis and is the mandatory pre-procedural step. Specialist mobilization happens in parallel.

opts.text: IV crystalloid bolus 500-1000 mL (LR or NS) to support preload; norepinephrine ready for MAP <65 despite fluids; explicitly AVOID diuretics, nitrates, beta-blockers, and ACE inhibitors
opts.rat: THIRD - hemodynamic bridge to drainage. The avoidance list must be communicated to the entire team to prevent reflexive use of the wrong drug.

opts.text: Emergency pericardiocentesis under echo guidance (subxiphoid approach preferred); leave pericardial drain in place; send pericardial fluid for cytology, cell count and differential, gram stain and culture, AFB stain and culture if TB suspected, protein and LDH (Light's criteria for transudate vs exudate), and tumor markers if malignancy suspected
opts.rat: FOURTH - definitive treatment. Send the right fluid studies the FIRST time - obtaining additional samples later requires repeat procedure. Cytology yield is highest with first drainage.

opts.text: Post-drainage management: repeat echo to confirm resolution and assess for re-accumulation; admit to cardiac ICU for 24-48 hours; oncology consult for malignant effusion (sclerotherapy, intrapericardial chemotherapy, or definitive surgical pericardial window for recurrence prevention); family meeting and goals-of-care discussion given prognostic implications of malignant tamponade (median survival 2-4 months); CT angiography of chest if dissection has not been clearly excluded
opts.rat: LAST in immediate sequence - prevention of recurrence and addressing underlying cause. Family meeting is critical - malignant tamponade is often a life-limiting event and patient-centered goals of care directly affect treatment decisions.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: After fluid resuscitation and pericardiocentesis, classify each finding over the next several hours and days.

opts.text: Within 5 minutes of pericardiocentesis (800 mL serosanguineous fluid drained): BP 122/76 from 86/64, HR 92 from 124, JVD resolved, pulsus paradoxus 4 mmHg from 18 mmHg, lactate normalizing over 2 hours, mental status clear and conversational - dramatic hemodynamic recovery characteristic of tamponade relief
opts.rat: The hallmark response of tamponade to drainage. Hemodynamics improve within seconds to minutes because the steep pericardial pressure-volume curve means small volume removal produces large pressure drops. This dramatic improvement essentially confirms the diagnosis retrospectively.

opts.text: Pericardial fluid cytology positive for adenocarcinoma cells consistent with NSCLC primary; oncology coordinates definitive treatment with surgical pericardial window plus intrapericardial bleomycin sclerotherapy to prevent recurrence; resumed second-line systemic chemotherapy with palliative intent
opts.rat: Confirmed malignant etiology guides targeted recurrence prevention. Pericardial window with sclerotherapy reduces recurrence rates substantially compared to drainage alone. Median survival with malignant tamponade is 2-4 months - definitive procedure improves quality of remaining time.

opts.text: Family meeting completed at bedside on hospital day 2; goals of care clarified including palliative care consultation; patient discharged on hospital day 5 with home palliative services, oncology follow-up, and clear instructions on warning signs of re-accumulation
opts.rat: Critical patient-centered outcome. Malignant tamponade is often a sentinel event prognostically. Aligning treatment with patient values prevents inappropriately aggressive interventions and supports quality of remaining life.

opts.text: Mid-procedure: sudden bradycardia to HR 38 and BP 60/30, then PEA arrest; pericardiocentesis aspirate is bright red blood instead of expected straw-colored serosanguineous fluid - team initiates ACLS and emergent transfer to OR
opts.rat: PROCEDURAL COMPLICATION - possible right ventricular puncture, coronary artery laceration, or unmasking of underlying aortic dissection with hemopericardium (which is why dissection must be considered before pericardiocentesis). Bright red blood that clots suggests cardiac chamber puncture. Emergent sternotomy and surgical repair required. Mortality high.

opts.text: Day 2 post-drainage: re-accumulation of pericardial effusion to moderate size on repeat bedside echo with return of pulsus paradoxus 14 mmHg, JVD recurring, mild hypotension; repeat drainage performed and decision made for definitive surgical pericardial window
opts.rat: RECURRENT TAMPONADE - common in malignant effusions (recurrence rate up to 50% within weeks if drainage alone). Pericardial window allows continuous drainage into pleural space and reduces recurrence dramatically. May also be performed via subxiphoid pericardial fenestration for less invasive option.

opts.text: Day 4: new fever 38.7, leukocytosis 18.4, purulent drainage from pericardial drain site, growing pericardial effusion on repeat echo with fibrinous stranding suggesting infection - team initiates broad-spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin + cefepime), removes and replaces drain at fresh site, surgical washout consultation
opts.rat: IATROGENIC PURULENT PERICARDITIS - rare but serious complication of indwelling pericardial drain. Mortality high without aggressive intervention. Removal of contaminated hardware, antimicrobial coverage for skin flora and gram-negatives, and surgical drainage often needed. Drain duration should be limited (typically 48-72 hours).

opts.text: Mild residual fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, and decreased exercise tolerance over 1-2 weeks - improving gradually; no new pulsus paradoxus, no JVD, stable echo without re-accumulation
opts.rat: Expected recovery course in setting of underlying metastatic malignancy and recovery from procedure. Stable hemodynamics and stable echo confirm no recurrent tamponade. Continued vigilance for re-accumulation appropriate but no acute intervention needed.

opts.text: Mild subxiphoid tenderness and small bruise at pericardiocentesis access site, resolving over 5-7 days; no expanding hematoma, normal chest auscultation, no SpO2 decline, no signs of pneumothorax on follow-up imaging
opts.rat: Expected post-procedural recovery. Concerning findings would be expanding hematoma, new chest pain or dyspnea (suggesting pneumothorax or hemothorax), new murmur (coronary injury), or hemodynamic instability.

title: Postpartum Hemorrhage with Hypovolemic Shock
subtitle: Obstetric Emergency & Class III Hemorrhagic Shock
nursesNote: 0730 — Client is a 32 y/o G4P4 female, 1.5 hours s/p spontaneous vaginal delivery of viable male infant, 4,250 g (macrosomic).
