opts.text: Administer furosemide 80 mg IV push AND give sublingual nitroglycerin 0.4 mg; prepare IV nitro drip
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical. Both reduce the workload on the failing heart within minutes. Furosemide also begins venodilation before the diuretic effect kicks in (~5–10 min onset). SL nitro is the bridge to IV nitro drip.

opts.text: Initiate BiPAP 12/5 cm H2O for hypoxemic-hypercapnic respiratory failure
opts.rat: #4 — Critical airway/breathing intervention. BiPAP often produces dramatic improvement within 15–30 minutes — improved oxygenation, reduced work of breathing, reduced preload. Coach the client through mask acclimation; reassure that the pressure helps push fluid out.

opts.text: Insert Foley catheter for hourly UOP monitoring; transfer to CCU; continue serial troponins and reassessment q15min initially
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management. Foley quantifies diuretic response. Serial troponins distinguish demand ischemia from active ACS. Ongoing reassessment catches improvement (allowing weaning) or deterioration (escalation to intubation or inotropic support).

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 1 hour post-treatment: RR 20, SpO2 95% on BiPAP, crackles improved to bases only, JVD reduced, UOP 850 mL in last hour
opts.rat: Excellent acute response. UOP >100 mL/hr confirms diuretic effectiveness. Resolution of crackles and reduction in JVD indicate decongestion is working. SpO2 improvement reflects fluid moving out of alveoli back into vascular space and then being excreted.

opts.text: 4 hours post: weaned from BiPAP to 2L NC, SpO2 94%, BP 142/82, RR 18, no more frothy sputum, conversational with family
opts.rat: Successful trajectory. The ability to wean from BiPAP indicates resolution of the respiratory failure. Comfort and conversation are critical bedside markers. BP normalizing reflects reduction in sympathetic surge as oxygenation improves.

opts.text: Day 4: weight down 6 kg from admission, transitioned to PO furosemide 80 mg BID, optimized GDMT (carvedilol, lisinopril, spironolactone, added empagliflozin SGLT2i); HF nurse teaching completed on daily weights, sodium restriction, and red flag symptoms
opts.rat: Successful discharge trajectory. Adding SGLT2 inhibitor is evidence-based GDMT addition (DAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced trials reduce HF hospitalization and mortality). Comprehensive teaching addresses the precipitant (dietary sodium, missed daily weights) — the single highest-impact discharge intervention.

opts.text: Despite BiPAP and 2 doses of furosemide, RR climbs to 38, SpO2 drops to 78%, client becoming exhausted with paradoxical breathing — emergent intubation
opts.rat: BiPAP failure indicating need for invasive mechanical ventilation. Paradoxical breathing (chest moving in during inspiration) is a sign of severe diaphragmatic fatigue. After intubation, lung-protective ventilation with PEEP titrated to oxygenation, sedation, and ongoing decongestion.

opts.text: Troponin rises from 0.18 to 4.8 ng/mL with new ST elevations V1-V4 on repeat ECG
opts.rat: Active anterior STEMI — the precipitating cause of decompensation, not just demand ischemia. Activate cath lab emergently. Aspirin and heparin now appropriate. PCI within the door-to-balloon window is the goal.

opts.text: BP drops to 76/42, extremities mottled and cold, lactate climbs to 6.8, UOP drops despite continued furosemide infusion
opts.rat: Cardiogenic shock — the failing pump can no longer maintain BP. Hold all afterload-reducing agents (nitrates, ACE/ARB). Start inotropic support (dobutamine or milrinone). Consider mechanical circulatory support: intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP), Impella, or VA-ECMO. Cardiology and surgery consultation.

opts.text: Refractory pulmonary edema with poor diuretic response despite high-dose IV furosemide and continuous infusion; UOP 25 mL/hr after 4 hours
opts.rat: Diuretic resistance. Options include: thiazide addition (metolazone) for sequential nephron blockade; ultrafiltration via dialysis access; CRRT if AKI develops. This subset of HF clients has the worst prognosis and may need advanced therapies (LVAD, transplant evaluation).

opts.text: New rapid atrial fibrillation with ventricular response 156 bpm; BP drops to 96/58 with new chest discomfort
opts.rat: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response acutely worsens HF by reducing diastolic filling time and removing the atrial 'kick' (loss of ~20% CO contribution). If hemodynamically unstable, synchronized cardioversion. If stable, IV diltiazem or amiodarone for rate control (avoid IV beta blocker in acute decompensation).

opts.text: Day 2: mild residual lower extremity edema 1+ improving slowly, weight down 3 kg, continued need for IV diuretics before transition to oral
opts.rat: Expected post-decompensation trajectory. Peripheral edema is the slowest to resolve (mobilization from interstitial space takes days). Continued IV diuresis for 2–3 days followed by transition to oral when fluid status, electrolytes, and renal function stable is the typical course.

opts.text: Continued need for overnight BiPAP for sleep apnea (newly diagnosed during this admission via sleep study)
opts.rat: Common comorbidity — obstructive sleep apnea is highly prevalent in HF (>50%) and contributes to decompensation. New OSA diagnosis with appropriate CPAP/BiPAP setup is part of comprehensive HF management. Continued use prevents future decompensations.

title: Adrenal Crisis (Addisonian Crisis)
subtitle: Endocrine Emergency & Glucocorticoid-First Resuscitation

patient.name: P. Wilson
patient.admitDate: Today, 1100 (EMS transport, found obtunded by daughter)

labs.v: Pending (expected critically low for stress state)
labs.r: >18 mcg/dL in stress; <5 mcg/dL diagnostic of AI

labs.v: Sinus tachycardia 138 with peaked T-waves V2-V4 (hyperkalemia), prolonged PR 224 ms

nursesNote: 1130 — 54 y/o female with known autoimmune primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease) diagnosed 6 years ago. Maintenance regimen: hydrocortisone 20 mg PO at 0700 + 10 mg PO at 1500, plus fludrocortisone 0.1 mg PO daily. Wears a medical alert bracelet stating 'Addison's disease — needs steroids in emergency.' Family reports 4 days of viral gastroenteritis with frequent vomiting and watery diarrhea. Per daughter, client has been unable to keep oral medications down for the past 36 hours and 'thought she could just rest and ride it out — didn't want to bother anyone.' Today the daughter found her obtunded on the couch, barely arousable, called 911. PMH: Addison's disease (autoimmune), Hashimoto's thyroiditis on levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily, GERD. EMS report: scene SpO2 92%, glucose 48 (administered IV D50W 25 g en route), HR 134 sinus tach, BP 72/40 unresponsive to first 1L 0.9% NS bolus, IV NS continued during transport. On ED arrival: client lethargic, oriented to person only, withdraws to noxious stimulus, follows simple commands intermittently. NOTABLE HYPERPIGMENTATION of buccal mucosa, palmar creases, knuckles, and an old C-section surgical scar — classic for primary AI (chronic ACTH elevation drives MSH production). Skin warm centrally but mottled and cool at knees and feet. Capillary refill 5 seconds. Heart sounds regular tachycardic without S3/S4 or murmur. Lungs clear bilaterally. Abdomen soft, mildly diffusely tender, hyperactive bowel sounds. No focal neurological deficits when arousable. Cardiac monitor: sinus tachycardia 138 with peaked T-waves V2-V4 on 12-lead ECG. UOP 30 mL since EMS arrival. Daughter very distressed: 'I should have brought her in days ago.'
providerOrders: Activate adrenal crisis protocol; ICU pending bed
Continuous cardiac monitoring (hyperkalemia ECG surveillance)
Continuous pulse oximetry, frequent (q5min) BP cycling until stable
100% O2 via non-rebreather mask
STAT cortisol level AND ACTH level — DRAW BEFORE hydrocortisone if access allows; do NOT delay hydrocortisone for this
Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV PUSH STAT — single most time-critical intervention
Follow with hydrocortisone 200 mg IV continuous infusion over 24 hours (OR 50 mg IV q6h)
0.9% NS 1 L IV bolus over 30 min (in addition to EMS's 1 L); then transition to D5NS at 200 mL/hr
POC glucose q15min initially; D50W 25 g IV push for recurrent glucose <70

Hyperkalemia management: calcium gluconate 1 g IV slowly (membrane stabilization for K+ 6.4 with peaked T-waves); regular insulin 10 units IV + D50W 25 g IV; albuterol 10 mg nebulized; consider sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) 30 g PO
Repeat BMP and K+ q2h until stable
STAT CBC with diff, ABG, lactate, blood cultures x2 from separate peripheral sites (rule out concurrent sepsis as trigger)
TSH, free T4 (concurrent Hashimoto's; severe hypothyroidism can compound)
Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics (ceftriaxone) if sepsis is suspected — do NOT delay if febrile or signs of infection
Indwelling Foley catheter for hourly UOP monitoring
NPO initially while obtunded; advance to clears as mentation and N/V resolve
Pre-discharge: stress dose education for client and family; prescribe injectable hydrocortisone (Solu-Cortef 100 mg) IM emergency kit for future severe illness; reinforce medical alert bracelet

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Hypotension 76/44 unresponsive to 1 L IV NS bolus from EMS
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — refractory shock pattern is the hallmark of adrenal crisis. Glucocorticoid deficiency causes vascular hyporesponsiveness to catecholamines; the vessels cannot constrict appropriately even when filled. Fluid bolus alone WILL NOT WORK — hydrocortisone is required. This is the single most distinguishing feature.

opts.text: Known autoimmune Addison's disease for 6 years; unable to take oral hydrocortisone for 36 hours due to vomiting; did not stress-dose during illness
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — establishes the diagnosis. Any chronic steroid user who becomes acutely ill and cannot absorb their replacement dose is at IMMEDIATE risk for adrenal crisis. The classic teaching: 'sick day rules' — double or triple the dose during minor illness, switch to IV for vomiting that prevents PO absorption. This client did neither.

opts.text: Hyperpigmentation of buccal mucosa, palmar creases, knuckles, and old surgical scar
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — distinguishes PRIMARY adrenal insufficiency (Addison's) from secondary AI (pituitary or chronic exogenous steroid suppression). In primary AI, the failed adrenals don't make cortisol → pituitary releases massive ACTH → ACTH's precursor (POMC) is also processed into MSH (melanocyte-stimulating hormone) → hyperpigmentation. Secondary AI does NOT have this finding.

opts.text: Sodium 122 mEq/L with concurrent potassium 6.4 mEq/L
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the pathognomonic electrolyte pattern of primary AI. Aldosterone normally reabsorbs sodium and excretes potassium in the distal tubule. Without aldosterone (mineralocorticoid deficiency), the kidneys waste sodium (→ hyponatremia + volume depletion) and retain potassium (→ hyperkalemia). This pattern is essentially diagnostic in the right clinical context.

opts.text: Hyperkalemia 6.4 with peaked T-waves in V2-V4 and prolonged PR 224 ms on ECG
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — immediately life-threatening. The combination of K+ >6 with ECG changes mandates urgent cardiac membrane stabilization (calcium gluconate) and K+ shifting (insulin/D50, albuterol). Untreated, can progress to widened QRS → sine wave → asystole within minutes.

opts.text: Hypoglycemia 52 mg/dL
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Cortisol is one of the four counter-regulatory hormones that maintain glucose (along with glucagon, epinephrine, and growth hormone). Cortisol deficiency removes this safety net — clients become hypoglycemic, especially when not eating. Treat with D50W IV and continuous D5-containing fluid; recheck q15min until stable.

opts.text: Eosinophilia 8% on differential
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — under-recognized finding. Cortisol normally suppresses eosinophil counts (which is why steroid administration drops eos within hours). In cortisol deficiency, eosinophils are uninhibited → mild eosinophilia. A small but clinically useful clue in undiagnosed AI.

opts.text: Mental status: lethargic, oriented to person only, withdraws to noxious stimulus
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — multifactorial encephalopathy. Contributors: hypoglycemia (cerebral fuel deficit), hyponatremia (cerebral edema), hypoperfusion (oxygen delivery), and cortisol deficiency itself (which has direct CNS effects on alertness and mood). Improves rapidly with combined treatment.

opts.text: Sinus tachycardia 138 with mottled cool extremities and cap refill 5 sec
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Circulation. Compensatory tachycardia for hypotension; peripheral vasoconstriction is the body's attempt to maintain central perfusion despite glucocorticoid-deficient vascular bed. Lactate 3.4 confirms cellular hypoperfusion. The persistence despite fluids confirms the diagnosis.

opts.text: Lactate 3.4 mmol/L with metabolic acidosis (pH 7.30, HCO3 18)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Lactate reflects tissue hypoperfusion + anaerobic metabolism. Will improve with hydrocortisone (restoring vascular tone) AND volume (filling the vascular space) AND glucose (providing aerobic fuel). All three are needed.

opts.text: Temperature 99.6°F
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue in itself, but raises the possibility of concurrent infection as a precipitating trigger. Draw blood cultures and consider empiric antibiotics if any other infection signs emerge. Don't anchor on 'just adrenal crisis' if sepsis features develop.

opts.text: Wearing a medical alert bracelet stating 'Addison's disease — needs steroids in emergency'
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — life-saving information. Medical alert jewelry is critical in clients with adrenal insufficiency because they may be found unconscious; first responders see the bracelet, administer empiric IM hydrocortisone, and save the client's life. Reinforce wearing at discharge.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: In primary adrenal insufficiency, BOTH cortisol AND aldosterone production fail — cortisol deficiency causes hypoglycemia, GI symptoms, eosinophilia, and vascular hyporesponsiveness; aldosterone deficiency causes hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and volume depletion
opts.rat: CORRECT — the dual-deficiency physiology that defines Addison's disease and distinguishes it from secondary AI (where aldosterone is preserved because the renin-angiotensin axis is independent of pituitary ACTH). This explains why primary AI patients have a more dramatic electrolyte picture than secondary AI patients.

opts.text: Hyperpigmentation in primary AI results from the failed adrenals not providing negative feedback to the pituitary → ACTH levels rise → ACTH's precursor proopiomelanocortin (POMC) is also processed into MSH (melanocyte-stimulating hormone) → increased melanin production
opts.rat: CORRECT — the elegant biochemistry explaining a classic exam finding. Hyperpigmentation appears in sun-exposed areas, scars made AFTER the disease developed, mucous membranes (buccal, gingival), palmar creases, areolas, and pressure points. This finding does NOT occur in secondary AI.

opts.text: Hypotension in adrenal crisis is REFRACTORY to fluids because glucocorticoids are required for normal vascular smooth muscle responsiveness to catecholamines — without cortisol, vessels cannot constrict appropriately even when intravascular volume is restored
opts.rat: CORRECT — THE most important pathophysiologic concept. Cortisol upregulates and sensitizes alpha-1 adrenergic receptors. Without it, endogenous and exogenous catecholamines have markedly reduced effect. This is why hydrocortisone (NOT fluids alone, NOT vasopressors alone) is the cornerstone of adrenal crisis treatment.

opts.text: The 4 days of vomiting and diarrhea created a 'perfect storm': inability to absorb oral hydrocortisone replacement, increased physiologic stress (cortisol demand normally rises 2–5x during illness), volume losses, and hypoglycemia from poor PO intake
opts.rat: CORRECT — the typical adrenal crisis trigger. Other classic precipitants: surgery without stress-dose coverage, severe infection (sepsis), trauma, MI, sudden withdrawal of chronic steroids. The lesson: any chronic steroid user MUST stress-dose during illness or switch to parenteral if vomiting.

opts.text: Hydrocortisone is the drug of choice for acute adrenal crisis because at high doses (>100 mg/24 hr) it has SIGNIFICANT mineralocorticoid activity in addition to glucocorticoid activity — so fludrocortisone usually does not need to be given separately during the acute phase
opts.rat: CORRECT — practical pharmacology. Hydrocortisone 100 mg has roughly equivalent mineralocorticoid effect to 0.1 mg fludrocortisone. Once the dose drops below 50 mg/day during taper, fludrocortisone is added or reinstituted. Dexamethasone is sometimes preferred when the diagnosis isn't yet confirmed because it does NOT interfere with the cosyntropin stimulation test.

opts.text: Findings represent severe sepsis (fever, tachycardia, hypotension, leukocytosis, lactic acidosis) and should be treated primarily with the Surviving Sepsis Hour-1 bundle
opts.rat: INCORRECT — anchoring error. While sepsis can mimic adrenal crisis (and can also PRECIPITATE adrenal crisis in known AI clients), the pattern here is more consistent with primary AI: known Addison's, missed stress doses, hyperpigmentation, classic electrolytes (low Na, high K), hypoglycemia, eosinophilia. Treat both possibilities (steroids + fluids + empiric antibiotics + cultures) but recognize that this is adrenal crisis first.

opts.text: Hydrocortisone should be held until cortisol and ACTH levels confirm the diagnosis
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — DANGEROUS. Adrenal crisis kills via hours of delay. Draw cortisol/ACTH BEFORE giving hydrocortisone IF doing so does not delay treatment more than a few minutes (single venipuncture). If confirmatory testing isn't immediately feasible, GIVE THE HYDROCORTISONE FIRST. Dexamethasone is an alternative when concerned about test interference; the cosyntropin stimulation test can be done after the first dose.

opts.text: Vasopressors (norepinephrine, vasopressin) are the FIRST-line therapy for the refractory hypotension and hydrocortisone is just a supportive measure
opts.rat: INCORRECT. Hydrocortisone IS the first-line therapy. Vasopressors are largely INEFFECTIVE without cortisol because the vascular bed is hyporesponsive. After hydrocortisone, if BP is still inadequate, norepinephrine is the second-line agent (and will work better once steroids restore vascular tone).

opts.text: Rapid 3% hypertonic saline correction is indicated for the sodium of 122 mEq/L
opts.rat: INCORRECT in this client. Hypertonic saline is reserved for severely symptomatic hyponatremia with seizures, coma, or imminent herniation. This client's mental status will improve with restoration of intravascular volume (with isotonic fluids), glucose, and cortisol. Rapid sodium correction (>8–10 mEq/L per 24 hours) risks central pontine myelinolysis — a devastating, often irreversible neurologic injury.

opts.text: The hyperkalemia will resolve on its own once volume status improves; no specific treatment is required acutely
opts.rat: INCORRECT — K+ 6.4 with peaked T-waves is a medical emergency requiring immediate cardiac membrane stabilization (calcium gluconate) and K+-shifting therapy (insulin/D50, albuterol, kayexalate). While hydrocortisone will eventually correct the mineralocorticoid deficiency, that takes hours; the hyperkalemia could cause a lethal arrhythmia in minutes.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Cardiovascular Collapse r/t glucocorticoid-deficient vascular hyporesponsiveness and volume depletion
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Circulation (ABCs). Refractory hypotension despite fluids confirms the depth of glucocorticoid deficiency. Without immediate hydrocortisone, the client cannot maintain perfusion and will progress to cardiovascular collapse. Hydrocortisone is the ONLY intervention that addresses the root cause.

opts.text: Risk for Lethal Arrhythmia r/t hyperkalemia 6.4 with peaked T-waves and prolonged PR
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. ECG changes confirm the K+ is affecting cardiac conduction. Without urgent membrane stabilization (calcium) and K+ shifting (insulin/D50, albuterol), progression to widened QRS, sine wave, and asystole can occur within minutes.

opts.text: Acute Hypoglycemia r/t cortisol-deficient gluconeogenesis
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Glucose 52 even AFTER 25 g D50W from EMS suggests ongoing hypoglycemia driver. Brain depends on glucose; profound hypoglycemia causes seizures and irreversible cerebral injury. Continuous D5-containing fluids plus serial POC glucose checks address this.

opts.text: Severe Hyponatremia r/t aldosterone-deficient renal sodium wasting
opts.rat: #4 — Important but secondary to immediate hemodynamic and arrhythmia risks. Will correct gradually with volume restoration using isotonic fluids and hydrocortisone (which has mineralocorticoid effect at high doses). Avoid rapid correction to prevent CPM.

opts.text: Knowledge Deficit r/t sick day rules and stress dosing for chronic adrenal insufficiency
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in this acute moment but CRITICAL for safe discharge. This is the reason for the crisis. Without comprehensive teaching (sick day rules, stress dosing protocol, injectable hydrocortisone kit for emergencies, medical alert bracelet reinforcement), recurrence is highly likely. Family education matters because the client may be obtunded next time.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV PUSH STAT, followed by 200 mg IV continuous infusion over 24 hours
opts.rat: Single most time-critical intervention. Acts within 30 minutes; full effect within 4–6 hours. The 100 mg loading dose ensures rapid receptor saturation. Continuous infusion maintains stable levels (vs. peaks and troughs of q6h bolus dosing, although that's also acceptable). At >100 mg/24 hr, mineralocorticoid effect covers aldosterone deficiency.

opts.text: Draw cortisol AND ACTH levels BEFORE administering hydrocortisone if access allows (single venipuncture); do NOT delay hydrocortisone if drawing labs would take more than a few minutes
opts.rat: Pre-treatment cortisol and ACTH provide diagnostic confirmation: in primary AI, cortisol is low AND ACTH is high; in secondary AI, both are low. Once hydrocortisone is given, the cortisol assay is unreliable. BUT: never delay hydrocortisone more than a few minutes for testing — the test confirms what you already suspect clinically; the steroid saves the life.

opts.text: 0.9% NS 1 L IV bolus, followed by D5NS at 200 mL/hr maintenance
opts.rat: Restores intravascular volume; D5 component addresses concurrent hypoglycemia. NS gradually replaces sodium without rapid correction (avoid CPM). Hydrocortisone alone cannot fix the volume depletion — both are needed.

opts.text: D50W 25 g IV push for glucose 52, then recheck q15min and repeat as needed
opts.rat: Glucose <70 in a symptomatic client requires immediate correction. EMS already gave one dose; recheck and repeat if recurrent. Long-term coverage comes from the continuous D5-containing fluids.

opts.text: Calcium gluconate 1 g IV slowly for hyperkalemia with ECG changes
opts.rat: K+ 6.4 with peaked T-waves and prolonged PR mandates membrane stabilization. Onset 1–3 min; duration ~30–60 min. Does NOT lower K+ but protects cardiac membrane while other interventions take effect. Use calcium GLUCONATE preferentially (less tissue irritation than calcium chloride for peripheral IV).

opts.text: Regular insulin 10 units IV + D50W 25 g IV simultaneously to shift K+ intracellularly
opts.rat: Drives K+ into cells within minutes. The D50W simultaneously addresses the hypoglycemia AND prevents iatrogenic hypoglycemia from insulin. Albuterol nebulized adds additional beta-2-mediated shift.

opts.text: Blood cultures x2 from separate peripheral sites before any antibiotics
opts.rat: Sepsis is a known precipitant of adrenal crisis in chronic AI clients (and can occur concurrently). The viral gastroenteritis history is most likely the trigger, but bacterial superinfection or unrelated sepsis must be excluded. If any sepsis signs emerge, treat empirically with broad-spectrum antibiotics.

opts.text: Withhold hydrocortisone until the cortisol level returns from the lab to confirm the diagnosis
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS — most important trap in this case. Adrenal crisis kills via delay. Treat based on clinical suspicion. Confirmatory testing follows. Many providers reflexively wait for labs in non-emergent situations; this is one where you cannot.

opts.text: Norepinephrine drip at 0.1 mcg/kg/min as the FIRST-line agent for refractory hypotension
opts.rat: Vasopressors are not first-line in adrenal crisis because vascular beds are hyporesponsive without cortisol. Give hydrocortisone first. If hypotension persists 30–60 min after steroids and adequate volume, norepinephrine becomes appropriate as second-line (and will work much better with cortisol on board).

opts.text: Rapid correction of sodium from 122 to 138 mEq/L over 4 hours with 3% hypertonic saline
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS — central pontine myelinolysis risk. Sodium correction should not exceed 8–10 mEq/L per 24 hours (some guidelines stricter, 6 mEq/L/24hr in chronic hyponatremia). 3% saline is reserved for severely symptomatic hyponatremia (seizures, coma, herniation). This client's mental status will improve with treatment of the underlying crisis.

opts.text: IV potassium replacement to 'protect against arrhythmias'
opts.rat: ⚠️ CATASTROPHIC — K+ is critically HIGH at 6.4. Adding more potassium would cause immediate arrhythmia. The mineralocorticoid deficiency in primary AI causes K+ retention, the OPPOSITE of what occurs in chronic loop diuretic users. Always check the actual K+ before assuming.

opts.text: Discharge home once mental status improves with instructions to resume oral hydrocortisone tomorrow
opts.rat: Premature and unsafe. Client needs: (1) ICU/IMC observation through resolution of the crisis, (2) IV steroids for 24–48 hours, (3) gradual taper to oral at 2–3x maintenance dose, (4) complete sick day rule education, (5) injectable hydrocortisone emergency kit, (6) plan for outpatient endocrine follow-up, (7) reinforcement of medical alert bracelet.

opts.text: Discharge teaching: sick day rules (double or triple maintenance dose during minor illness, switch to IV/IM for severe vomiting), Solu-Cortef 100 mg IM emergency kit, medical alert bracelet, low threshold to return to ED
opts.rat: Critical for safe disposition and recurrence prevention. The reason this crisis occurred was lack of stress dosing during illness. Every chronic steroid user should leave the hospital with: written sick day rules, an injectable hydrocortisone kit they (or family) can administer at home, and a medical alert bracelet or wallet card.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Call rapid response / endocrine / ICU; ensure team mobilization; confirm IV access patent; do not leave the client
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Adrenal crisis is a time-sensitive emergency requiring multidisciplinary care. Confirming IV access ensures the next step (hydrocortisone) can be given without delay. Do not leave the client alone — mental status can deteriorate rapidly.

opts.text: Draw cortisol and ACTH levels (single venipuncture if possible), then administer hydrocortisone 100 mg IV PUSH immediately
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. Cortisol/ACTH BEFORE steroid if access allows — provides diagnostic confirmation. Then IV push hydrocortisone immediately. If drawing labs would take more than 2–3 minutes, give the hydrocortisone FIRST and draw labs after. Saving the life trumps confirming the diagnosis.

opts.text: Administer calcium gluconate 1 g IV slowly for hyperkalemia with ECG changes; follow with regular insulin 10 units IV + D50W 25 g IV
opts.rat: #3 — Concurrent emergency. Hyperkalemic ECG changes mandate immediate membrane stabilization and K+ shifting. Acts within minutes. Buys time while hydrocortisone and definitive K+ removal (kayexalate, dialysis if needed) take effect.

opts.text: Initiate 0.9% NS 1 L IV bolus, then D5NS at 200 mL/hr; check POC glucose q15min and give D50W IV push if glucose <70
opts.rat: #4 — Volume + glucose support. Restores intravascular volume (working with the hydrocortisone-restored vascular tone). D5 component prevents recurrent hypoglycemia. Frequent glucose monitoring catches recurrence before symptoms.

opts.text: Insert Foley for hourly UOP monitoring; draw remaining labs (CBC, ABG, lactate, blood cultures, TSH); arrange ICU transfer; begin stress-dose education with family
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management. Foley quantifies fluid response. Labs identify precipitating sepsis and concurrent conditions. ICU transfer ensures ongoing high-acuity care. Beginning education early (especially with the daughter present) starts the safe-discharge preparation.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 1 hour post-hydrocortisone: BP 104/68, HR 96, mental status clearing to A&Ox4, ECG with normalized T-waves, glucose 98
opts.rat: TEXTBOOK successful resuscitation. Hydrocortisone restored vascular tone (BP up, HR down as compensation resolves); cardiac stabilization resolved the K+ ECG changes; volume + D5 corrected glucose. The dramatic clinical response within 1 hour is characteristic of adrenal crisis and is sometimes diagnostic in itself.

opts.text: 4 hours post: Na trending up to 130, K+ down to 4.8 after combined treatment, lactate cleared to 1.4, UOP 70 mL/hr; transferred to IMC
opts.rat: Excellent trajectory across all parameters. Sodium correction is appropriate (122 → 130 over 4 hours = 8 mEq/L/4hr — actually slightly fast; slow down maintenance fluids to ensure correction <8–10 mEq/L per 24 hours total). K+ down through both hydrocortisone (mineralocorticoid effect) and insulin shift.

opts.text: Day 3: transitioned to PO hydrocortisone 40 mg AM + 20 mg PM (2x maintenance) with planned taper to baseline over 5–7 days; receives Solu-Cortef 100 mg IM kit, family demonstrates kit use, reinforced medical alert bracelet, scheduled endo follow-up in 1 week
opts.rat: Comprehensive disposition. The IM emergency kit is the single most impactful intervention to prevent the next crisis — the family can now administer it if she's vomiting at home. Plan for endocrine follow-up ensures ongoing optimization. Cortisol and ACTH (drawn pre-hydrocortisone) returned at 1.2 mcg/dL and 580 pg/mL respectively — confirming primary AI.

opts.text: Despite hydrocortisone, 3 L NS, and norepinephrine 0.4 mcg/kg/min, BP remains 78/46 with rising lactate 5.4 and oliguria
opts.rat: Refractory shock despite combination therapy. Consider: (1) concurrent septic shock — broaden antibiotics, add vasopressin; (2) inadequate steroid dose — increase to 200 mg/24h continuous infusion or 50 mg IV q4h; (3) other shock causes (cardiogenic from MI in older client). Aggressive workup with bedside echo and consideration of mechanical circulatory support.

opts.text: Develops new focal seizure activity with right-sided weakness; head CT shows osmotic demyelination changes in the pons
opts.rat: Central Pontine Myelinolysis (osmotic demyelination syndrome) from too-rapid sodium correction. Devastating, often irreversible. PREVENTABLE: sodium correction must not exceed 8–10 mEq/L per 24 hours. If overcorrection occurs, can cautiously give free water (D5W) to lower sodium back down within first 24 hours. This is the worst preventable complication of adrenal crisis management.

opts.text: Blood cultures grow E. coli at 24 hours; client has been on empiric ceftriaxone since admission; UA confirms pyelonephritis; antibiotics narrowed
opts.rat: Concurrent urosepsis was contributing to crisis. Empiric broad-spectrum coverage from admission caught it; identification allows narrowing (de-escalation = antimicrobial stewardship). Clinical response confirms both adrenal crisis AND sepsis were treated. Common pattern — sepsis precipitates crisis in chronic AI clients.

opts.text: Day 1: develops new chest pain, ECG with ST elevations II, III, aVF; troponin rises; emergent cath shows RCA occlusion
opts.rat: Acute inferior MI — can both precipitate adrenal crisis (massive stress) and occur secondary to the crisis (demand ischemia from hypotension + tachycardia in known CAD patient). Activate cath lab. Continue hydrocortisone — steroid pretreatment is actually protective in cardiac surgery and possibly in ACS as well.

opts.text: Mild residual fatigue and weakness expected for several days; advised gradual return to baseline activity
opts.rat: Expected post-crisis course. The body needs time to recalibrate after a major stress event. Counsel client and family that fatigue may last 1–2 weeks; gradual return to activity is appropriate. Importantly, this fatigue is NOT a sign of recurrent crisis — true crisis would have hemodynamic and electrolyte derangements.

opts.text: Hyperpigmentation of buccal mucosa, palmar creases, and old scar unchanged on discharge
opts.rat: Expected. Hyperpigmentation in primary AI is a chronic finding from years of elevated ACTH/MSH. It does NOT reverse with acute hydrocortisone treatment. Long-term, with optimal replacement and proper ACTH suppression, pigmentation may slowly fade — but this takes months to years.

opts.text: Continued need for IV hydrocortisone for 24–48 hours before transitioning to oral; family completes return-demonstration of injectable hydrocortisone IM kit administration
opts.rat: Expected discharge preparation course. Education and demonstration ensure that if she ever vomits/decompensates at home again, the family can give IM Solu-Cortef and call 911. This single intervention dramatically reduces recurrent crisis mortality.

title: Myasthenic vs Cholinergic Crisis
subtitle: Neuromuscular Respiratory Failure & the Tensilon Differential

patient.name: P. Donnelly
patient.room: ED Acute Bed 4, neurology + MICU evaluating

labs.r: More negative than −60 normal; intubate if less negative than −20 to −30
60–80 mL/kg normal; intubate if <15 mL/kg or <1 L

labs.n: Acetylcholine receptor (AChR) binding antibody — known from prior workup

labs.v: Bibasilar plate-like atelectasis; lung fields otherwise clear; no consolidation or pulmonary edema

labs.n: CT sinus (from urgent care 5 days ago)
labs.v: Mild bilateral maxillary mucosal thickening; no air-fluid levels; no acute findings
FAILED — nasal regurgitation with 5 mL water; wet, gurgly voice quality after sip
labs.r: Passes 30 mL water trial without choking or wet voice

labs.v: 4 mm bilateral, briskly reactive (NORMAL — no miosis)
No excessive salivation, lacrimation, diaphoresis. No abdominal cramping, urinary urgency, or diarrhea. No fasciculations.

nursesNote: 1410 — 64 y/o female with an 8-year history of generalized seropositive (AChR-Ab +) myasthenia gravis, normally well-controlled on pyridostigmine 60 mg PO QID, prednisone 20 mg PO daily, and mycophenolate mofetil 1,000 mg PO BID. Followed in our neurology clinic; last clinic visit 1 month ago was at her baseline (NIF −75 cm H2O, FVC 3.2 L, no bulbar symptoms, fully ambulatory and working part-time as a librarian). Husband reports she developed nasal congestion and mild facial pressure 6 days ago and was seen at urgent care. Started on AZITHROMYCIN 500 mg PO day 1, then 250 mg daily x 4 days (Z-Pak) for presumed bacterial sinusitis — neither the urgent care provider nor the client recognized that macrolide antibiotics can precipitate myasthenic crisis. The patient did not call her neurologist before filling the antibiotic. Over the last 4 days she has developed progressively worsening ptosis (bilateral, worse late in the day), diplopia on lateral gaze, generalized fatigability, a soft and nasal-sounding voice, and difficulty swallowing — to the point that this morning she could NOT swallow her 0800 pyridostigmine dose without choking. She has not taken pyridostigmine since the 2300 dose last night. Husband became alarmed when he found her struggling to breathe on the couch and brought her to the ED. On assessment: alert, anxious, sitting upright on the gurney, dyspneic with paradoxical abdominal breathing pattern, speaking in short broken phrases with a softly nasal voice; bilateral ptosis worse on the left; weak neck flexion (unable to lift head off bed against gravity); shoulder shrug weak (3/5 bilaterally); proximal lower extremity weakness (3/5 hip flexion); distal strength preserved. CRITICALLY for the differential: pupils are 4 mm and briskly reactive (NO miosis), there is NO excessive salivation, NO lacrimation, NO diaphoresis, NO urinary urgency, NO diarrhea, NO abdominal cramping, NO muscle fasciculations, and NO bradycardia — i.e., absent SLUDGE/B symptoms. Bedside swallow screen FAILED — nasal regurgitation with 5 mL of water and a wet, gurgly voice quality. NIF has dropped from a baseline of −75 cm H2O to −18 cm H2O. FVC is 0.9 L (≈14 mL/kg). ABG shows type II respiratory failure (pH 7.30, PaCO2 54, PaO2 64 on 4 L NC). Pyridostigmine level sent but not back. NPO; neurology and MICU at bedside. Clinical picture: MYASTHENIC CRISIS precipitated by macrolide antibiotic exposure and missed maintenance pyridostigmine, with imminent respiratory failure and high aspiration risk from bulbar weakness.
providerOrders: Strict NPO (bulbar weakness with failed swallow screen — high aspiration risk)
Continuous cardiac monitoring, continuous pulse oximetry, end-tidal CO2
DISCONTINUE azithromycin immediately; document on the chart as a 'myasthenia-precipitating drug — avoid future use'
Bedside NIF and FVC q2h, more frequently if trending down
If NIF less negative than −20 OR FVC <15 mL/kg OR clinical paradoxical breathing/bulbar incompetence: prepare for intubation
Avoid succinylcholine (prolonged duration in MG); use rocuronium at REDUCED dose if RSI required; have neostigmine + sugammadex available
Avoid BiPAP if bulbar weakness is severe (cannot protect airway) — intubate directly if respiratory failure

Edrophonium (Tensilon) test if diagnosis uncertain (myasthenic vs cholinergic): 2 mg IV test dose; if no adverse reaction in 1 min, give 8 mg IV (total 10 mg). Atropine 0.5–1 mg IV at bedside in pre-drawn syringe (treats bradycardia/cholinergic adverse effects).
HOLD pyridostigmine pending Tensilon result if cholinergic crisis cannot be confidently excluded
Initiate PLASMAPHERESIS (5 exchanges over 7–10 days) OR IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days — neurology to choose
Continue prednisone 20 mg PO daily (do not abruptly stop chronic immunosuppression); consider IV methylprednisolone if NPO and worsening
Hold mycophenolate during acute infection workup; resume when stable per neurology
Aspiration precautions: HOB elevated 30°, oral suction at bedside
DVT prophylaxis: sequential compression devices; enoxaparin if not bleeding risk
MICU admission; neurology consultation
Family teaching once stable: list of myasthenia-precipitating drugs and the importance of contacting the MG neurologist before starting any new medication

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: NIF dropped from −75 cm H2O (baseline) to −18 cm H2O; FVC 0.9 L (≈14 mL/kg)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the most important objective measurements of neuromuscular respiratory failure. The 'rule of 20-30-40' for impending intubation in MG: FVC <20 mL/kg, NIF less negative than −30 cm H2O, MEP <40 cm H2O. This client meets ALL THREE — intubation should be planned NOW, not when she crashes.

opts.text: ABG: pH 7.30, PaCO2 54, PaO2 64 on 4 L NC — type II (hypercapnic) respiratory failure
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. PaCO2 elevation indicates the respiratory muscles can no longer maintain alveolar ventilation. This is FATIGUE, not 'just hypoxia.' In neuromuscular disease, hypercapnia is a late sign — by the time CO2 rises, respiratory arrest is close.

opts.text: Failed bedside swallow screen with nasal regurgitation of water and wet, gurgly voice after sip
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — bulbar weakness. Bulbar dysfunction means the client cannot protect her airway. Strict NPO. Aspiration is a major cause of morbidity in MG crisis — and one of the strongest indications to intubate EARLY, even before NIF/FVC reach the absolute thresholds.

opts.text: Paradoxical abdominal breathing (chest moving in during inspiration) and accessory muscle use
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diaphragmatic fatigue. The diaphragm normally pulls the abdomen out during inspiration. When it fails, the abdomen retracts inward (paradoxical movement). Accessory muscle recruitment is a temporary compensation — those muscles also fatigue, and the client is now using them just to breathe at rest.

opts.text: Azithromycin (Z-Pak) started 5 days ago at urgent care — a macrolide antibiotic — with progressive weakness developing over the same period
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — IDENTIFIES THE PRECIPITANT. Macrolides (azithromycin, clarithromycin, erythromycin), aminoglycosides (gentamicin, tobramycin), fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin), beta-blockers, magnesium, certain anesthetics, and quinine all impair neuromuscular transmission and can precipitate myasthenic crisis. DISCONTINUE the offending drug and flag it as 'avoid lifelong.'

opts.text: Missed her 0800 pyridostigmine dose because she could no longer swallow it; last dose was 2300 the night before
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — undermedication contributing. Pyridostigmine half-life is ~3–4 hours. By the time she reached the ED, she had been without her AChE inhibitor for ~14 hours. Missing the drug combined with the macrolide trigger is the two-hit combination that produced this crisis.

opts.text: Bilateral ptosis, weak neck flexion, weak shoulder shrug (3/5), proximal lower extremity weakness (3/5)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the classic generalized MG distribution. Ocular, bulbar, neck flexor, and proximal limb weakness with relative sparing of distal strength. The 'fatigability pattern' — strength worsens with repeated effort and improves with rest.

opts.text: Pupils 4 mm and briskly reactive (NO miosis); no excessive salivation, lacrimation, urinary urgency, diarrhea, abdominal cramping, fasciculations, or bradycardia — i.e., ABSENT SLUDGE/B symptoms
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — these PERTINENT NEGATIVES help distinguish MYASTHENIC from CHOLINERGIC crisis. SLUDGE = Salivation, Lacrimation, Urinary incontinence, Diarrhea, GI cramping/Emesis; plus B for bronchospasm/bradycardia. The mnemonic 'DUMBBELS' or 'SLUDGE' captures muscarinic excess from too much pyridostigmine. Their ABSENCE here makes myasthenic crisis (undermedication + drug trigger) much more likely than cholinergic crisis (overmedication).

opts.text: Speaking in short broken phrases with a soft, nasal voice quality
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Nasal voice quality indicates weakness of the palatal musculature (bulbar dysfunction); short phrases reflect inadequate breath support. Both worsen aspiration and ventilatory failure risk. The bedside speech pattern is one of the most sensitive early markers of crisis progression.

opts.text: Temperature 99.8°F
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue in isolation. Low-grade temperature is nonspecific — can occur with increased work of breathing alone. No leukocytosis, no localizing infectious signs other than the resolving sinusitis. Document but not actionable.

opts.text: Sodium 138, potassium 4.0, magnesium 2.0
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue — values are normal. However, magnesium specifically deserves a 'no IV mag, ever' flag in the MG chart: IV magnesium impairs neuromuscular transmission and can precipitate crisis. Normal serum Mg is fine; therapeutic IV magnesium boluses are CONTRAINDICATED.

opts.text: Bibasilar plate-like atelectasis on chest X-ray
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue. Mild atelectasis is expected with shallow breathing and reduced cough strength. Will improve with respiratory support and improved tidal volumes. Not a sign of consolidation/pneumonia and does not change immediate management.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Antibodies against the post-synaptic acetylcholine receptor reduce available receptors at the neuromuscular junction; this client's normally adequate ACh release now fails to depolarize the muscle with repetitive effort — producing fatigability that worsens later in the day
opts.rat: CORRECT — the central pathophysiology. AChR-Ab binding causes receptor internalization and complement-mediated NMJ damage. With each successive nerve impulse, the rundown of available receptors produces decremental muscle response (the same finding seen on repetitive nerve stimulation EMG). Hence: stronger in the morning, weaker through the day.

opts.text: Macrolides (and aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, IV magnesium) impair neuromuscular transmission by interfering with ACh release or postsynaptic function — DISCONTINUE the offending agent at once
opts.rat: CORRECT and a critical clinical decision. Mechanisms vary (macrolides have direct curare-like postsynaptic effects; aminoglycosides reduce presynaptic ACh release; magnesium competes with calcium at presynaptic terminals) but the common result is impaired NMJ function in a client already running on reduced receptor reserve. ALL MG clients should have an 'avoid' list posted at the bedside and in their chart.

opts.text: Myasthenic crisis (UNDERmedication / trigger): weakness from too little ACh effect; absent SLUDGE; pupils normal/dilated; Tensilon IMPROVES strength; treatment is plasmapheresis or IVIG + maintenance pyridostigmine
opts.rat: CORRECT — the myasthenic side of the differential. Insufficient ACh action at the NMJ from antibody-mediated receptor depletion plus precipitants. Edrophonium (Tensilon) — a short-acting AChE inhibitor — provides transient additional ACh, producing measurable improvement in ptosis, neck flexion, or grip strength within 1–2 minutes.

opts.text: Cholinergic crisis (OVERmedication): weakness from excessive ACh saturating and desensitizing the NMJ; PRESENT SLUDGE symptoms; miosis; fasciculations; bradycardia; Tensilon WORSENS strength; treatment is HOLD pyridostigmine, atropine for muscarinic symptoms, supportive care
opts.rat: CORRECT — the cholinergic side. Excess ACh from too-frequent or too-high pyridostigmine doses initially overstimulates and then desensitizes nicotinic receptors. The muscarinic 'side effects' (SLUDGE) precede or accompany the nicotinic weakness. Atropine blocks the muscarinic effects but does NOT reverse the nicotinic weakness — that simply has to wash out with time.

opts.text: In neuromuscular respiratory failure, HYPERCAPNIA is a LATE sign — by the time PaCO2 rises, respiratory arrest is close; clinicians must intubate based on TRENDS in NIF/FVC and bulbar function, NOT wait for ABG to be 'bad enough'
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield teaching point. Unlike COPD where hypercapnia is a chronic compensated baseline, in MG crisis a rising CO2 reflects diaphragmatic fatigue and impending arrest. The bedside metrics (NIF, FVC, bulbar exam) detect failure HOURS before the ABG does. Intubation in MG is a deliberate, anticipated, controlled event — never wait for the crash.

opts.text: Severe bulbar weakness with failed swallow screen and nasal regurgitation creates HIGH aspiration risk; BiPAP may not be appropriate when airway protection is compromised — direct intubation is often safer
opts.rat: CORRECT. BiPAP is useful in selected MG clients with respiratory muscle fatigue BUT intact bulbar function and protected airway. With this client's failed swallow and pooled secretions, BiPAP risks aspiration of gastric contents under positive pressure. Intubation provides definitive airway protection and assisted ventilation.

opts.text: This client's clinical picture (absent SLUDGE, recent pyridostigmine miss, macrolide trigger, normal pupils, no fasciculations) strongly favors MYASTHENIC crisis over cholinergic crisis
opts.rat: CORRECT — the bedside differential. Each pertinent negative is part of the picture: normal pupils argue against cholinergic; absent SLUDGE argues against cholinergic; recent macrolide and missed pyridostigmine doses argue FOR myasthenic. If clinical picture were ambiguous, a Tensilon test would resolve it — but here the clinical picture is fairly clear.

opts.text: Succinylcholine should be used at standard doses for rapid sequence intubation in this client
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT and dangerous. MG clients have UNPREDICTABLE responses to neuromuscular blocking drugs: resistant to succinylcholine due to reduced receptors but PROLONGED depolarization once given; markedly SENSITIVE to non-depolarizing agents (rocuronium, vecuronium) with greatly extended duration. If RSI is required, use rocuronium at REDUCED dose and have sugammadex available for reversal.

opts.text: IV magnesium sulfate should be given routinely to all MG clients in crisis for neuroprotection
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — DIRECTLY CONTRAINDICATED. Magnesium competes with calcium at the presynaptic terminal, reducing ACh release. IV magnesium can precipitate or worsen MG crisis. Some MG client charts post a 'NO IV MAGNESIUM' alert. Even oral magnesium-containing laxatives should be reviewed with the neurologist.

opts.text: Plasmapheresis works by removing the AChR antibodies from circulation; IVIG works by saturating Fc receptors and modulating the immune response — BOTH are short-term bridges that allow steroids and chronic immunosuppression to take effect over weeks
opts.rat: CORRECT. Plasmapheresis (5 exchanges over 7–10 days) removes circulating pathogenic antibodies — fastest onset (improvement within days) but technically demanding and requires good vascular access. IVIG (0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days) has equivalent efficacy with a different mechanism, no need for central line, and useful when plasmapheresis is unavailable. Choice depends on local availability and patient factors.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Impaired Spontaneous Ventilation r/t neuromuscular weakness with diaphragmatic fatigue (NIF −18, FVC 14 mL/kg, paradoxical breathing, PaCO2 54)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Breathing (ABCs). The client is in established type II respiratory failure with all three '20-30-40' criteria met. Intubation is anticipated and should occur before respiratory arrest, NOT after.

opts.text: Risk for Aspiration r/t bulbar weakness with failed swallow, nasal regurgitation, and pooling secretions
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Airway. Bulbar weakness compromises the client's ability to protect her airway. Strict NPO, HOB elevated 30°, oral suction at bedside. Bulbar failure with poor secretion clearance is one of the strongest single indications to intubate even if NIF/FVC have not crossed thresholds.

opts.text: Need to Differentiate Myasthenic vs Cholinergic Crisis (treatment is opposite for each)
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical decision. Treatment differs fundamentally: myasthenic = MORE ACh effect (pyridostigmine + immunomodulation); cholinergic = LESS ACh effect (HOLD pyridostigmine + atropine for muscarinic symptoms). Mistake either direction worsens the patient. Clinical picture + Tensilon test if needed.

opts.text: Decreased Mobility / Activity Intolerance r/t generalized neuromuscular weakness (proximal 3/5, neck flexion 3/5)
opts.rat: #4 — Real but not immediately life-threatening. Bed rest with assistance; range-of-motion, pressure injury prevention, DVT prophylaxis. Strength will improve as immunomodulation takes effect. Early mobilization once weaned from ventilator.

opts.text: Deficient Knowledge r/t MG-precipitating drugs and the importance of contacting the neurologist before starting any new medication
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in this acute moment, but THE preventable factor for the NEXT crisis. The urgent care visit that started this whole admission is the perfect teaching example. List of dangerous drugs (macrolides, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, IV magnesium, certain anesthetics), MedicAlert bracelet, and a 'call MG neurologist before any new med' rule.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Discontinue azithromycin immediately and document on the chart and home medication list as a 'myasthenia-precipitating drug — avoid'
opts.rat: Removes the active precipitant. Most importantly, prevents future prescribers (urgent care, dentist, ED) from making the same error. Many institutions use an EMR allergy/intolerance flag specifically for 'MG-precipitating drugs.'

opts.text: Strict NPO with HOB elevated 30°, oral suction at bedside
opts.rat: Aspiration precautions in a client with failed swallow and pooling secretions. Even small aspiration events in a neuromuscular patient with weak cough can produce dramatic pneumonia and worsen the crisis.

opts.text: Continuous cardiac monitoring, continuous pulse oximetry, end-tidal CO2, and serial NIF/FVC every 2 hours (more frequently if trending down)
opts.rat: Bedside trends drive the intubation decision. The single biggest mistake in MG crisis is waiting for the ABG or for respiratory arrest. NIF/FVC are checked by the respiratory therapist and decline is a clear indication to plan intubation EARLY.

opts.text: Prepare for intubation now: anticipated (not crash) intubation given NIF −18, FVC 0.9 L, paradoxical breathing, and failed swallow
opts.rat: All three '20-30-40' criteria are met PLUS bulbar incompetence. Anticipated intubation in a controlled setting with experienced operator and full pre-oxygenation is vastly safer than a crash intubation after respiratory arrest in a client with already-compromised airway protection.

opts.text: If rapid sequence intubation needed: use rocuronium at REDUCED dose (avoid succinylcholine in MG); have sugammadex available for reversal; atropine and standard resuscitation drugs at bedside
opts.rat: MG clients are markedly sensitive to non-depolarizing NMBDs. Reduced doses produce normal blockade with prolonged duration; standard doses produce prolonged paralysis. Sugammadex selectively reverses rocuronium and is the preferred reversal agent. Succinylcholine is avoided — unpredictable response.

opts.text: Edrophonium (Tensilon) test ONLY if the myasthenic vs cholinergic differential cannot be made clinically: 2 mg IV test dose; if no adverse reaction at 1 min, give 8 mg IV (total 10 mg). Atropine 0.5–1 mg IV pre-drawn at bedside.
opts.rat: Reasonable diagnostic intervention when the clinical picture is ambiguous. Tensilon (edrophonium) is a short-acting AChE inhibitor (~5 min duration). IMPROVES strength → myasthenic crisis; WORSENS or no change → cholinergic crisis. Have atropine at bedside in case of severe muscarinic adverse effects (bradycardia, bronchospasm, severe salivation). NOTE: many centers now skip the Tensilon test and rely on history + clinical picture; it is becoming less commonly performed but remains testable NCLEX content.

opts.text: Initiate plasmapheresis (5 exchanges over 7–10 days) OR IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days — neurology to choose based on local availability and patient factors
opts.rat: Both are first-line immunomodulating treatments for myasthenic crisis. Plasmapheresis: fastest onset (days), requires central venous access, can be hemodynamically demanding. IVIG: equivalent efficacy, no need for central line, monitor for headache/aseptic meningitis/renal injury/thromboembolism. Either is appropriate.

opts.text: Continue prednisone 20 mg PO daily (or IV methylprednisolone if NPO) — do NOT abruptly stop chronic immunosuppression
opts.rat: Abrupt cessation of chronic steroids risks adrenal insufficiency AND removes baseline immunosuppression. Continue at current dose; some centers ADD high-dose IV methylprednisolone (1 g IV daily x 3–5 days) — though this can transiently WORSEN MG strength in the first week, which is why plasmapheresis/IVIG is given concurrently. Coordinate with neurology.

opts.text: Magnesium sulfate 4 g IV bolus to 'protect the airway and prevent seizures'
opts.rat: ⚠️ DIRECTLY CONTRAINDICATED. IV magnesium impairs neuromuscular transmission and can precipitate or worsen MG crisis. There is no indication for it here (no eclampsia, no severe asthma, no torsades). This is the kind of order a covering provider might write without realizing the MG context — the bedside nurse should QUESTION it before administering.

opts.text: Gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV q24h empirically for aspiration pneumonia
opts.rat: ⚠️ Aminoglycosides are CONTRAINDICATED in MG (reduce presynaptic ACh release → worsen weakness). The chest X-ray does not show consolidation, and there is no clinical evidence of pneumonia. Even if pneumonia were present, MG-safe alternatives exist (ceftriaxone, vancomycin, linezolid, ampicillin-sulbactam). Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin) are ALSO contraindicated.

opts.text: Increase pyridostigmine to 120 mg PO every 2 hours empirically to overcome the weakness
opts.rat: Risky without first excluding cholinergic crisis. If cholinergic crisis IS the cause, more pyridostigmine worsens the weakness AND the muscarinic symptoms. Even in confirmed myasthenic crisis, dose escalation should be modest, deliberate, and after immunomodulation has begun. Also: this client is NPO with failed swallow — oral pyridostigmine cannot be safely given right now.

opts.text: BiPAP 12/5 cm H2O as definitive ventilatory support — avoid intubation
opts.rat: Wrong choice with significant bulbar weakness and failed swallow. BiPAP forces air under pressure and can drive gastric contents into the airway. With this client's failed swallow and pooling secretions, intubation (which protects the airway) is safer. BiPAP CAN be used in selected MG clients with respiratory muscle fatigue but intact bulbar function — not this one.

opts.text: Discharge teaching once stable: MG-precipitating drug list (macrolides, aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, IV magnesium, certain anesthetics), MedicAlert bracelet identifying MG, rule to contact MG neurologist BEFORE starting any new medication (including over-the-counter)
opts.rat: The single highest-leverage preventive intervention. Most MG crises are precipitated by medication, infection, or noncompliance. A written drug list carried in the wallet, an MG MedicAlert bracelet, and a clear rule about new medications prevents the urgent-care-style trigger that produced this admission.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Strict NPO; HOB 30°; oral suction at bedside; discontinue azithromycin; continuous cardiac and end-tidal CO2 monitoring
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Immediate safety measures requiring no orders or delay: protect the airway (NPO + positioning + suction), remove the active trigger (azithromycin), and detect ventilatory failure in real time (ETCO2 rises before SpO2 falls in neuromuscular weakness).

opts.text: Call neurology + MICU; prepare for anticipated intubation with rocuronium-reduced-dose RSI; have sugammadex, atropine, and standard resuscitation drugs at bedside; pre-oxygenate the client
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. Anticipated intubation in a controlled setting is far safer than crash intubation after arrest. Pre-oxygenation, experienced operator, and the right drugs (NOT succinylcholine, reduced rocuronium, sugammadex available) make the difference between a controlled airway and an aspiration disaster.

opts.text: If myasthenic vs cholinergic crisis is clinically clear (as in this client — absent SLUDGE, recent macrolide trigger, missed pyridostigmine): skip the Tensilon test; if ambiguous, perform Tensilon (2 mg test → 8 mg if no AE) with atropine pre-drawn at bedside
opts.rat: #3 — Diagnostic decision. The bedside differential is straightforward in this client. In ambiguous cases, Tensilon — short-acting AChE inhibitor — is given as 2 mg IV test dose with continuous monitoring; if no adverse reaction in 1 minute, the remaining 8 mg is pushed. Strength improves in myasthenic crisis (≈80% of patients show clear improvement) and worsens or stays the same in cholinergic crisis.

opts.text: Initiate plasmapheresis OR IVIG (neurology to choose); continue prednisone; coordinate with hospital pharmacy and dialysis/apheresis team
opts.rat: #4 — Definitive immunomodulating treatment. Plasmapheresis removes antibodies; IVIG modulates the immune response. Both produce improvement within days. Onset is fast enough that improvements may be visible during the same hospitalization. Maintenance immunosuppression (prednisone ± steroid-sparing agents) continues for long-term control.

opts.text: Once stable and extubated: bedside teaching on MG-precipitating drug list, MedicAlert bracelet, and rule to contact MG neurologist before any new medication; arrange neurology follow-up in 1–2 weeks
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management and prevention. This is what prevents the NEXT crisis. The macrolide that started this admission would not have been prescribed if the urgent care had known — or if the client had been carrying a written list and had said 'I have MG, here is my drug list.' Patient empowerment is the single most effective long-term intervention.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 3 after plasmapheresis exchange #2: NIF improved from −18 to −42 cm H2O, FVC 1.8 L, swallow screen now passes 30 mL water, no nasal regurgitation, extubated to room air with SpO2 96%
opts.rat: Classic plasmapheresis response. Antibody removal allows the remaining functional AChR receptors to support adequate neuromuscular transmission. Bulbar function returns; respiratory mechanics recover. Pyridostigmine PO restarted at maintenance dose. Continue exchanges per neurology (typically 5 total).

opts.text: Day 7: completed 5 plasmapheresis exchanges; ambulating with PT; full diet; pyridostigmine 60 mg PO QID + prednisone 20 mg PO daily resumed; MG-precipitating drug list and MedicAlert bracelet completed; discharge home with neurology follow-up in 1 week
opts.rat: Successful disposition trajectory. The teaching component is critical — discharge with written drug list, bracelet, and a clear plan to contact MG neurologist before any new prescription. Many MG centers also provide a wallet card listing dangerous medications.

opts.text: If Tensilon test had been performed: 2 mg IV produced clear improvement in ptosis and neck flexion within 60 seconds — full 8 mg dose then given with sustained 5-minute improvement
opts.rat: Strongly supports MYASTHENIC crisis. The transient improvement confirms the diagnosis and supports proceeding with immunomodulation (plasmapheresis or IVIG) plus eventual resumption of pyridostigmine at appropriate maintenance dose.

opts.text: 1 hour post-admission, before intubation: NIF drops from −18 to −10; client becomes diaphoretic, more obtunded, RR drops from 32 to 8 with shallow breaths, SpO2 75% on 4 L NC
opts.rat: EMERGENT — respiratory arrest is imminent. Immediate bag-mask ventilation, call code/RRT, urgent intubation with reduced-dose rocuronium (NOT succinylcholine). This is exactly why anticipated intubation BEFORE this point is the standard of care in MG crisis.

opts.text: Tensilon test produces dramatic worsening: 30 seconds after 2 mg IV the client develops salivation, diaphoresis, bradycardia to 42, miosis, and increased weakness — atropine 0.5 mg IV given with resolution of bradycardia and muscarinic symptoms
opts.rat: This is CHOLINERGIC crisis (the alternative diagnosis). HOLD pyridostigmine entirely; supportive care; atropine for muscarinic symptoms. The nicotinic weakness will resolve as pyridostigmine washes out over 24–48 hours. Re-evaluate pyridostigmine dosing once recovered. This case demonstrates why atropine MUST be pre-drawn at the bedside before any Tensilon test.

opts.text: Post-intubation day 2: develops new fever 102°F, increased purulent endotracheal secretions, new right lower lobe infiltrate on CXR, WBC 16 K with bandemia
opts.rat: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP). Obtain endotracheal aspirate culture; start MG-SAFE antibiotics (ceftriaxone, ampicillin-sulbactam, vancomycin, linezolid). AVOID aminoglycosides (gentamicin, tobramycin) and fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin). VAP can precipitate prolonged crisis — aggressive treatment is essential.

opts.text: Day 4 of IVIG (after total dose 1.6 g/kg given): new severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia; LP shows mildly elevated WBC with normal glucose and protein; cultures negative
opts.rat: Aseptic meningitis — a known IVIG complication, typically self-limited. Other IVIG adverse effects to monitor for: anaphylaxis (especially in IgA-deficient patients), acute kidney injury (older sucrose-stabilized products), thromboembolism (especially in older patients with vascular risk factors). Symptomatic management; resolves within days. Future IVIG infusions: slower rate, pre-medication.

opts.text: Bilateral mild ptosis persists at end of day on day 5 despite plasmapheresis, but is markedly improved from admission and present only with sustained upgaze >30 sec
opts.rat: Expected residual finding. Many MG clients have mild fluctuating ptosis at their baseline. The clinically important fact is the magnitude relative to baseline (markedly improved) and the absence of bulbar/respiratory involvement. Ptosis with sustained upgaze is the bedside fatigability test.

opts.text: Day 6: client tells you 'I think I'll skip the MedicAlert bracelet, my husband will remember to tell future doctors I have MG'
opts.rat: Knowledge deficit unresolved. Husbands are not always present (work, errands, separate ED visits). The bracelet is durable, always-on documentation that anesthesiology, EMS, and ED providers will see during an emergency. Re-teach; consider involving social work or the MG support group; emphasize the cost of THIS admission was a single urgent-care prescription that the bracelet might have prevented.

opts.text: Persistent positive AChR-Ab titer (now 14.8 nmol/L on discharge labs) consistent with her known underlying generalized MG
opts.rat: Expected. AChR-Ab levels don't track tightly with disease severity in individuals and can persist for years on treatment. The clinically relevant marker is symptoms + respiratory mechanics, not the titer. Long-term immunosuppression (prednisone + steroid-sparing agent) aims for symptomatic remission, not antibody clearance.

title: Guillain-Barré Syndrome with Autonomic Dysfunction
subtitle: Ascending Paralysis, Respiratory Failure, and the Anti-MG Mirror

labs.r: More negative than −60 cm H2O normal; intubate if less negative than −20 to −30
60–80 mL/kg normal; intubate if <15 mL/kg or <1 L (or <20 mL/kg with downward trend)

labs.n: Lumbar puncture — CSF analysis (drawn at outside hospital)
labs.v: Protein 142 mg/dL; WBC 2 cells/µL; glucose 68 mg/dL; opening pressure 14 cm H2O
labs.r: Protein 15–45 mg/dL; WBC <5/µL — ALBUMINO-CYTOLOGIC DISSOCIATION (high protein, normal cells) is classic for GBS after day 7

labs.v: Prolonged distal motor latencies, conduction block, slowed conduction velocities, absent F waves — pattern consistent with acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (AIDP)

labs.n: AChR antibody (sent to rule out MG)
Stool culture and Campylobacter jejuni serology
labs.v: Campylobacter jejuni IgM POSITIVE (recent infection); stool culture still pending

labs.n: MRI brain and cervical/lumbar spine with contrast
labs.v: No acute infarct, no cord compression; faint enhancement of cauda equina nerve roots (supports GBS)
Sinus rhythm with brief sinus pause to 3 sec during nasogastric tube placement; otherwise normal intervals; no ST changes
Diminished gag, mild dysphagia with thin liquids on speech therapy bedside screen
labs.r: Intact gag, no choking on water trial

labs.v: AREFLEXIA — biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, Achilles ALL ABSENT bilaterally

nursesNote: 0930 — 38 y/o previously healthy male transferred from outside ED for higher-level neurology and ICU care. Two and a half weeks ago he had a self-limited 'food poisoning' episode (3 days of profuse non-bloody diarrhea after a backyard chicken cookout — never sought care). One week ago developed bilateral foot tingling and 'rubbery' legs. Three days ago noted difficulty climbing stairs and rising from a chair. Two days ago could not walk and was brought to outside ED — weakness had ascended to hands and proximal arms; lumbar puncture showed protein 142 with only 2 WBC (albumino-cytologic dissociation); GBS suspected and IVIG 0.4 g/kg started yesterday (dose 1 of 5). Overnight: weakness progressed further with new bilateral facial weakness (cannot fully close eyes, weak smile), dysphagia, and softening voice. On arrival here: profound flaccid quadriparesis (1/5 lower extremities distally and proximally; 2/5 upper extremities; 3/5 neck flexion); bilateral facial diplegia; diminished gag; AREFLEXIA throughout (biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, Achilles all absent bilaterally); sensation grossly preserved with mild distal paresthesias; pupils 3 mm and reactive; cognition intact and clearly distressed; no bowel or bladder involvement yet (denies retention; voided 350 mL spontaneously 2 hr ago). Respiratory mechanics on arrival NIF −24 cm H2O, FVC 1.2 L (~17 mL/kg ideal body weight) with downward trend over 36 hr; ABG on 4 L NC shows pH 7.31 / PaCO2 52 / PaO2 68 — type II respiratory failure. During a routine left-lateral repositioning at 0925, client experienced a sudden hemodynamic swing: HR dropped from 112 to 44 bpm with a 3-second sinus pause on telemetry, BP 168/96 → 82/48, mild diaphoresis, no chest pain or loss of consciousness. Atropine 0.5 mg IV drawn at bedside but NOT yet given; HR returned to 96 within 90 seconds with reverse Trendelenburg and 250 mL fluid bolus. This is autonomic dysfunction — classic for GBS. Campylobacter jejuni IgM positive on serology, confirming the most common antecedent infection. AChR-Ab and anti-GQ1b antibodies negative (rules out MG and Miller Fisher variant). MRI shows faint cauda equina nerve root enhancement supporting demyelinating polyneuropathy. Continuing IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day (currently dose 2 of 5). Family at bedside, wife present and quietly tearful but composed. Clinical picture: ACUTE INFLAMMATORY DEMYELINATING POLYNEUROPATHY (classic GBS / AIDP) post-Campylobacter, with rapidly evolving respiratory failure AND autonomic instability — both potentially fatal complications. Anticipated intubation within hours; cardiology aware for possible pacing pads.
providerOrders: ICU admission with continuous cardiac monitoring and continuous pulse oximetry; arterial line for beat-to-beat BP monitoring during autonomic swings
Strict NPO until formal speech therapy swallow eval (bulbar weakness with diminished gag)
Bedside NIF and FVC q4h (more frequently if trending down); rapid response criteria if FVC <15 mL/kg, NIF less negative than −25, or any bulbar incompetence with poor secretion clearance

Continue IVIG 0.4 g/kg IV daily x 5 days total (currently dose 2 of 5); pre-medicate with acetaminophen 650 mg PO and diphenhydramine 25 mg IV; slow infusion rate over 6–8 hr; monitor for aseptic meningitis, AKI, anaphylaxis (especially IgA-deficient), and thromboembolism
DO NOT GIVE CORTICOSTEROIDS — clinical trials have shown NO benefit in GBS (and possible harm); this is OPPOSITE of MG treatment

Anticipated intubation: use REDUCED-DOSE rocuronium (sensitivity unpredictable in neuromuscular disease); AVOID succinylcholine (risk of severe hyperkalemia from upregulated denervation-pattern receptors after first ~5 days of weakness); sugammadex available for reversal
Atropine 0.5 mg IV pre-drawn at bedside for symptomatic bradycardia; transcutaneous pacing pads ON the client; cardiology aware

Treat autonomic hypertension with SHORT-acting agents only (e.g., labetalol 5 mg IV titrated, or nicardipine drip); AVOID long-acting antihypertensives — autonomic swings can crash the BP minutes later
DVT prophylaxis MANDATORY: enoxaparin 40 mg SubQ daily plus sequential compression devices on both legs (high VTE risk with immobility and autonomic dysfunction)
Pain management for neuropathic dysesthesias: gabapentin 300 mg PO TID titrated up; avoid opioids if possible (autonomic dysfunction + ileus risk)
Stool softener (docusate) and bowel regimen; bladder scan q6h with straight cath if retention >300 mL (autonomic bladder dysfunction common)
Eye care q2h with artificial tears and tape eyelids closed during sleep (facial diplegia → incomplete eye closure → corneal abrasion risk)
Pressure injury prevention: turn q2h, heel float pillows, specialty mattress; meticulous skin assessment with each turn
Communication board / yes-no eye-blink system established BEFORE intubation while client can still nod and speak
Social work, chaplaincy if desired by client/family; daily family meeting; physical and occupational therapy consult for early ROM and positioning

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Symmetric ASCENDING flaccid weakness over ~7 days with AREFLEXIA on exam (biceps, triceps, patellar, Achilles all absent bilaterally)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the bedside fingerprint of GBS. The combination of ascending symmetric flaccid weakness with AREFLEXIA distinguishes GBS from MG (reflexes preserved), from stroke (asymmetric, hyperreflexic over time), and from spinal cord syndromes (sensory level, bowel/bladder involvement early). Areflexia + ascending weakness = AIDP until proven otherwise.

opts.text: NIF trending from −60 → −38 → −24 cm H2O over 36 hours, FVC 1.2 L (~17 mL/kg)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the TREND is what matters, not just the absolute number. A clinically deteriorating respiratory mechanic profile in GBS means intubation should be PLANNED, not reactive. The '20-30-40 rule' (FVC <20 mL/kg, NIF less negative than −30, MEP <40) signals imminent failure. This client has crossed all three thresholds.

opts.text: ABG: pH 7.31, PaCO2 52, PaO2 68 on 4 L NC — type II (hypercapnic) respiratory failure
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. PaCO2 elevation in neuromuscular disease is a LATE sign of diaphragmatic fatigue. By the time CO2 rises, alveolar ventilation is failing. Like in MG crisis, the bedside mechanics (NIF/FVC) detect failure HOURS before the ABG; do not wait for the ABG to be 'bad enough.'

opts.text: Sudden HR 44 with 3-second sinus pause + BP swing from 168/96 to 82/48 during routine repositioning
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — AUTONOMIC DYSFUNCTION. Up to 70% of severe GBS clients develop autonomic instability: labile BP, tachycardia or bradycardia, sinus pauses, arrhythmias, ileus, urinary retention, abnormal sweating. Sudden bradycardia/asystole can occur with vagal stimulation (suctioning, NG placement, turning, defecation). Have atropine and pacing pads ready.

opts.text: Bilateral facial diplegia with diminished gag and dysphagia to thin liquids
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — bulbar involvement. Cannot fully close eyes (corneal abrasion risk), cannot protect airway (aspiration risk). Strict NPO, aspiration precautions, eye care q2h with taping during sleep. Bulbar involvement is a strong indication to intubate EARLY in GBS, often before NIF/FVC thresholds are crossed.

opts.text: Albumino-cytologic dissociation on CSF: protein 142 mg/dL with only 2 WBC/µL
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnostic. Elevated protein with normal cell count is the classic CSF pattern of GBS (typically appears after day 7 — earlier LP can be normal). Rules out bacterial meningitis (high WBC) and many other mimics. Combined with the clinical picture and NCS, confirms the diagnosis.

opts.text: Campylobacter jejuni IgM positive on serology with recent diarrheal illness 2.5 weeks ago
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — identifies the antecedent trigger. Campylobacter is the most common preceding infection in GBS (~30% of cases) and is associated with the axonal AMAN variant and worse prognosis. Other triggers: CMV, EBV, Mycoplasma, HIV, hepatitis E, Zika, COVID-19, and rarely vaccinations. Identifying the trigger does not change acute treatment but informs prognosis and counseling.

opts.text: Nerve conduction studies: prolonged distal motor latencies, conduction block, slowed velocities, absent F waves — consistent with AIDP (demyelinating)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms demyelinating subtype (AIDP) vs axonal (AMAN/AMSAN) vs Miller Fisher (ataxia, areflexia, ophthalmoplegia). AIDP is the most common Western subtype, generally with better recovery prognosis than axonal variants. Treatment (plasmapheresis or IVIG) is the same across subtypes.

opts.text: Negative AChR-Ab and negative anti-GQ1b antibodies
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pertinent negatives that REFINE the differential. Negative AChR-Ab makes MG unlikely (no MG, no concurrent disease). Negative anti-GQ1b rules out Miller Fisher variant (which presents with the classic triad of ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and areflexia). Documenting these clinches the AIDP diagnosis.

opts.text: Severe immobility with rapidly progressive paralysis (1/5 in legs, 2/5 in arms) and autonomic dysfunction
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — HIGH VTE risk. Immobility plus autonomic dysfunction (which can cause venous pooling and stasis) plus inflammatory state plus possible mechanical ventilation later equals one of the highest-risk VTE profiles in medicine. Pharmacologic AND mechanical prophylaxis are mandatory unless contraindicated.

opts.text: Cognition intact; client is alert, oriented, clearly distressed and able to communicate concern about 'what is happening to me'
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — psychosocial and ethical. GBS preserves cognition while progressively paralyzing the body — an experience patients describe as terrifying. Establish a communication system (eye blinks, communication board, letter board) BEFORE intubation while the client can still nod. Frequent reassurance, presence, explanation, and family involvement are not optional in GBS — they are clinical care.

opts.text: Mild distal paresthesias ('tingling') in feet and fingers
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue in isolation. Sensory symptoms are common but generally mild in classic AIDP — motor involvement dominates. Treat associated neuropathic pain with gabapentin, document distribution, but no immediate intervention needed beyond comfort measures.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.
