opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Hyponatremia worsens cerebral edema in an already inflamed brain. Volume status assessment will determine SIADH vs CSW — this patient is currently euvolemic-trending-toward-overload after the initial resuscitation, picture more consistent with SIADH. Fluid restriction once initial resuscitation is complete; monitor Na q6h.

opts.text: Risk for Sensorineural Hearing Loss r/t pneumococcal meningitis (up to 30% incidence) — dexamethasone reduces this complication; audiology follow-up before discharge
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in acute ranking but a critical LONG-TERM consideration. Hearing loss is a major morbidity that's preventable in part with dexamethasone (a core reason for the steroid). All survivors need audiology testing before discharge. This is nursing leadership in discharge planning that's easy to forget when focused on acute crises.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Dexamethasone 10 mg IV push NOW, 15 minutes BEFORE the first antibiotic dose; then 10 mg IV q6h x 4 days (discontinue if non-pneumococcal organism confirmed)
opts.rat: Core intervention for adult pneumococcal meningitis. Reduces mortality and hearing loss. The TIMING (before antibiotics) is the high-yield element — students often miss this. Discontinue if the organism turns out to be non-pneumococcal (benefit not established).

opts.text: Empiric antibiotic trio within 1 hour: ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h + vancomycin 25 mg/kg IV load then 15–20 mg/kg q8–12h + ampicillin 2 g IV q4h (age >50, covers Listeria)
opts.rat: Standard empiric coverage for adult community-acquired bacterial meningitis age >50 or immunocompromised. Each agent fills a gap (ceftriaxone for most strep/Neisseria/H. flu, vancomycin for cephalosporin-resistant pneumococcus, ampicillin for Listeria). Narrow once cultures return.

opts.text: Droplet precautions (surgical mask within 6 feet, single room) until N. meningitidis is ruled OUT or 24 hr after effective antibiotic if confirmed
opts.rat: Standard infection control for suspected meningococcal disease. Continue until ruled out (multiplex PCR usually returns within hours). Even if pneumococcal is confirmed, droplet precautions for routine respiratory secretion management until the patient is afebrile and stable is reasonable institutional practice.

opts.text: Sepsis bundle: 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid (LR or NS) within 3 hr; repeat lactate q2h; norepinephrine drip 0.05 mcg/kg/min titrated to MAP ≥65 if hypotension persists after fluids
opts.rat: Concurrent sepsis management. The meningitis pathway and the sepsis pathway are not sequential — they run in parallel. Cultures BEFORE first antibiotic dose; antibiotics within 1 hr; aggressive fluid resuscitation; vasopressor support to maintain MAP ≥65.

opts.text: HOB 30°, minimize stimulation, group cares, q1h neuro checks with GCS + pupil assessment
opts.rat: ICP optimization in the inflamed brain. Head elevation promotes cerebral venous drainage; minimizing stimulation reduces ICP spikes; q1h neuro checks detect early deterioration before pupillary changes or posturing occur.

opts.text: Seizure precautions; have IV lorazepam 4 mg available; load levetiracetam 1,000 mg IV BID if seizures occur
opts.rat: Seizure risk is elevated in bacterial meningitis (cortical irritation from inflammation, fever, electrolyte derangements). Routine prophylaxis is NOT given without seizures; reactive treatment if they occur. Seizures worsen ICP and cerebral metabolic demand — treat aggressively.

opts.text: Identify close contacts in past 7 days and arrange chemoprophylaxis (rifampin 600 mg PO BID x 2 days OR ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO single dose OR ceftriaxone 250 mg IM single dose) ONLY IF N. meningitidis or H. influenzae type b is confirmed (not for pneumococcal)
opts.rat: Public health imperative — contingent on organism. Pneumococcus is NOT transmitted at significant rates and contacts do NOT need prophylaxis. Meningococcal and Hib contacts DO need prophylaxis. Identify the contacts NOW so the call list is ready when the organism is confirmed.

opts.text: Audiology consult and formal hearing testing arranged before discharge
opts.rat: Sensorineural hearing loss is a major long-term complication of pneumococcal meningitis (up to 30%). Easy to miss in a recovering patient focused on bigger problems. Arrange audiology BEFORE discharge — and document the plan. Dexamethasone helps reduce but doesn't eliminate this complication.

opts.text: Delay antibiotics until after the LP to preserve culture yield
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS — and a classic NCLEX trap. Antibiotics within 1 hour of suspected bacterial meningitis. Each hour of delay increases mortality. CSF Gram stain remains useful even after first antibiotic; multiplex PCR is unaffected. If imaging is needed before LP, START ANTIBIOTICS FIRST, then image, then LP.

opts.text: Vancomycin alone — skip ceftriaxone and ampicillin since gram-positive diplococci were seen
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT. Gram stain is presumptive; full empiric coverage continues until cultures and sensitivities return. Ceftriaxone is the workhorse for pneumococcal CNS infection; vancomycin is added to cover resistant pneumococcus. Ampicillin is for Listeria (a gram-positive ROD, not diplococcus — Gram stain might miss it). Narrow ONLY after culture and sensitivity.

opts.text: Liberal IV fluids at 250 mL/hr maintenance to prevent dehydration
opts.rat: Inappropriate maintenance rate. The patient has had initial resuscitation and is becoming euvolemic; bacterial meningitis predisposes to SIADH (free water retention). High maintenance rates would worsen dilutional hyponatremia and cerebral edema. Use minimum maintenance with goal-directed sodium and volume status monitoring.

opts.text: Bolus hypertonic 3% saline 250 mL IV for the hyponatremia of Na 131
opts.rat: Not at this Na and clinical status. The patient is not severely hyponatremic (Na 131, asymptomatic from this) and is in active sepsis with ongoing IVF resuscitation. Treat the meningitis/sepsis; mild fluid restriction and serial sodium monitoring is the early SIADH approach. Hypertonic saline is reserved for severe symptomatic hyponatremia or established ICP crisis.

opts.text: Family teaching on pneumococcal vaccine (PCV20 or PPSV23 series) once survivor stabilizes — major recurrence prevention
opts.rat: Discharge education essential. Pneumococcal vaccines (PCV20 single dose or sequential PCV15→PPSV23) are recommended for adults ≥50 (or younger with risk factors like diabetes). Many invasive pneumococcal disease cases occur in unvaccinated or under-vaccinated adults. Survivor and household contacts review vaccine status as part of discharge teaching.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Place droplet precautions (single room, mask within 6 feet); draw blood cultures x2 BEFORE antibiotics; obtain large-bore IVs
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Infection control and access. Droplet precautions go on the moment meningitis is suspected (before any organism is known). Blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics — once antibiotics are in, yield drops. Two large-bore IVs allow simultaneous antibiotic, vasopressor, and fluid delivery.

opts.text: Administer DEXAMETHASONE 10 mg IV push NOW (15 minutes before antibiotics); start sepsis bundle with 30 mL/kg crystalloid
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. Dexamethasone first (before antibiotic-induced bacterial lysis triggers the inflammatory cascade). Fluid resuscitation begins simultaneously — these are not mutually exclusive; the steroid is a single push and fluids run continuously.

opts.text: Administer empiric antibiotic trio within 1 hr: ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h + vancomycin 25 mg/kg IV load + ampicillin 2 g IV q4h (covers Listeria given age >50)
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical. Antibiotics within 1 hour of suspected bacterial meningitis is a clear quality metric tied to mortality. The triple combination addresses the most likely organisms in adults >50. Each hour of delay increases mortality.

opts.text: Initiate continuous cardiac monitoring, arterial line if vasopressor-dependent; q1h neuro checks; HOB 30°, minimize stimulation; norepinephrine drip ready if MAP <65 after fluid resuscitation
opts.rat: #4 — Sustained safety. Hemodynamic surveillance and ICP-optimization measures run continuously. Norepinephrine ready (drawn up, in line) for MAP <65 after the planned 30 mL/kg crystalloid. Neuro checks q1h to catch early deterioration before pupil changes or posturing.

opts.text: Family meeting; identify close contacts in past 7 days for potential chemoprophylaxis; coordinate with infection control / public health; arrange audiology follow-up before discharge
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management. Once the acute pathway is in motion, public health coordination (especially if N. meningitidis is on the differential), contact identification, and survivor follow-up planning are the next layer of nursing leadership. Audiology referral on day 1, not at discharge planning.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Hour 6 after dexamethasone + ceftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin + 2 L crystalloid: GCS 12 → 14, MAP 78 off vasopressors, temperature 100.2°F, lactate 1.8, repeat blood pressure stable
opts.rat: Excellent early response to source control and resuscitation. Continue antibiotic regimen and dexamethasone, narrow once cultures and sensitivities return (typically 48–72 hr), continue q1h neuro checks until stable. Confirms the diagnostic pathway and treatment choice.

opts.text: Day 3: CSF cultures grew Streptococcus pneumoniae sensitive to ceftriaxone; vancomycin and ampicillin discontinued; continuing ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h x 14-day total course; GCS 15, neck stiffness resolving, audiology scheduled for hospital day 10
opts.rat: Successful narrowing trajectory. Once organism and sensitivities are confirmed, narrow to the most targeted agent. 10–14 day course typical for pneumococcal meningitis. Audiology before discharge is the nursing-driven discharge planning element.

opts.text: Day 14: completed 14-day ceftriaxone course; discharged home with audiology hearing test 'mild high-frequency hearing loss on left,' outpatient audiology follow-up arranged; pneumococcal vaccine PCV20 administered before discharge; counseled wife about household pneumococcal vaccine review
opts.rat: Successful disposition with critical loose ends tied. Hearing loss is the expected residual; outpatient audiology and possible hearing aid evaluation. PCV20 prevents recurrence (survivors of invasive pneumococcal disease are at elevated risk for second episode). Household teaching on vaccine completes the prevention story.

opts.text: Hour 4: GCS drops from 12 → 8, right pupil now 7 mm sluggish, BP 218/118, bradycardia 52 (Cushing's triad)
opts.rat: TRANSTENTORIAL HERNIATION — neurosurgical emergency. Immediate intubation, hyperventilate to PaCO2 30–35 (TEMPORARY measure), hypertonic saline 3% 250 mL IV bolus or mannitol 1 g/kg IV, head of bed 30° neutral, neurosurgery STAT for possible decompression or EVD. Don't lose the moment — minutes matter.

opts.text: Hour 12: new generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 3 minutes; postictal GCS 10; repeat seizure 20 minutes later
opts.rat: Status epilepticus risk. Treat acutely with lorazepam 4 mg IV; load levetiracetam 1,000 mg IV; continuous EEG; rule out hyponatremia (Na 131 → recheck), hypoglycemia, contributing electrolyte derangements. Bacterial meningitis with seizures has higher mortality and morbidity (case-026 covers status epilepticus management in detail).

opts.text: Day 2: Na has dropped from 131 to 124 with euvolemia (CVP 8, balanced I&O, no weight loss, no UOP elevation); urine Na 95, urine osmolality 580
opts.rat: SIADH — confirmed by the picture (euvolemic hyponatremia, concentrated urine, elevated urine Na). Treatment is FLUID RESTRICTION to <800 mL/day, slow Na correction. This is OPPOSITE of SAH-CSW from case-041 (where hypovolemia → salt + volume). Same Na, opposite mechanism, opposite treatment — volume status is the discriminator.

opts.text: Hour 8: new large purpuric lesions developing on extremities; platelets 78 (down from 142); fibrinogen 95; bleeding from line sites; INR 2.1
opts.rat: Fulminant DIC (and possibly purpura fulminans). Replace fibrinogen (cryoprecipitate 10 units if <100 mg/dL), platelets if <50 K and bleeding, FFP for INR >1.5–2.0 with bleeding. Source control (antibiotics) is the definitive treatment — keep them going. Most-severe DIC carries grim prognosis but is reversible with aggressive sepsis control.

opts.text: Day 5: persistent fever 101.4°F, new headache worse on the left, focal weakness right arm, repeat CT shows new ring-enhancing lesion in left frontal lobe
opts.rat: Brain abscess — a known complication of bacterial meningitis (about 5–10% in pneumococcal). Neurosurgical aspiration or excision plus targeted antibiotics; may need anaerobic coverage (metronidazole) added; longer duration of therapy. MRI with contrast for better characterization. Neurology and neurosurgery joint care.

opts.text: Day 21 follow-up: completed antibiotic course, audiology shows mild high-frequency hearing loss left ear, mild fatigue and 'brain fog' persisting; no focal deficits; gradually returning to work part-time
opts.rat: Expected residual profile. Mild cognitive symptoms, fatigue, and hearing loss can persist for weeks to months after bacterial meningitis. Outpatient audiology, gradual return to work, cognitive rehab if persistent issues, and vaccine completion are nursing-led discharge themes.

opts.text: Public health notification completed; wife and adult son being followed by health department; cultures confirmed S. pneumoniae (not meningococcal); contact chemoprophylaxis NOT required
opts.rat: Expected outcome with pneumococcal vs meningococcal disease. Pneumococcus does not require contact chemoprophylaxis. Health department notification is still appropriate for severe invasive disease (mandatory state reporting in CA). Wife and son monitored for any symptoms but not given antibiotics.

title: Spontaneous Intracerebral Hemorrhage on Apixaban
subtitle: Anticoagulant Reversal, SBP-140 Target, and the Cerebrovascular BP Pivot

patient.name: M. Patel
patient.code: Full Code (advance directive being reviewed with family)
patient.admitDate: Today, 0930 (ED arrival via EMS — symptom onset 0900)

vitals.time: Cardiology clinic — 1 month ago (baseline AFib follow-up)
1015 (CURRENT — after nicardipine drip and partial andexanet alfa bolus)

labs.v: 35 mL hyperdense hematoma in LEFT basal ganglia/thalamus with mild surrounding edema; small intraventricular extension into the posterior horn of the left lateral ventricle; no significant midline shift (<4 mm); no acute hydrocephalus on this study
2 (GCS 13–15 = 0, age <80 = 0, supratentorial = 0, volume 30–60 mL = 1, IVH present = 1) → predicted ~26% 30-day mortality

No underlying AVM, aneurysm, or vascular malformation. NO 'spot sign' on CTA (negative spot sign predicts LOW risk of hematoma expansion). Patent intracranial arteries; bilateral carotids without significant stenosis.
labs.r: Normal vessels without bleeding source or spot sign

labs.v: 220 ng/mL (therapeutic range ~50–250 ng/mL trough/peak — confirms ACTIVE anticoagulation)
labs.r: <30 ng/mL = clinically insignificant; this level indicates full therapeutic effect
INR <1.1; platelets 150–400; standard coags do NOT reliably reflect DOAC activity — drug-specific assays are needed

labs.v: 0700 today (2.5 hr before symptom onset; 5 mg dose)
labs.r: Critical to determine dosing of reversal agent (andexanet alfa high-dose if last apixaban ≥5 mg within 8 hr; otherwise low-dose)

labs.v: GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6); RIGHT face droop with forehead spared (UMN pattern); right arm 1/5; right leg 2/5; right pronator drift; expressive aphasia; gaze preference to LEFT; pupils 3 mm bilateral reactive; NIHSS 16
labs.r: GCS 15; NIHSS 0; symmetric strength and intact speech

labs.v: GCS 12 (E3 V3 M6) — VERBAL component declined; right arm 1/5 unchanged; right leg 1/5 (worse than admission); pupils 3 mm bilateral reactive; NIHSS 18 (climbing 2 points in 45 min)
Atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular response ~90; no acute ST changes; no QT prolongation
Wet voice quality, audible gurgling with 5 mL water trial — FAILED swallow screen
labs.r: Passes water trial without choking or wet voice

labs.v: 4 (HTN 1, age 75+ 2, T2DM 1) → annual stroke risk ~4% off anticoagulation — strong indication for chronic OAC, complicating the post-ICH restart decision
labs.r: Used to assess stroke risk in AFib

labs.v: A positive; antibody screen negative; 2 units PRBC and FFP on standby
3 (HTN 1, age >65 1, antiplatelet/NSAID 0, prior bleed 1 [NOW]) → meaningful bleeding risk on resumed anticoagulation

nursesNote: 1015 — 76 y/o male with chronic non-valvular atrial fibrillation (CHA2DS2-VASc 4) on apixaban 5 mg PO BID for stroke prophylaxis, HTN on amlodipine 10 mg daily, and T2DM on metformin 1,000 mg PO BID. Brought to ED by EMS this morning after sudden onset of right-sided weakness, dysarthria, and severe left-sided headache while playing chess with his grandson at home at 0900. Last known well at 0859. His daughter (a nurse) was present and immediately called 911. He took his usual 0700 apixaban dose this morning (5 mg) approximately 2 hr before symptom onset — confirms ACTIVE anticoagulation at time of bleed. EMS noted BP 218/118; brought directly to our comprehensive stroke center. On arrival at 0930 he was awake but lethargic (GCS 13: E3 V4 M6), markedly dysarthric with expressive aphasia, gaze preference to the left, right face droop with forehead spared (UMN pattern), right arm 1/5, right leg 2/5, NIHSS 16. STAT CT at 0950 revealed a 35 mL hyperdense hematoma in the LEFT basal ganglia/thalamus with mild surrounding edema and a small intraventricular extension into the posterior horn of the left lateral ventricle; no significant midline shift. ICH Score = 2 (volume 1, IVH 1) → predicted ~26% 30-day mortality. CTA at 1010 shows NO underlying AVM/aneurysm/vascular malformation and importantly NO 'spot sign' (negative spot sign on CTA predicts LOW risk of further hematoma expansion). Apixaban anti-Xa level = 220 ng/mL confirming full therapeutic effect. Nicardipine drip started immediately for SBP target 140 mmHg (per INTERACT-3 trial); andexanet alfa HIGH-DOSE regimen (800 mg IV bolus over ~30 min then 8 mg/min infusion x 120 min) initiated for reversal (last apixaban ≥5 mg within 8 hr → high-dose protocol). Mannitol and hypertonic 3% saline available at bedside; HOB 30°; strict bed rest; failed bedside swallow — NPO; q15min neuro checks. Repeat exam at 1015 shows EARLY DECLINE (GCS 13 → 12, NIHSS 16 → 18, right leg 2/5 → 1/5) — concerning for early hematoma expansion or worsening edema. Stat repeat CT in 30 min; neurosurgery at bedside reviewing for possible minimally invasive evacuation (ENRICH-eligible lobar/deep clot >20 mL). Patient's wife and daughter at bedside; daughter (nurse) understands the gravity; goals-of-care conversation initiated. Anticipated MICU/Neuro ICU admission, likely intubation if GCS continues to decline. Family also being prepared for the complex post-ICH question that will follow stabilization: WHEN and WHETHER to restart anticoagulation given AFib stroke risk vs hemorrhage recurrence risk.
providerOrders: STAT andexanet alfa HIGH-DOSE regimen: 800 mg IV bolus at 30 mg/min, then 8 mg/min IV infusion x 120 min (continuous) — for apixaban ≥5 mg within 8 hr
NICARDIPINE drip 5 mg/hr IV, titrate up by 2.5 mg/hr every 5–15 min (max 15 mg/hr) — TARGET SBP 140 mmHg (per INTERACT-3 trial); add labetalol 10 mg IV bolus PRN for breakthrough HTN
AVOID nitroprusside (potential cerebral vasodilation may raise ICP; cyanide toxicity with prolonged use)
AVOID hypotension below MAP 80 (maintain CPP); use cautious nicardipine titration in elderly
Strict bed rest with HOB 30°; neck in neutral alignment to optimize cerebral venous drainage
Strict NPO until formal speech therapy swallow eval (failed bedside screen)
Q15min neuro checks x 2 hr, then q1h x 24 hr; NIHSS and GCS documented; immediate provider notification for ANY decline (GCS drop ≥1 point, new deficit, pupil change, posturing)
Stat REPEAT non-contrast CT in 30 min to assess for hematoma expansion (and again at 6 hr and 24 hr if stable)
Continuous cardiac monitoring (chronic AFib); continuous pulse oximetry; arterial line for beat-to-beat BP monitoring
Two large-bore IVs; type and screen on standby; PRBC and FFP available
DO NOT GIVE tPA or any thrombolytic — ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in ICH
NO ROUTINE seizure prophylaxis (no benefit shown in ICH per AHA/ASA guidelines); reactive levetiracetam 1,000 mg IV BID load only IF seizures occur
Hypertonic 3% saline 250 mL IV bolus available at bedside for ICP crisis or worsening edema
DVT prophylaxis: bilateral sequential compression devices NOW; HOLD pharmacologic anticoagulation for now and reassess at 24–48 hr after stable hematoma confirmed on serial CTs
Hold metformin (acute illness + risk of lactic acidosis); start insulin sliding scale per protocol with goal 140–180 mg/dL
Acetaminophen 1 g IV/PO q6h scheduled for headache and any fever (fever worsens outcomes in ICH)

Neurosurgery consultation for review of possible minimally invasive surgical evacuation (per ENRICH trial — select lobar/deep ICH >20 mL within 24 hr); cerebellar ICH > 3 cm or with brainstem compression → urgent suboccipital craniectomy (not applicable here, basal ganglia bleed)

Pharmacy reconciliation: confirm exact time of last apixaban dose for reversal protocol; confirm no concurrent antiplatelet (aspirin/clopidogrel) — if present, separate management considerations apply

Goals-of-care conversation with patient (while able to participate) and family — ICH Score 2 means meaningful mortality risk but also meaningful survivor population; do not give a prognostic determination at hour 1; revisit after 24–72 hr trajectory

After stabilization: cardiology consultation for post-ICH AFib management — restart anticoagulation in 4–8 weeks vs left atrial appendage closure (Watchman device) vs accept untreated stroke risk; multidisciplinary decision

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: CT showing 35 mL hyperdense hematoma in LEFT basal ganglia/thalamus with intraventricular extension into the lateral ventricle
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the diagnosis. Hyperdense (white) on non-contrast CT = fresh blood. Basal ganglia location is the classic hypertensive ICH site (lenticulostriate artery rupture). The IVH presence increases the ICH Score and the mortality risk (and the risk of obstructive hydrocephalus from clot in the ventricular system).

opts.text: BP 218/118 on arrival, 162/94 currently (still above SBP 140 target)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — high BP drives hematoma expansion. Aggressive control to SBP 140 (per INTERACT-3 trial) reduces hematoma growth and improves outcomes. This is OPPOSITE of ischemic stroke (where permissive HTN <185 pre-tPA is the rule) and OPPOSITE of SAH-vasospasm (where INDUCED HTN 180–220 is the rule).

opts.text: Patient is on apixaban 5 mg PO BID with last dose at 0700 (2.5 hr before onset) and anti-Xa level 220 ng/mL confirming therapeutic anticoagulation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — REVERSAL IS URGENT. Anticoagulant-associated ICH has higher mortality and larger initial hematoma volumes. Andexanet alfa is the FDA-approved factor Xa inhibitor reversal agent (binds and sequesters apixaban/rivaroxaban). The TIMING of the last dose determines dose (high-dose if last dose ≥5 mg within 8 hr). 4-factor PCC (50 units/kg) is an off-label alternative when andexanet not available.

opts.text: Early neurologic decline: GCS 13 → 12, NIHSS 16 → 18, right leg 2/5 → 1/5 over 45 min
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — early hematoma expansion vs edema vs IVH evolving. Up to 30% of ICHs expand in the first 6 hr. Stat repeat CT in 30 min; neurosurgery at bedside. Aggressive BP control + complete reversal of anticoagulation are the primary medical interventions. Surgical evacuation considered if eligible (ENRICH-style minimally invasive for select cases).

opts.text: Negative 'spot sign' on CTA
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — favorable prognostic sign. The CTA spot sign is contrast extravasation within the hematoma indicating active bleeding and predicting expansion. ABSENT spot sign suggests lower expansion risk. But it is NOT a guarantee — serial CT monitoring continues regardless.

opts.text: ICH Score = 2 (volume 30–60 mL = 1 point, IVH present = 1 point) → predicted ~26% 30-day mortality
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — useful for prognosis and family discussion. The ICH Score uses GCS, age, location, volume, and IVH; higher score = worse prognosis. BUT it is a population-level statistic; individual outcomes vary. Don't make goals-of-care decisions at hour 1 based on a score alone — re-evaluate at 24–72 hr trajectory.

opts.text: Failed bedside swallow screen with wet voice and gurgling on 5 mL water trial
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — aspiration risk. Strict NPO. Aspiration pneumonia is a common complication of stroke (ischemic AND hemorrhagic) and significantly worsens outcomes. Speech therapy formal evaluation when stable; modified diet or NG/OG feeding for nutrition support if oral intake delayed.

opts.text: CHA2DS2-VASc 4 with chronic AFib — significant baseline stroke risk that drove the anticoagulation in the first place
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — sets up the post-stabilization dilemma. The same patient has a 4%/year ischemic stroke risk OFF anticoagulation. After surviving this ICH, the question of restart timing (typically 4–8 weeks if no contraindication) or LAA closure (Watchman device) requires multidisciplinary input. Not an acute decision but worth anticipating.

opts.text: GCS 12 (declined from 13) with right-sided weakness, expressive aphasia, gaze preference left, right face droop with forehead spared
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — focal deficit pattern. Left hemisphere lesion → right hemiparesis, right face droop (UMN — forehead spared), expressive aphasia (if dominant hemisphere — most right-handed patients), gaze preference toward the lesion side (left). The forehead-sparing distinguishes UMN (central) facial weakness from peripheral (Bell's palsy).

opts.text: INR 1.3 with normal aPTT 38
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — DOAC TRAP. Standard coagulation tests (INR, PTT) DO NOT reliably reflect DOAC activity. INR 1.3 might falsely suggest mild anticoagulation when the apixaban anti-Xa level confirms full therapeutic effect (220 ng/mL). High-yield NCLEX/board exam concept: do NOT use INR to assess DOAC reversal need.

opts.text: Glucose 168 mg/dL
opts.rat: Mildly elevated stress hyperglycemia; not the priority. Hold metformin (risk of lactic acidosis with acute illness), start insulin sliding scale with goal 140–180 mg/dL. Aggressive glucose control is NOT beneficial in acute stroke and can cause harmful hypoglycemia.

opts.text: Pupils 3 mm bilateral and briskly reactive
opts.rat: Reassuring — no current herniation, no oculomotor compression. Document and recheck q15min initially. Any change (anisocoria, sluggish, fixed) is a herniation warning sign and requires immediate ICP management.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Chronic hypertension causes degenerative changes in small penetrating arteries (lipohyalinosis, Charcot-Bouchard microaneurysms) — the lenticulostriate branches of the MCA supplying the basal ganglia are particularly vulnerable, producing the classic deep ICH location
opts.rat: CORRECT — the mechanism of classic hypertensive ICH. Common sites in descending frequency: basal ganglia/thalamus, lobar, cerebellar, pontine. Lobar location (especially in older non-hypertensive patients) raises concern for cerebral amyloid angiopathy. The location informs the differential cause (HTN vs amyloid vs vascular malformation vs tumor).

opts.text: Acute BP control to SBP 140 mmHg reduces hematoma expansion AND improves outcomes (per INTERACT-3 trial); preferred agents are short-acting IV titratables: nicardipine (preferred), labetalol (alternative). AVOID nitroprusside (cerebral vasodilator + cyanide accumulation).
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield trial-based knowledge. INTERACT-3 (and earlier INTERACT-2) showed safety and benefit of SBP target 140. INTENSIVE-BP confirmed durability. The bundle of early intensive BP control + reversal + neurosurgical consultation forms the core ICH care bundle. Note: do NOT drop BP below MAP 80 — adequate CPP must be maintained.

opts.text: Anticoagulant reversal is matched to the drug: WARFARIN → 4F-PCC (Kcentra) + Vitamin K 10 mg IV; DABIGATRAN (direct thrombin inhibitor) → idarucizumab 5 g IV; APIXABAN/RIVAROXABAN (factor Xa inhibitors) → andexanet alfa OR 4F-PCC; HEPARIN → protamine sulfate (1 mg per 100 units in last 4 hr); ANTIPLATELETS → DDAVP +/- platelet transfusion (PATCH trial showed possible harm from routine platelet transfusion)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the reversal-by-class framework. Each agent has a specific reversal strategy. ICH on any anticoagulant is a TIME-CRITICAL drug-reversal scenario. Andexanet alfa was specifically developed for the factor Xa inhibitor class; idarucizumab specifically binds dabigatran. 4F-PCC is the workhorse for warfarin and an off-label option for Xa inhibitor reversal when andexanet is unavailable.

opts.text: Standard coagulation tests (INR, PTT) DO NOT reliably reflect DOAC activity — drug-specific assays (anti-Xa for apixaban/rivaroxaban, dilute thrombin time or ecarin clotting time for dabigatran) are needed; INR 'looks normal' on a DOAC does NOT mean no anticoagulation effect
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield NCLEX/board exam point. Many providers default to INR for 'anticoagulation status' but DOACs may not prolong it. The decision to reverse is based on: (1) the drug name, (2) the time of last dose, (3) the clinical urgency. The anti-Xa level here confirms therapeutic effect and supports the high-dose andexanet regimen.

opts.text: Up to 30% of ICHs expand in the first 6 hours — early aggressive BP control + complete anticoagulation reversal are the two interventions proven to reduce expansion; CTA 'spot sign' (active contrast extravasation in hematoma) predicts higher expansion risk
opts.rat: CORRECT. Expansion is the most modifiable predictor of poor outcome. Time-critical bundle: SBP 140 within first hour; reversal initiated within first hour; serial CTs to detect expansion (typically at admission, 30 min after intervention, 6 hr, and 24 hr if stable). The spot sign is on CTA; on routine CT, you cannot predict expansion.

opts.text: ICH is an ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION to tPA or any thrombolytic — even a 'small' ICH precludes thrombolysis indefinitely
opts.rat: CORRECT — opposite physiology of ischemic stroke. The 'last known well' question and the CT distinction (hypodense early infarct vs hyperdense blood) are why imaging is ABSOLUTELY required before tPA. A patient with a stroke-like presentation has ICH excluded by CT before tPA is given. History of prior ICH is itself a tPA contraindication.

opts.text: Routine seizure prophylaxis IS indicated for all ICH and should be started immediately (levetiracetam 1,000 mg IV BID)
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — common but wrong. Routine seizure prophylaxis in ICH is NOT recommended (AHA/ASA guidelines). Studies have not shown benefit and some show worse outcomes with phenytoin. Treat seizures REACTIVELY if they occur (levetiracetam is preferred — fewer drug interactions, no level monitoring). This is OPPOSITE of SAH (where short-term prophylaxis is sometimes given) and TBI (where 7-day prophylaxis has evidence).

opts.text: Nitroprusside is the preferred IV antihypertensive for ICH because of its rapid onset and offset
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT and dangerous. Nitroprusside CAN raise ICP (cerebral vasodilator) and carries cyanide toxicity risk with prolonged use. Preferred agents are nicardipine (clean titratability, no ICP effect) and labetalol. Nitroprusside is reserved for refractory severe HTN under careful monitoring.

opts.text: Surgical evacuation is indicated for ALL supratentorial ICHs >20 mL within 24 hours
opts.rat: INCORRECT — overstated. The STICH I and II trials showed NO routine benefit for open surgical evacuation of supratentorial ICH. The 2024 ENRICH trial suggested benefit for MINIMALLY INVASIVE evacuation of select lobar/deep clots >20 mL — but routine evacuation of all ICHs is not the standard. Cerebellar ICH > 3 cm with brainstem compression IS an urgent surgical indication.

opts.text: Restarting anticoagulation after ICH is controversial; the general approach is to wait 4–8 weeks then resume in patients with compelling indication (mechanical valve, high-risk AFib), OR consider left atrial appendage closure (Watchman) for those who cannot resume
opts.rat: CORRECT — the post-ICH planning step. Pivotal observational data and ongoing trials support resumption at 4–8 weeks for many high-stroke-risk patients (the ischemic risk often exceeds the rebleeding risk after the acute period). LAA closure is an alternative for those who cannot tolerate any oral anticoagulant. This requires multidisciplinary input (neurology + cardiology + neurosurgery).

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Hematoma Expansion / Cerebral Herniation r/t active anticoagulant effect, severe HTN, and early neurologic decline (GCS 13→12)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The brain is actively bleeding (or could re-expand) until BP is controlled AND anticoagulation is reversed. The early GCS decline is the warning sign. Both interventions (SBP 140 via nicardipine drip; andexanet alfa high-dose) within the first hour are the highest-yield interventions to prevent expansion.

opts.text: Risk for Aspiration r/t failed swallow screen with wet voice and gurgling
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Aspiration pneumonia in a stroke patient significantly worsens outcomes. Strict NPO, HOB 30°, oral suction at bedside. Formal speech therapy eval when stable. Consider NG/OG tube placement for nutrition if oral intake will be delayed beyond 24–48 hr.

opts.text: Risk for Increased ICP / Obstructive Hydrocephalus r/t intraventricular extension and cerebral edema
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. The IVH can obstruct CSF flow causing hydrocephalus; surrounding edema can develop over hours to days. HOB 30°, neck neutral, minimize stimulation, hypertonic saline 3% available; q15min initial neuro checks; serial CTs. EVD consideration if obstructive hydrocephalus develops.

opts.text: Risk for Hemorrhagic Conversion / Continued Bleeding r/t residual anticoagulant effect after reversal initiated but not complete
opts.rat: #4 — Real but actively being addressed by reversal protocol. Andexanet alfa effect is rapid (within 30 min of bolus) and sustained through the infusion (~2 hr). 4F-PCC is alternative or adjunct. Watch for ongoing bleeding evidence (line site, GU, GI, mucosal). NOT a reason to delay or under-dose the reversal agent.

opts.text: Anxiety / Ineffective Coping r/t acute critical illness, communication impairment (expressive aphasia), and uncertainty
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in the acute ranking but high-impact nursing presence. The patient can hear and understand but cannot easily express himself. Use simple yes/no questions, communication board, family at bedside, brief honest updates. Reduces the cognitive isolation that worsens outcomes and family distress.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: ANDEXANET ALFA HIGH-DOSE: 800 mg IV bolus at 30 mg/min, then 8 mg/min infusion x 120 min (last apixaban ≥5 mg within 8 hr)
opts.rat: FDA-approved factor Xa inhibitor reversal — the most specific intervention for apixaban-associated ICH. Dosing is based on the dose and timing of the last anticoagulant dose. High-dose regimen here is correct given the 5 mg dose taken within 8 hr.

opts.text: NICARDIPINE drip 5 mg/hr titrated to TARGET SBP 140 mmHg; labetalol IV 10 mg bolus PRN for breakthrough
opts.rat: Per INTERACT-3 trial — intensive BP control to SBP 140 within the first hour reduces hematoma expansion and improves outcomes. Nicardipine and labetalol are the preferred agents (clean titration, no ICP elevation, no cyanide concerns). Avoid hypotension below MAP 80.

opts.text: Strict NPO; HOB 30°; bilateral SCDs; q15min neuro checks for first 2 hr then q1h x 24 hr
opts.rat: Bundle of bedside safety measures. NPO until swallow eval; HOB optimizes venous drainage; SCDs for DVT prevention while pharmacologic anticoagulation is held; frequent neuro checks catch early decline. None require additional orders — bedside nursing decisions.

opts.text: Stat REPEAT non-contrast CT in 30 min, then at 6 hr and 24 hr to assess for hematoma expansion
opts.rat: Serial imaging detects expansion (up to 30% in first 6 hr). The 30-min CT confirms whether the early neurologic decline reflects expansion (vs edema, IVH evolution, or shock). Findings guide whether to escalate (surgical evacuation, additional reversal, EVD).

opts.text: Bilateral sequential compression devices NOW; reassess pharmacologic DVT prophylaxis at 24–48 hr after stable hematoma confirmed on serial CTs
opts.rat: Mechanical prophylaxis immediately; pharmacologic prophylaxis held in acute hemorrhage and resumed in 24–48 hr if stable. DVT/PE is a leading cause of late morbidity and mortality in immobile stroke patients.

opts.text: Neurosurgical consultation for review of ENRICH-eligible minimally invasive evacuation (deep ICH >20 mL within 24 hr)
opts.rat: Multidisciplinary input. The 2024 ENRICH trial suggested benefit for minimally invasive evacuation of select supratentorial ICHs. This patient has a 35 mL deep clot with IVH and early decline — surgical evaluation is appropriate. Final decision is the neurosurgical team's; the nurse's role is timely consultation and family preparation.

opts.text: Cardiology consultation for post-ICH AFib management (timing of anticoagulation restart vs LAA closure) — to be addressed once stabilized, not in the acute moment
opts.rat: The post-stabilization decision matrix. Once the acute bleed is controlled and the patient stabilized, the AFib stroke risk (CHA2DS2-VASc 4 → ~4%/yr) must be reweighed against rebleed risk. Restart at 4–8 weeks for many; LAA closure (Watchman) for those who cannot tolerate OAC. Multidisciplinary decision.

opts.text: tPA (alteplase) 0.9 mg/kg IV to address the new focal deficit — patient is within the 4.5-hr window
opts.rat: ⚠️ ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED. Active ICH on CT is the most absolute of all tPA contraindications. tPA would catastrophically worsen the bleed. The whole reason CT is mandatory before tPA in stroke is to exclude ICH. This is the highest-yield NCLEX/board exam pitfall: stroke symptoms ≠ tPA without imaging.

opts.text: Nitroprusside drip for blood pressure control
opts.rat: Not preferred in ICH. Cerebral vasodilation can raise ICP; cyanide accumulation with prolonged infusion. Nicardipine and labetalol are the preferred agents — clean titration, no ICP elevation, no cyanide concerns. Reserve nitroprusside for refractory severe HTN under careful monitoring.

opts.text: Levetiracetam 1,000 mg IV BID prophylactically
opts.rat: Not routine in ICH (AHA/ASA guidelines). Studies have not shown benefit and some show harm with phenytoin. Treat seizures REACTIVELY if they occur. (This contrasts with SAH where short-term prophylaxis is sometimes given, and TBI where 7-day prophylaxis has evidence.)

opts.text: Mannitol 1 g/kg IV every 6 hours scheduled for the next 48 hours
opts.rat: Not routine — no scheduled osmotic therapy is recommended. Mannitol and hypertonic saline are reserved for actual ICP crises (worsening exam, herniation signs) NOT prophylaxis. Scheduled mannitol can cause volume depletion and AKI. Hypertonic saline 3% is increasingly the preferred PRN agent.

opts.text: Communication board / simple yes-no questions with family at bedside and frequent reassurance — patient has expressive aphasia but receptive language intact
opts.rat: Critical nursing care. Expressive aphasia means the patient understands but cannot easily produce speech. Loss of communication is a major source of distress. Yes/no questions, communication boards, gesture, family interpretation, and patience are nursing responsibilities — not nice-to-haves.

opts.text: Goals-of-care conversation with patient (while able to participate) and family at appropriate moment — initial discussion now, formal decision at 24–72 hr trajectory
opts.rat: ICH Score 2 with ~26% mortality means meaningful risk but also meaningful survivor population. Do NOT make goals-of-care decisions at hour 1 based on a single point in time — the trajectory over 24–72 hr is much more predictive. Initiate the conversation, gather advance directive information, but reserve final decisions for after stabilization.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Strict bed rest, HOB 30°, NPO, two large-bore IVs, continuous cardiac monitoring, arterial line; bilateral SCDs on
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Immediate safety bundle. None of these require new orders or specialist consultation — they are bedside nursing decisions executed in minutes. They protect the airway, optimize cerebral venous drainage, enable beat-to-beat BP monitoring, and prevent VTE.

opts.text: Start ANDEXANET ALFA high-dose (800 mg IV bolus at 30 mg/min, then 8 mg/min infusion x 120 min); confirm time of last apixaban dose and dose-mg with pharmacy
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. Reversal within the first hour is the single highest-yield intervention to prevent hematoma expansion in DOAC-associated ICH. Pharmacy verifies the regimen and timing; nurse manages the IV infusion. Andexanet effect is rapid (within 30 min of bolus).

opts.text: Start NICARDIPINE drip 5 mg/hr, titrate to SBP 140 mmHg; have labetalol 10 mg IV bolus available for breakthrough
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical. Per INTERACT-3 trial — intensive early BP control to SBP 140 reduces hematoma expansion. Nicardipine is preferred; labetalol is the alternative/PRN. AVOID nitroprusside (ICP, cyanide); AVOID dropping below MAP 80 (CPP).

opts.text: Stat REPEAT non-contrast CT at 30 min after BP control and reversal initiated; serial CTs at 6 hr and 24 hr; neurosurgery consultation for ENRICH-eligible surgical evacuation review
opts.rat: #4 — Diagnostic-therapeutic. The 30-min repeat CT confirms whether the early decline reflects expansion (requiring escalation) vs edema/IVH evolution. Neurosurgery determines surgical candidacy. Time-sensitive — keep the next steps queued.

opts.text: Family meeting; communication board for the expressively aphasic patient; cardiology consultation for post-stabilization anticoagulation restart discussion; speech therapy when stable
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management. The post-stabilization arc: communication restoration, swallow rehab, the AFib anticoagulation decision (restart vs LAA closure), goals-of-care conversation. These layers come after the acute hour is managed but are no less important to the overall outcome.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Hour 6: BP stable SBP 138 on weaning nicardipine, GCS 13 (back to admission), NIHSS 14 (slight improvement), repeat CT shows STABLE 36 mL hematoma without expansion or new IVH
opts.rat: Successful early trajectory — intensive BP control + andexanet alfa worked. Continue nicardipine wean as tolerated to SBP 130–140 maintenance. Confirm stable hematoma on serial CTs. Reassess pharmacologic DVT prophylaxis at 24–48 hr.

opts.text: Day 5: extubated, tolerating thickened liquids with speech therapy clearance, working with PT/OT, communicating with yes/no plus communication board, family meeting completed with cardiology — restart apixaban planned in 4 weeks vs left atrial appendage closure
opts.rat: Excellent disposition trajectory. Speech rehab restoring communication; PT/OT for motor recovery; AFib anticoagulation decision multidisciplinary. The patient may go home with ongoing outpatient rehab and follow-up at 4 weeks for the OAC restart vs LAA closure decision.

opts.text: Week 8 outpatient: patient ambulating with cane, mild residual right arm weakness, expressive aphasia significantly improved with speech therapy, returned to apixaban 5 mg PO BID 6 weeks after ICH after multidisciplinary review (LAA closure declined by patient)
opts.rat: Realistic recovery profile. Many ICH survivors regain meaningful function with intensive rehab. The restart-OAC decision is individualized — many patients with strong stroke-risk profiles can safely resume OAC at 4–8 weeks post-ICH. LAA closure is an alternative for those who cannot tolerate any OAC.

opts.text: Hour 1.5: stat repeat CT shows hematoma expansion 35 → 58 mL with 5 mm midline shift; GCS 8; right pupil 6 mm sluggish; patient intubated emergently
opts.rat: Hematoma expansion despite reversal and BP control. Urgent neurosurgical consultation for evacuation; aggressive ICP management (hypertonic 3% saline 250 mL IV, HOB 30°, hyperventilate to PaCO2 30–35 as a temporizing measure). Re-dose or extend andexanet/4F-PCC if reversal incomplete. Family meeting to update on prognosis.

opts.text: Hour 8: new tonic-clonic seizure lasting 3 minutes; postictal GCS 9; lorazepam 4 mg IV and levetiracetam 1,000 mg IV load given; continuous EEG started
opts.rat: Reactive seizure treatment (the right call — not routine prophylaxis). Levetiracetam load + maintenance, continuous EEG to detect ongoing electrographic seizures (which can drive ICP and worsen outcomes). Rule out hyponatremia, hypoglycemia, contributing electrolyte derangements.

opts.text: Day 3: developing obstructive hydrocephalus on repeat CT (dilated lateral and third ventricles, IVH clot persistent in fourth ventricle); GCS dropping 12 → 9
opts.rat: Hydrocephalus from persistent IVH — common ICH complication. EVD placement by neurosurgery to divert CSF, reduce ICP, and allow drainage of bloody CSF. Monitor EVD output and ICP; consider intraventricular tPA in some protocols to dissolve IVH clot.

opts.text: Day 4: aspiration pneumonia with new fever 102°F, purulent secretions, right lower lobe consolidation, WBC 16
opts.rat: Common post-stroke complication, especially with bulbar weakness/dysphagia. Empiric coverage (e.g., ceftriaxone + metronidazole if aspiration suspected, or vancomycin + cefepime if hospital-acquired). Aggressive pulmonary toilet, HOB 30°, oral care, NG/OG feeding rather than oral until swallow improves.

opts.text: Day 2: hemoglobin drops from 13.2 → 9.2 with melena and coffee-ground emesis from NG tube; bleeding workup positive for upper GI bleed; patient still on andexanet alfa-reversed apixaban with mechanical SCDs only
opts.rat: GI bleed in the post-reversal period — concerning. Hold ALL anticoagulation (still no pharmacologic prophylaxis), GI consultation for endoscopy, transfuse PRBC, IV PPI drip, type and crossmatch. Even though the patient is 'reversed,' the bleeding tendency from underlying disease (gastritis, ulcers, malignancy) is unmasked. Source control via endoscopy.

opts.text: Day 14: GCS 14 with persistent expressive aphasia, right arm 2/5, right leg 3/5; transferred to acute inpatient rehab; tolerating soft diet with thickened liquids; family meeting confirmed plan for outpatient cardiology + neurology follow-up; LAA closure (Watchman) scheduled in 6 weeks
opts.rat: Expected residual deficits in a moderate-severity ICH survivor. The plan for LAA closure rather than OAC restart is reasonable for some patients balancing stroke risk vs rebleed risk. Acute rehab is the next stage; many such patients regain significant function over months.

opts.text: Day 1 post-event: BP target 140 maintained off vasopressors, repeat CTs at 6 and 24 hr show STABLE hematoma without expansion, repeat anti-Xa level <30 ng/mL confirming successful reversal
opts.rat: Confirmed reversal + stable hematoma is the goal of the first 24 hr. Now the focus shifts to subacute management: aspiration precautions, DVT prophylaxis transition, communication restoration, swallow rehab, ICH recurrence prevention (long-term BP control), and the post-stabilization OAC restart decision.

title: Severe Preeclampsia with HELLP Syndrome at 33 Weeks
subtitle: Magnesium for Seizure Prophylaxis, Labetalol for BP, and Delivery as Definitive Treatment

patient.name: L. Brennan
patient.room: L&D Room 3 → MFM ICU pending delivery decision

vitals.time: 0710 (CURRENT — after 4 g IV mag load + 20 mg IV labetalol)
vitals.bp: 162/104 — still in severe range, repeat dose needed

labs.v: 245 U/L (>2x upper limit; baseline 22 at 28-wk visit)
<10 mg/dL (consumed by free hemoglobin from hemolysis)
INR 1.1 / PTT 28 / Fibrinogen 280 / D-dimer 1.6 (early/mild coag changes; not full DIC yet)

labs.n: 24-hr urine protein (if completed; otherwise from spot ratio)
labs.v: Spot ratio extrapolates to >4 g/24 hr

labs.n: Magnesium level (drawn 1 hr after 4 g IV load + 2 g/hr maintenance)
labs.r: Therapeutic 4–7 mg/dL; toxicity at higher levels (see step 2)

labs.v: 4+ brisk biceps and patellar bilaterally with 3-beat clonus at the ankles
Baseline FHR 140; MINIMAL variability; intermittent variable decelerations; no late decelerations YET; gestational age 33+5 weeks; estimated fetal weight 1,950 g (~5th percentile — IUGR)
labs.r: Baseline 110–160; moderate variability; accelerations; no decelerations

labs.v: Singleton viable fetus, cephalic presentation, AFI 6 (oligohydramnios; normal 8–24); no placental abruption visualized; estimated weight 1,950 g consistent with mild IUGR

Severe frontal headache rated 8/10 unrelieved by acetaminophen; intermittent scotomata (small dark spots) in vision over the past 6 hr; photophobia; SEVERE epigastric/RUQ pain rated 7/10 worsened by deep breath; nausea but no vomiting

nursesNote: 0710 — 34 y/o G2P1 at 33+5 weeks gestation by reliable LMP (confirmed by first-trimester ultrasound) walked into L&D triage at 0540 with severe frontal headache rated 8/10 unrelieved by acetaminophen at home, intermittent scotomata, photophobia, and 'pain on the right side under my ribs that won't go away' for the past 12 hr. PMH significant for MILD preeclampsia at 38 weeks with first pregnancy (uncomplicated NSVD of healthy 3,200 g infant after IOL; resolved fully postpartum). She is otherwise healthy with no chronic HTN before pregnancy. BP at 28-week prenatal visit was 116/74 with normal labs and no proteinuria; at her 32+5-week visit ONE WEEK AGO her BP was newly elevated at 138/86 with trace proteinuria on dipstick — her OB had asked her to return in 3 days, but symptoms developed before that follow-up. On L&D arrival BP was 178/112, repeated 15 minutes apart confirmed severe-range hypertension. STAT labs at 0600 returned at 0640 and show full HELLP CRITERIA: Hemolysis (LDH 850, indirect bilirubin 1.8, haptoglobin <10, SCHISTOCYTES on smear, Hgb 9.2 down from 11.8), Elevated Liver enzymes (AST 245, ALT 218), Low Platelets (78 K from 195 K). Plus severe proteinuria (urine P/C ratio 2.8 → >4 g/24 hr extrapolated), new AKI (Cr 1.2 from 0.7), and uric acid 8.4. DTRs are markedly brisk (4+) with 3-beat clonus — strong warning sign of impending eclampsia. Continuous fetal monitoring shows baseline FHR 140 with MINIMAL variability and intermittent variable decelerations (no late decelerations YET), with bedside ultrasound showing oligohydramnios (AFI 6) and estimated fetal weight at the 5th percentile consistent with IUGR — picture of chronic uteroplacental insufficiency from the underlying preeclampsia. No vaginal bleeding, no contractions, fundus non-tender, no signs of placental abruption. MAGNESIUM SULFATE 4 g IV LOADING DOSE infused over 20 min beginning 0625, followed by 2 g/hr continuous infusion for seizure prophylaxis. LABETALOL 20 mg IV bolus given at 0635 for severe-range HTN; current BP at 0710 is 162/104 (still in severe range — repeat 40 mg IV labetalol now in line). BETAMETHASONE 12 mg IM given at 0650 (first of two doses, 24 hr apart) for fetal lung maturity given the 33+5-week gestational age. IV fluids set carefully at 80 mL/hr (cautious — pulmonary edema risk in severe preeclampsia/HELLP). Foley catheter placed: 110 mL clear urine returned — strict hourly UOP monitoring (must remain >30 mL/hr; magnesium clearance is renal and toxicity rises rapidly with oliguria). Calcium gluconate 1 g IV is drawn and at the bedside as the magnesium-toxicity antidote. Patient is in left lateral position to optimize uteroplacental flow; coordinating with MFM, anesthesia, and NICU for likely delivery within 24–48 hr after the betamethasone window (or sooner if maternal/fetal status worsens). Husband at bedside, tearful but tracking; her OB is en route. The plan is being explained in plain language — including the unfamiliar concept that magnesium is for SEIZURE PROPHYLAXIS, not blood pressure control, and that DELIVERY is the only definitive treatment for the disease.
providerOrders: MAGNESIUM SULFATE 4 g IV loading dose over 20 min, then 2 g/hr continuous IV infusion for seizure prophylaxis (NOT for BP control); continue magnesium for 24 HOURS POSTPARTUM
Magnesium toxicity surveillance: DTRs q1h, UOP q1h, RR q1h, SpO2 continuous; have CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV (10 mL of 10%) AT THE BEDSIDE as the antidote; serum magnesium level q4h initially
HOLD magnesium and notify provider for any of: DTRs ABSENT, RR <12, UOP <30 mL/hr over 4 hr, SpO2 <94%, or serum Mg >8 mg/dL

LABETALOL 20 mg IV bolus over 2 min for severe-range HTN; if no response in 10 min give 40 mg IV; then 80 mg IV q10min PRN to a maximum cumulative dose of 300 mg in 24 hr — TARGET SBP 140–155 and DBP 90–105 (NOT lower — placental perfusion)
ALTERNATIVE / ADD-ON: hydralazine 5–10 mg IV q20–40 min PRN (may cause more reflex tachycardia and unpredictable drops in BP)
ALTERNATIVE for outpatient/PO: nifedipine immediate-release 10 mg PO; may repeat 20 mg in 20 min
AVOID ACE inhibitors, ARBs, nitroprusside, and atenolol in pregnancy (teratogenicity / hypoperfusion / cyanide)
BETAMETHASONE 12 mg IM now (dose 1 of 2); repeat 12 mg IM in 24 hr (dose 2 of 2) for fetal lung maturity at 33+5 weeks
Continuous fetal monitoring; left lateral position to optimize uteroplacental flow; intermittent reposition to right lateral as tolerated
Strict IV fluid management — TOTAL FLUID NO MORE THAN 80–125 mL/hr (pulmonary edema risk in severe preeclampsia/HELLP from endothelial leak)
Foley catheter for strict hourly UOP; daily weights

STAT labs: CBC with platelet count, CMP, AST/ALT, LDH, total/direct bilirubin, haptoglobin, peripheral smear, INR/PTT/fibrinogen/D-dimer, uric acid, urine protein-to-creatinine ratio; REPEAT CBC and CMP q6h to detect HELLP progression and DIC
Type and screen, crossmatch 2 units PRBC; have FFP and platelets available
Seizure precautions: padded side rails up, suction at bedside, oxygen and bag-mask ventilation at bedside, IV lorazepam 4 mg AT THE BEDSIDE for breakthrough seizure despite mag
MFM (Maternal-Fetal Medicine) consult; anesthesiology consult for delivery planning; NICU consult for resuscitation team standby; OB at bedside for ongoing assessment
Quiet environment, dim lights, minimize stimulation (high cortical irritability — visual/auditory triggers can precipitate seizure)

Delivery plan: deliver after the betamethasone course (24+ hr after first dose) IF maternal/fetal status remains stable on therapy; deliver SOONER if any of: uncontrollable HTN despite max antihypertensives, eclamptic seizure, worsening HELLP with DIC progression, placental abruption, non-reassuring fetal status (recurrent late decelerations, bradycardia, loss of variability), pulmonary edema, or hepatic rupture

Postpartum management: continue mag x 24 hr after delivery (50% of eclamptic seizures occur POSTPARTUM); BP monitoring q4h x 24 hr then per protocol; outpatient BP cuff for 6 weeks home monitoring; PATIENT EDUCATION on postpartum HELLP/eclampsia warning signs persisting up to 6 weeks

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: BP 178/112 on arrival, persistently 162/104 after labetalol — SEVERE-RANGE hypertension (≥160/110)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — severe-range BP. Defined as SBP ≥160 OR DBP ≥110, confirmed by repeat measurement 15 min apart (or any single severe reading with symptoms). Severe-range HTN must be treated within 30–60 min with first-line IV agents (labetalol, hydralazine) to reduce maternal stroke and abruption risk.

opts.text: Full HELLP criteria: Hemolysis (LDH 850, schistocytes, haptoglobin <10), Elevated Liver enzymes (AST 245), Low Platelets (78 K)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms HELLP syndrome (variant of severe preeclampsia with multi-organ involvement). HELLP is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY with risk of subcapsular hepatic hematoma/rupture, DIC, AKI, pulmonary edema, abruption, and maternal/fetal death. Delivery is the definitive treatment regardless of gestational age once HELLP is confirmed (after betamethasone if time allows and mother is stable).

opts.text: Severe headache 8/10, intermittent scotomata, photophobia, hyperreflexia 4+ with 3-beat clonus
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — CNS irritability warning signs for IMPENDING ECLAMPSIA. Headache and visual changes reflect cerebral edema and posterior reversible encephalopathy (PRES). Hyperreflexia and clonus reflect cortical hyperexcitability. These are the symptoms magnesium sulfate is given to PREVENT progression to seizure.

opts.text: Severe RUQ/epigastric pain 7/10 worsened by deep breath
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — hepatic capsule stretch from sinusoidal congestion and microhemorrhage in HELLP. Concerning for subcapsular hematoma; severe persistent pain with hemodynamic change suggests possible hepatic RUPTURE — a surgical emergency. Bedside ultrasound or CT abdomen if RUQ pain worsens or new signs develop.

opts.text: Severe proteinuria (urine P/C ratio 2.8 → >4 g/24 hr) with new AKI (Cr 1.2 from baseline 0.7)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — multi-organ involvement. Severe proteinuria is part of the preeclampsia diagnostic criteria; AKI reflects renal endothelial damage and hypoperfusion. The Cr is RELATIVELY high (pregnancy baseline is normally 0.4–0.8 with increased GFR; 1.2 is significantly abnormal).

opts.text: Fetal monitoring: MINIMAL variability + intermittent variable decelerations; AFI 6 (oligohydramnios); estimated fetal weight at 5th percentile (IUGR)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — fetal compromise from chronic uteroplacental insufficiency. Minimal variability and decelerations suggest the fetus is under stress; IUGR and oligohydramnios reflect chronic placental dysfunction. NEW LATE decelerations or sustained bradycardia would trigger emergent delivery.

opts.text: Gestational age 33+5 weeks — preterm but viable; benefits from betamethasone for fetal lung maturity if delivery is not imminent
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — gestational age guides timing decisions. Betamethasone 12 mg IM q24h x 2 doses given between 24–34 weeks reduces neonatal RDS, IVH, and mortality. The 24–48-hr betamethasone window is a critical reason to STABILIZE before delivery — but it can be cut short by maternal/fetal deterioration.

opts.text: Hemoglobin 9.2 (down from 11.8) with peripheral smear showing schistocytes
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA). RBCs are mechanically sheared as they pass through small vessels narrowed by endothelial damage and fibrin deposition. Same physical mechanism as TTP and DIC. Confirms the 'H' of HELLP and signals systemic microvascular disease.

opts.text: Mag level 5.8 mg/dL after 4 g IV load + 2 g/hr maintenance
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — therapeutic range (4–7 mg/dL). Monitoring: trough levels q4–6h initially, then q6–12h once stable. Mag levels rise with renal dysfunction; AKI (Cr 1.2 here) raises toxicity risk and necessitates closer monitoring and possible dose reduction.

opts.text: Prior history of mild preeclampsia at 38 weeks in first pregnancy
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — major risk factor. Prior preeclampsia ~4x increases risk for current pregnancy preeclampsia AND increases severity. Standard prevention: ASA 81 mg daily from ~12 weeks for high-risk patients — important secondary-prevention teaching point for FUTURE pregnancies and long-term cardiovascular risk counseling.

opts.text: Temperature 98.6°F
opts.rat: Normal — not actionable. Fever would raise concern for chorioamnionitis or other infectious process and would change the differential, but the current temperature is reassuring.

opts.text: Negative for vaginal bleeding, no contractions, fundus non-tender
opts.rat: Reassuring (no clinical evidence of placental abruption) but PERTINENT and should be re-checked frequently. Abruption is a major complication of severe preeclampsia/HELLP — sudden onset of pain, contractions, bleeding (may be concealed), or change in fetal status warrants immediate evaluation.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Preeclampsia begins with abnormal placental implantation and spiral artery remodeling early in pregnancy — placental ischemia releases antiangiogenic factors (sFlt-1, soluble endoglin) that cause SYSTEMIC ENDOTHELIAL DYSFUNCTION across all organs, producing the multisystem picture (HTN, proteinuria, hepatic injury, microangiopathic hemolysis, cerebral edema, pulmonary edema, IUGR)
opts.rat: CORRECT — central mechanism. The placenta drives the disease via circulating antiangiogenic factors; the maternal endothelium responds with vasoconstriction, leak, and microthrombi. This is WHY delivery is the definitive treatment — removing the placenta removes the source of antiangiogenic factors.

opts.text: MAGNESIUM SULFATE is given for SEIZURE PROPHYLAXIS — it stabilizes cerebral cortical irritability, antagonizes NMDA receptors, and may reduce cerebral vasospasm; it is NOT a primary antihypertensive (BP drops mildly but unreliably)
opts.rat: CORRECT and HIGH-YIELD. The most common conceptual error among students. Magnesium reduces eclamptic seizures by ~50% compared to phenytoin or placebo (MAGPIE, Collaborative Eclampsia Trial). BP control requires a separate agent (labetalol, hydralazine, nifedipine). Continue magnesium throughout labor/delivery AND for 24 hours postpartum.

opts.text: Magnesium toxicity is dose- and renal-function dependent: therapeutic 4–7 mg/dL; loss of DTRs ~8–10; respiratory depression ~12–15; cardiac arrest >25. Antidote is CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV (10 mL of 10%) — must be at the bedside whenever mag is infusing.
opts.rat: CORRECT — the toxicity ladder and antidote. DTR loss is the EARLIEST clinical sign and usually precedes respiratory depression by a comfortable margin. UOP <30 mL/hr signals impaired renal mag clearance and rising toxicity risk; HOLD mag and reassess. Calcium gluconate displaces magnesium from neuromuscular and cardiac binding sites.

opts.text: Severe-range hypertension (SBP ≥160 OR DBP ≥110) must be treated within 30–60 minutes to reduce risk of maternal stroke, abruption, and pulmonary edema. First-line agents: labetalol IV, hydralazine IV, or PO immediate-release nifedipine. AVOID ACE inhibitors and ARBs (teratogenic in any trimester), nitroprusside (cyanide toxicity), and atenolol (IUGR).
opts.rat: CORRECT — preeclampsia BP management framework. Target SBP 140–155 / DBP 90–105 — NOT lower (placental perfusion). The 30–60 min window comes from ACOG guidelines tied to maternal stroke prevention. Note this BP target is HIGHER than the ICH SBP-140 target (case-043) because of placental perfusion needs.

opts.text: HELLP can present POSTPARTUM (up to 7 days, occasionally longer) — patients are at risk for new-onset or progressive HELLP, severe HTN, and eclamptic seizure for days after delivery; ~50% of all eclamptic seizures occur postpartum
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield discharge teaching. Patients are sent home thinking they are 'cured' by delivery and may not recognize warning signs (severe headache, visual changes, RUQ pain, swelling, decreased UOP) that emerge in the first 6 weeks. The teaching is mandatory for survival of mother and family vigilance is essential. Home BP cuff monitoring x 6 weeks postpartum is standard.

opts.text: Magnesium IS contraindicated in this patient because of impaired neuromuscular transmission
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — this would apply to MYASTHENIA GRAVIS (case-039) where IV magnesium can precipitate or worsen crisis. In preeclampsia, magnesium is the STANDARD seizure prophylaxis. Same drug, opposite physiology, opposite indication. The contrast is one of the highest-yield teaching points across these two cases.

opts.text: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril) and ARBs (losartan) should be added for tight BP control
opts.rat: ⚠️ DIRECTLY CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy across all trimesters. Cause renal dysgenesis, oligohydramnios, pulmonary hypoplasia, IUGR, and neonatal hypotension/renal failure. The acceptable agents are: labetalol, hydralazine, methyldopa (chronic), nifedipine. For acute severe HTN: labetalol, hydralazine, or IR nifedipine.

opts.text: The goal of BP control is to bring SBP <120 mmHg as quickly as possible
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — overcorrection risk. Target is SBP 140–155 and DBP 90–105 to preserve uteroplacental perfusion. Lowering MAP excessively can produce placental hypoperfusion, fetal bradycardia, and worsen the IUGR. This contrasts with ICH-SBP-140 target (case-043) where placental perfusion is not a consideration.

opts.text: Delivery is the definitive treatment for preeclampsia/HELLP — at 33+5 weeks, the optimal plan if mother stable is to administer betamethasone for fetal lung maturity over 24–48 hr and then deliver, with earlier delivery if maternal or fetal status deteriorates
opts.rat: CORRECT. The timing is a balance: severe preeclampsia/HELLP <34 weeks is generally managed with betamethasone course and delivery soon after if mother stable; HELLP itself often demands earlier delivery. ANY deterioration (uncontrolled HTN, seizure, abruption, hepatic decline, DIC, non-reassuring fetal monitoring, pulmonary edema, hepatic rupture) → expedite delivery regardless of betamethasone status.

opts.text: Aggressive crystalloid bolus 30 mL/kg should be given to support BP and renal perfusion
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT and DANGEROUS in severe preeclampsia/HELLP. Endothelial leak makes these patients exquisitely vulnerable to pulmonary edema — fluid bolus can precipitate flash pulmonary edema. Limit total fluid to ~80–125 mL/hr; resist the sepsis-style reflex to give aggressive volume. The BP problem is fixed with antihypertensives, not fluids.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Eclamptic Seizure r/t severe preeclampsia with HELLP and impending-eclampsia symptoms (severe HA, scotomata, hyperreflexia 4+ with clonus)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The CNS irritability warning signs all point to imminent seizure. Eclamptic seizures cause maternal hypoxia, aspiration, abruption, and fetal bradycardia. Mag sulfate prophylaxis already loading; lorazepam at bedside for breakthrough; quiet environment to minimize cortical stimulation.

opts.text: Risk for Maternal Stroke and Abruption r/t persistent severe-range hypertension (162/104 despite 20 mg labetalol)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Severe-range HTN must be controlled within 30–60 min. Each additional labetalol/hydralazine dose is a step; if the BP doesn't respond to escalating labetalol (40, 80 mg), switch to hydralazine or nicardipine drip. Stroke and placental abruption are the feared maternal complications of persistent severe HTN.

opts.text: Risk for Fetal Compromise r/t chronic uteroplacental insufficiency, IUGR, oligohydramnios, and ongoing maternal disease (minimal variability + variable decelerations)
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. The fetus shows chronic compromise (IUGR, oligohydramnios) AND acute concerning patterns (minimal variability, variable decelerations). Optimization measures: left lateral position, maternal O2 if non-reassuring patterns persist, IV fluid bolus carefully limited. New LATE decelerations or sustained bradycardia → expedite delivery.

opts.text: Risk for Hepatic Hematoma/Rupture r/t HELLP with severe RUQ pain (subcapsular hematoma is a feared complication)
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Severe persistent RUQ pain is a warning sign for subcapsular hepatic hematoma; sudden severe pain with hemodynamic change suggests hepatic rupture (catastrophic). Bedside US or CT abdomen if RUQ pain worsens or new signs appear. Treatment is emergent surgical repair / packing; mortality is high.

opts.text: Risk for Pulmonary Edema r/t endothelial leak, hypoalbuminemia, and possible iatrogenic fluid overload
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in the acute ranking but a real risk during management. Severe preeclampsia/HELLP has endothelial leak; aggressive fluid resuscitation or excessive maintenance fluids can precipitate flash pulmonary edema. Limit total fluids to 80–125 mL/hr; monitor SpO2 and lung exam; furosemide 20 mg IV if pulmonary edema develops.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: MAGNESIUM SULFATE 4 g IV load over 20 min, then 2 g/hr continuous infusion for seizure prophylaxis; continue x 24 hr postpartum
opts.rat: Standard. Magnesium reduces eclamptic seizures by ~50%. Continue throughout labor/delivery and for 24 hours postpartum (about 50% of eclamptic seizures occur postpartum). Have calcium gluconate at bedside as antidote.

opts.text: Magnesium toxicity surveillance: DTRs q1h, UOP q1h, RR q1h, SpO2 continuous; serum Mg q4h initially; HOLD mag and notify provider for DTRs absent, RR <12, UOP <30 mL/hr, SpO2 <94%, or Mg >8 mg/dL
opts.rat: Core bedside nursing surveillance. Mag is renally cleared and the patient has new AKI (Cr 1.2) — toxicity risk is elevated. DTR loss is the earliest clinical sign. Calcium gluconate 1 g IV at the bedside as the antidote.

opts.text: Repeat LABETALOL 40 mg IV bolus now; if no response in 10 min give 80 mg IV; escalate as needed up to cumulative max 300 mg in 24 hr — TARGET SBP 140–155 / DBP 90–105
opts.rat: First-line severe-range HTN treatment. Labetalol is preferred (clean beta+alpha blockade, predictable effect). Target preserves placental perfusion. If no response to labetalol max, switch to hydralazine 5–10 mg IV q20–40 min or initiate nicardipine drip.

opts.text: BETAMETHASONE 12 mg IM now (dose 1 of 2); repeat 12 mg IM in 24 hr for fetal lung maturity at 33+5 weeks
opts.rat: Standard antenatal corticosteroid for delivery between 24–34 weeks (and 34–37 weeks if not previously given). Reduces neonatal RDS, IVH, NEC, and mortality. Single-dose IM is ideal; rescue course possible if more than 14 days has elapsed and the patient is still <34 weeks at risk.

opts.text: Continuous fetal monitoring, left lateral position to optimize uteroplacental flow; maternal O2 if non-reassuring patterns persist
opts.rat: Real-time fetal surveillance is critical. Left lateral position takes the gravid uterus off the IVC, improves venous return and cardiac output, and improves uteroplacental flow. Supplemental O2 traditionally given for non-reassuring tracings (evidence is mixed but it's standard practice as a temporizing measure).

opts.text: Seizure precautions: padded side rails, oxygen and bag-mask at bedside, IV lorazepam 4 mg AT THE BEDSIDE for breakthrough seizure despite mag
opts.rat: Layered safety. Mag prophylaxis reduces but does not eliminate seizure risk; about 10–15% of eclamptic seizures occur despite therapeutic mag. Lorazepam is the breakthrough rescue. Quiet environment, dim lights, and minimal stimulation reduce cortical excitation.

opts.text: Quiet environment, dim lights, minimize stimulation (visual/auditory triggers can precipitate seizure)
opts.rat: Often overlooked nursing intervention. Photophobia and scotomata reflect cortical hyperexcitability — bright lights, loud noises, and excessive stimulation can lower seizure threshold. Cluster nursing tasks, dim the lights, post a sign on the door for staff.

opts.text: Aggressive IV fluid bolus 30 mL/kg of LR for the new AKI and possible volume support
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS in severe preeclampsia/HELLP. Endothelial leak makes these patients exquisitely vulnerable to flash pulmonary edema. Limit total fluid to 80–125 mL/hr. The AKI improves with delivery and BP control, NOT with fluids. The BP problem is fixed with antihypertensives, not volume.

opts.text: Lisinopril 10 mg PO for chronic BP control before discharge
opts.rat: ⚠️ ACE inhibitors are CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy across all trimesters (renal dysgenesis, oligohydramnios, pulmonary hypoplasia, fetal hypotension/renal failure). Postpartum AND not breastfeeding, an ACE inhibitor can be considered. For pregnancy and breastfeeding, options include: labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa, hydralazine.

opts.text: Nitroprusside drip for rapid BP control
opts.rat: Avoid in pregnancy — cyanide accumulation can cause fetal toxicity. Acceptable pregnancy IV options: labetalol, hydralazine, nicardipine. Nitroprusside is reserved for refractory hypertensive emergencies under careful monitoring and even then time-limited.

opts.text: Coordinate delivery plan with MFM, anesthesia, and NICU; deliver after 24–48 hr betamethasone window if mother stable; deliver SOONER if maternal/fetal status deteriorates
opts.rat: Definitive treatment. The interdisciplinary delivery plan balances betamethasone benefit against maternal/fetal deterioration risk. Modes: vaginal delivery is preferred if cervix favorable and time allows; cesarean for non-reassuring fetal status, failed induction, or other obstetric indication. Regional anesthesia is preferred IF platelets >70–80 K (this patient is borderline).

opts.text: Postpartum: continue magnesium for 24 hr; BP monitoring per protocol; home BP cuff at discharge with 6-week follow-up; ACOG-standard patient education on POSTPARTUM warning signs (severe headache, vision changes, RUQ pain, swelling, decreased UOP, shortness of breath)
opts.rat: High-yield. ~50% of eclamptic seizures and most HELLP-related morbidity occur POSTPARTUM. Many patients are sent home thinking they are 'cured' by delivery and miss warning signs. Mandatory teaching with written instructions, home BP cuff, and a clear plan for returning to the ED. Long-term cardiovascular risk counseling (preeclampsia 2x ASCVD risk lifelong).

opts.text: Calcium gluconate 1 g IV (10 mL of 10%) drawn and AT THE BEDSIDE — the magnesium-toxicity antidote
opts.rat: Standard of care whenever mag is infusing. Calcium gluconate displaces magnesium from neuromuscular and cardiac binding sites, reversing respiratory and cardiac depression. Push slowly (over 3 min) for respiratory or cardiac toxicity. The bedside availability is a quality-of-care metric — do NOT page pharmacy at the moment of crisis.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Place client in LEFT LATERAL position; continuous fetal monitoring; continuous maternal cardiac monitoring; SpO2; Foley catheter for strict hourly UOP; calcium gluconate 1 g IV drawn at bedside
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Immediate safety bundle. Left lateral optimizes uteroplacental flow; Foley enables strict UOP tracking (essential for mag safety with new AKI); calcium gluconate at bedside is a quality-of-care imperative whenever mag is infusing.

opts.text: Start MAGNESIUM SULFATE 4 g IV load over 20 min, then 2 g/hr maintenance infusion (already in progress); begin q1h DTR/UOP/RR/SpO2 surveillance
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. Mag prophylaxis is the primary intervention to reduce seizure progression in a patient with impending-eclampsia warning signs. Bedside surveillance for toxicity is concurrent with the infusion — DTR loss is the earliest clinical sign, generally precedes respiratory depression.

opts.text: Repeat LABETALOL 40 mg IV bolus NOW for persistent severe-range HTN (162/104); recheck BP in 10 min and escalate to 80 mg IV if needed; target SBP 140–155 and DBP 90–105
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical (within 30–60 min of severe-range BP). The first 20 mg labetalol was inadequate; escalate per protocol. Goal preserves placental perfusion — not <120/80. If labetalol max ineffective, switch to hydralazine or nicardipine drip.

opts.text: Administer BETAMETHASONE 12 mg IM (already given); MFM, anesthesia, and NICU consults for delivery planning; type and screen for crossmatch; STAT repeat CBC, CMP, AST/ALT, LDH, INR/PTT/fibrinogen, peripheral smear q6h to detect HELLP progression and DIC
opts.rat: #4 — Coordination of definitive treatment. Betamethasone gives the fetus a fighting chance for the next 24+ hours; the team coordinates the delivery plan; serial labs detect HELLP progression and DIC. If labs worsen or new severe symptoms develop, expedite delivery regardless of betamethasone status.

opts.text: Quiet environment, dim lights, minimize stimulation, family at bedside for emotional support; explain plan in plain language (esp. that mag is for SEIZURES, not BP, and that DELIVERY is the definitive treatment)
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management and patient-centered care. Cortical hyperexcitability is real — environmental noise/light can lower seizure threshold. Family understanding is essential for support. The plain-language explanation of magnesium and the role of delivery prevents the most common patient/family confusion.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Hour 4: 2 doses of labetalol effective with BP now 144/92, persistent therapeutic mag, headache and scotomata RESOLVED with mag, DTRs now 2+ without clonus, fetal HR baseline 145 with moderate variability after left lateral repositioning
opts.rat: Excellent response. The headache/scotomata resolution AND DTR normalization reflect mag's CNS-stabilizing effect. The fetal pattern improvement after positioning suggests reversible uteroplacental cause. Continue current plan toward betamethasone-completion and delivery.

opts.text: Hour 28: betamethasone course complete; cervix favorable (Bishop 7); induction of labor with oxytocin; vaginal delivery of 1,920 g female infant with Apgars 6/8 transferred to Level 3 NICU; mag continuing for 24-hr postpartum window
opts.rat: Successful delivery trajectory. Vaginal delivery preferred when cervix favorable and time allows. Infant transferred to NICU for prematurity care — Apgar 6/8 is acceptable given gestational age. Mag continues x 24 hr postpartum because most postpartum eclamptic seizures occur in this window.
