labs.v: MEETS multiple criteria for burn center transfer: (1) partial-thickness >10% TBSA; (2) any full-thickness burns; (3) burns of face and hand; (4) inhalation injury; (5) circumferential extremity burn with vascular compromise. WE ARE the regional burn center — no transfer needed.
labs.r: Burn center transfer indicated when any one criterion is met

nursesNote: 0610 — 28 y/o male brought in by EMS at 0520 after a house fire at his apartment. Per fire department: candles ignited bedding around 0440; client fell asleep on the couch and woke surrounded by smoke and flame at ~0445; escaped through a window with burns over the anterior trunk, both arms, face, and right thigh. Brief loss of consciousness in the smoke (~2 min per fire department report). EMS arrived at 0451 — high-flow O2 via NRB 15 L started, two large-bore IVs through unburned forearm skin, 1 L LR running, fentanyl 100 mcg IV for pain. EMS noted singed nasal hair, soot in oropharynx, hoarse voice, and progressive stridor en route but elected NOT to intubate in the field — they arrived at our ED at 0520. On arrival: GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6, confused but tracking), HR 138, BP 96/58, RR 32 with audible stridor, SpO2 100% on NRB. PULSE OX IS FALSELY HIGH due to carboxyhemoglobin (CO binds hemoglobin and reads similarly to O2 on standard pulse oximetry). STAT carboxyhemoglobin returned at 0540 = 32% (severe CO toxicity). Lactate is 11.4 with ABG showing pH 7.21, HCO3 14, BE −12, anion gap 24 — picture concerning for CONCURRENT CYANIDE TOXICITY from synthetic combustion of plastics and foam in the bedding/furniture (cyanide blocks the mitochondrial electron transport chain, producing severe lactic acidosis disproportionate to perfusion). Rule of Nines estimate ~45% TBSA partial-and-full-thickness burns: anterior trunk 18% (deep partial + some full-thickness), RIGHT arm 9% CIRCUMFERENTIAL FULL-THICKNESS (white leathery eschar with NO sensation), LEFT arm 9% partial-thickness, anterior right thigh ~9% partial. Face has flash burns with singed eyebrows but minimal counted TBSA. Weight 80 kg. PARKLAND FORMULA: 4 mL × 80 kg × 45 = 14,400 mL LR over 24 hr FROM TIME OF BURN (0445), with HALF (7,200 mL) over the first 8 hr ending at 1245 → target rate ~900 mL/hr currently (adjusted for ~1.5 L already given in the field/ED). UOP target 30–50 mL/hr. INTUBATION done at 0555 (anesthesia, awake fiberoptic) given singed nares, soot below cords on direct visualization, hoarseness, stridor, and impending airway loss; mucosal edema visible at carina. HYDROXOCOBALAMIN 5 g IV started at 0600 for empiric cyanide treatment (lactate >10 + smoke inhalation + severe metabolic acidosis + hemodynamic instability = strong indication). 100% FiO2 to reduce CO half-life from ~4–6 hr (room air) to ~1 hr. RIGHT ARM circumferential burn has lost the radial pulse and Doppler signal — ESCHAROTOMY by burn surgery now in progress at 0608. Foley placed; UOP 30 mL of dark cola-colored urine first hour — myoglobinuria from rhabdomyolysis (CK 12,400). NG tube placed (NPO; gastric ileus is common in major burns). IV opioid infusion (fentanyl 50–100 mcg/hr) for pain control. Tetanus-diphtheria-acellular pertussis (Tdap) booster being administered (last booster ~9 yr ago — burns are tetanus-prone wounds). Wife and 2 small children at bedside being supported by social work and chaplaincy. Notification to the police and fire investigators per protocol (suspected accidental cause confirmed; client at fault but no criminal implications). Plan: full burn ICU admission after stabilization in the ED resus bay; serial neurologic, perfusion, and respiratory checks; bronchoscopy already done; burn dressings (silver-impregnated) being applied after escharotomy; NO PROPHYLACTIC SYSTEMIC ANTIBIOTICS (burns are sterile from heat initially; infection risk rises after 48 hr and is treated REACTIVELY based on cultures and signs).
providerOrders: INTUBATION COMPLETED at 0555 (awake fiberoptic by anesthesia given anticipated difficult airway from facial burns and angioedema); ETT 8.0 oral; mechanical ventilation FiO2 100%, PEEP 5, TV 6 mL/kg ideal body weight; titrate FiO2 down only after carboxyhemoglobin <5% (typically 6–8 hr on 100% O2)
TWO LARGE-BORE IVs through UNBURNED skin if possible (forearms preferred over burned chest); central line if peripheral access difficult and major burn anticipated

PARKLAND FORMULA: 4 mL × 80 kg × 45 = 14,400 mL Lactated Ringer's over 24 hr FROM TIME OF BURN (not from arrival); first 7,200 mL by 1245 (8 hr from 0445 burn time); next 7,200 mL over the following 16 hr; TITRATE to UOP 30–50 mL/hr adult (0.5–1 mL/kg/hr); higher UOP target 75–100 mL/hr with myoglobinuria/rhabdo (this client)

HYDROXOCOBALAMIN 5 g IV over 15 min for empiric cyanide toxicity treatment (lactate >10 + smoke inhalation + acidosis + hemodynamic instability); turns urine and skin RED-pink — expected, not adverse; may interfere with some lab assays (co-oximetry, urine catecholamines)

AVOID sodium nitrite + sodium thiosulfate (older cyanide kit) in burn patients because nitrite-induced methemoglobinemia ADDED to existing carboxyhemoglobinemia further reduces oxygen-carrying capacity; hydroxocobalamin is the preferred agent for smoke-inhalation cyanide toxicity

100% FiO2 to clear carbon monoxide; CO half-life on room air ~4–6 hr, on 100% O2 ~60–80 min, on hyperbaric O2 ~20–30 min; consider HYPERBARIC O2 if available within 6 hr for COHb >25% OR neurologic symptoms OR pregnancy with COHb >15% OR LOC at the scene
Continuous CO-OXIMETRY (special bedside SpCO meter measures actual carboxyhemoglobin — STANDARD pulse oximetry reads FALSELY normal because it cannot distinguish O2-Hb from CO-Hb)

ESCHAROTOMY of RIGHT arm by burn surgery IN PROGRESS (loss of radial pulse and Doppler signal due to circumferential full-thickness eschar) — vertical incision along medial and lateral midline through the eschar to allow expansion; consider torso escharotomy if ventilator compliance worsens from circumferential trunk burns

Foley catheter for STRICT hourly UOP monitoring; target 30–50 mL/hr (0.5–1 mL/kg/hr) baseline; ESCALATE to 75–100 mL/hr WITH myoglobinuria/rhabdomyolysis (CK 12,400 + dark urine here) — flush the tubules of myoglobin

NG/OG tube for gastric decompression (paralytic ileus common in burns >20% TBSA); NPO initially; advance enteral nutrition via NG within 24–48 hr (early enteral feeding reduces gut translocation and improves outcomes in burns)

PAIN CONTROL: fentanyl drip 50–100 mcg/hr IV (avoid IM injections — variable absorption with fluid shifts and tissue edema); ketamine 0.1–0.3 mg/kg/hr IV as adjunct (analgesia + sedation + bronchodilation, no respiratory depression)
Tdap booster IM today (last dose ~9 yr ago; burns are tetanus-prone wounds); TIG only if vaccine history unknown or incomplete

Burn wound care: room-temperature water cooling (NEVER ice — risks frostbite and excessive heat loss in major burns); remove clothing/jewelry from burned areas; clean dressings with silver sulfadiazine 1% (avoid on face — staining and rare hematologic effects in large areas) OR mafenide acetate (penetrates eschar but causes metabolic acidosis) OR biologic dressings

DO NOT routinely start prophylactic systemic antibiotics — burns are initially sterile from the heat; infection risk rises after 48 hr; surveillance wound cultures; antibiotics ONLY for documented or strongly suspected infection (with culture-guided narrowing)

Hourly: vital signs, neuro checks, distal perfusion of escharotomy sites, UOP, drainage; q2h: turn (when stable) for skin integrity; q4h: ABG, lactate, electrolytes, CK, Cr; serial co-oximetry until CO-Hb <5%
GLUCOSE management: insulin sliding scale per protocol — stress hyperglycemia common in major burns and worsens outcomes; goal 140–180 mg/dL
DVT prophylaxis: bilateral SCDs immediately; enoxaparin 40 mg SubQ daily once bleeding risk acceptable (post-escharotomy, after first 24 hr)

Burn-center supportive care plan: PT/OT consult Day 1 for positioning and splinting (prevent contractures); chaplaincy + social work for family support; child life if visiting children; consult psychiatry for acute stress disorder/PTSD risk; ophthalmology for facial/eye burns; renal team if rhabdo worsens

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Singed nasal hair + soot in oropharynx + hoarse voice + audible STRIDOR (pre-intubation) + facial burns
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — airway threat. These are the bedside signs of INHALATION INJURY and predict imminent airway closure from progressive edema. The threshold for EARLY ELECTIVE INTUBATION should be LOW. Waiting for overt respiratory distress means a difficult/impossible intubation in a swollen airway.

opts.text: Carboxyhemoglobin 32% with FALSELY normal SpO2 of 100%
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — high-yield 'pulse-ox-lies' concept. Standard pulse oximetry uses two wavelengths and reads CO-Hb essentially the same as O2-Hb — so SpO2 can be 100% while the blood cannot deliver oxygen to tissues. Order CO-Hb (co-oximetry or arterial blood gas with co-oximetry); treat with 100% O2 (and hyperbaric O2 if criteria met).

opts.text: Lactate 11.4 + severe metabolic acidosis pH 7.21 / HCO3 14 / AG 24 + hemodynamic instability — concerning for CYANIDE toxicity from synthetic-combustion smoke
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — cyanide blocks cytochrome c oxidase in the mitochondrial electron transport chain, halting aerobic metabolism. Result: severe lactic acidosis with NORMAL SaO2 and normal arterial O2 (oxygen is delivered but cannot be USED). Empiric hydroxocobalamin 5 g IV when smoke inhalation + lactate >10 + acidosis + instability; turns urine and skin RED.

opts.text: TBSA ~45% by Rule of Nines (anterior trunk 18% + right arm 9% circumferential FT + left arm 9% partial + anterior right thigh 9% partial)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — determines the fluid resuscitation plan. Parkland formula: 4 mL × kg × %TBSA = total LR over 24 hr FROM TIME OF BURN; half in first 8 hr, half in next 16 hr. Rule of Nines is for adults (Lund-Browder for children — kids' heads are proportionally larger). Palm method (~1% per palm including fingers) for irregular burns.

opts.text: RIGHT arm circumferential full-thickness burn with LOSS of radial pulse and Doppler signal
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — ESCHAROTOMY indication. Full-thickness eschar is leathery, non-elastic, and cannot expand as edema accumulates underneath; pressure rises, compresses vessels, and produces a compartment-like syndrome. Escharotomy = vertical incision through the eschar to allow expansion. Time-critical to preserve the limb.

opts.text: Dark cola-colored urine with CK 12,400 — RHABDOMYOLYSIS with myoglobinuria
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Rhabdomyolysis is common with deep burns, electrical injury, crush, or prolonged immobilization. Myoglobin precipitates in renal tubules causing AKI. Treatment: aggressive saline-based hydration to target HIGHER UOP (75–100 mL/hr) than the standard burn target (30–50 mL/hr) to flush tubules. Avoid bicarbonate routinely — older practice with limited evidence.

opts.text: Hemoconcentration (Hgb 16.8, Hct 51) with hemodynamic instability — reflects massive intravascular volume depletion from capillary leak into burned tissue
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — explains the physiology. Capillary leak peaks in the first 24 hr, with maximum fluid shifts in the first 8 hr; Parkland delivers more volume in the first 8 hr to match the leak rate. Hemoconcentration normalizes as resuscitation progresses; an actually-low Hgb in the first 24 hr suggests hemorrhage from a separate trauma.

opts.text: Brief LOC at the fire scene (~2 min per fire department report) plus mental status changes (GCS 13 on arrival)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — strong indication for HYPERBARIC OXYGEN (HBO) therapy in some protocols. LOC at the scene OR any neurologic symptom OR CO-Hb >25% OR pregnancy with CO-Hb >15% are indications for HBO if available within 6 hr. HBO reduces CO half-life dramatically and is associated with reduced delayed neurologic sequelae.

opts.text: Weight 80 kg (used to calculate Parkland 4 × 80 × 45 = 14,400 mL LR over 24 hr from time of burn)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — required for resuscitation calculation. Time of BURN (0445), not time of arrival (0520), starts the clock. First 8 hr ends at 1245 — 7,200 mL by then. Each hour of delay risks 'fluid creep' (over-resuscitation that overshoots and causes pulmonary edema, abdominal compartment syndrome).

opts.text: Tetanus booster ~9 years ago
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — burns are TETANUS-PRONE wounds. Tdap booster if last dose >5 yr ago (general rule >10 yr; for tetanus-prone wounds >5 yr). Tetanus immune globulin (TIG) only if vaccine history unknown or incomplete. Easy to overlook in the chaos of a major burn — add it to the routine checklist.

opts.text: Chest x-ray clear bilateral lung fields
opts.rat: Initially reassuring but FALSELY so. Pulmonary injury from inhalation typically lags 24–48 hr behind the clinical/bronchoscopic injury. The clear CXR does NOT rule out inhalation injury — bronchoscopy is the gold standard, and it confirms the injury here. Expect ARDS-like picture to develop over the next 1–3 days.

opts.text: Glucose 168 mg/dL
opts.rat: Stress hyperglycemia is expected in major burns and is independently associated with worse outcomes. Insulin sliding scale to goal 140–180; not the immediate top priority but standard supportive care.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Inhalation injury has three components: (1) THERMAL injury to the UPPER airway (heat); (2) CHEMICAL injury to the LOWER airway from toxic combustion products; (3) SYSTEMIC toxicity from CO and cyanide. Each requires a different intervention.
opts.rat: CORRECT — the framework. Upper airway thermal injury demands early intubation BEFORE edema closes it. Lower airway chemical injury produces ARDS-like picture over 24–72 hr (no specific drug — supportive ventilation). Systemic CO/CN toxicity requires 100% O2 and possibly hydroxocobalamin / hyperbaric O2.

opts.text: Standard pulse oximetry uses two wavelengths and CANNOT distinguish carboxyhemoglobin from oxyhemoglobin — SpO2 reads FALSELY HIGH in CO poisoning; ORDER co-oximetry (multi-wavelength) or arterial blood gas with co-oximetry to measure actual CO-Hb
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield NCLEX trap. The PulseOx-lies concept is universally tested. The physiologically toxic state (severe hypoxia at the tissue level) can co-exist with a screen showing SpO2 100%. Trust the clinical picture (soot, hoarseness, stridor, neurologic symptoms, lactic acidosis) over the pulse ox.

opts.text: Cyanide blocks cytochrome c oxidase in the mitochondrial electron transport chain, halting aerobic metabolism — produces severe LACTIC ACIDOSIS, narrow arterio-venous O2 difference (oxygen is DELIVERED but not USED), and hemodynamic instability. HYDROXOCOBALAMIN 5 g IV is the preferred antidote — binds cyanide to form cyanocobalamin (vitamin B12, renally cleared), turns skin/urine RED, well tolerated.
opts.rat: CORRECT. Indications for empiric hydroxocobalamin in smoke inhalation: lactate >10 + acidosis + hemodynamic instability OR LOC at scene with severe symptoms. The older 'cyanide kit' (sodium nitrite + sodium thiosulfate) is AVOIDED in burn/smoke patients because nitrite-induced methemoglobinemia compounds CO impairment of oxygen delivery.

opts.text: PARKLAND formula: 4 mL × kg × %TBSA = total LR over 24 hr FROM TIME OF BURN; half over first 8 hr, half over next 16 hr. Titrate to UOP 30–50 mL/hr (0.5–1 mL/kg/hr) in adults; 75–100 mL/hr with myoglobinuria/rhabdo; 1 mL/kg/hr in children.
opts.rat: CORRECT — the burn fluid framework. The 'time of burn' starting point is essential — if the burn occurred 2 hr before arrival, the patient is already behind schedule. Titrate to UOP (the bedside marker of end-organ perfusion); do NOT chase the Parkland NUMBER if the patient is over- or under-resuscitating.

opts.text: FLUID CREEP is over-resuscitation — exceeding Parkland targets, often by titrating to nonexistent fluid goals (BP, mental status) — and causes pulmonary edema, ARDS worsening, ABDOMINAL COMPARTMENT SYNDROME (intra-abdominal hypertension → renal/respiratory failure), EXTREMITY compartment syndrome, and corneal edema. Titrate to UOP, not to BP.
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-yield modern concept. Earlier eras over-resuscitated; modern practice emphasizes 'permissive hypotension' early (allow MAP 60–65 as long as UOP and mental status are acceptable). Titrate DOWN if UOP exceeds the target. Abdominal compartment syndrome from massive fluids is a feared, often-fatal iatrogenic complication.

opts.text: Circumferential FULL-THICKNESS burns of the limbs or chest produce a compartment-like syndrome as edema accumulates under non-elastic eschar; escharotomy = vertical incision through the eschar (no general anesthesia needed — the eschar has no sensation) to allow expansion and restore perfusion (limbs) or ventilation (chest)
opts.rat: CORRECT — bedside or OR procedure. Limb indications: loss of distal pulse, decreased Doppler signal, severe pain on passive stretch, decreased sensation. Chest indications: worsening peak airway pressures on the ventilator with circumferential trunk burns. Performed at the medial and lateral midline of the limbs; T-shape for the trunk.

opts.text: Rhabdomyolysis from deep tissue burn injury releases myoglobin which precipitates in renal tubules causing AKI; treatment is aggressive saline-based hydration to push UOP up to 75–100 mL/hr until urine clears
opts.rat: CORRECT. Higher UOP target than standard burn resuscitation. Routine alkalinization with bicarbonate is no longer first-line (limited evidence). Mannitol is sometimes added for osmotic diuresis in severe rhabdo. CRRT may be needed if AKI severe and fluid balance not achievable.

opts.text: Prophylactic systemic antibiotics should be started in all burns >20% TBSA to prevent the inevitable wound infection
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — counterintuitive but true. Burns are initially STERILE from the heat. Routine prophylactic antibiotics select for resistant organisms and don't prevent infection — they DELAY accurate diagnosis when one develops. Surveillance wound cultures, clinical monitoring, and PROMPT empiric antibiotics for documented or strongly suspected infection is the modern approach.

opts.text: Burns should be cooled with ICE PACKS at the scene to reduce tissue damage and pain
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — ICE causes FROSTBITE injury and worsens tissue loss; in major burns it also causes excessive heat loss and hypothermia. Use room-temperature water briefly (1–2 min) if at all; in major burns, focus on removing clothing/jewelry, covering with clean dry sheets, and rapid transport.

opts.text: American Burn Association criteria for transfer to a burn center include: partial-thickness >10% TBSA, ANY full-thickness, burns of face/hands/feet/genitalia/joints/perineum, electrical/chemical, inhalation injury, comorbid trauma, special social/rehab needs. This patient meets MULTIPLE criteria.
opts.rat: CORRECT — the referral framework. If you are not a burn center, transfer EARLY (initial stabilization for transport: airway, fluids, pain, warm dry sheets). If you ARE the burn center (as in this scenario), the criteria still serve as the documentation framework for what makes this a 'major' burn requiring specialized care.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Airway Obstruction r/t inhalation injury with progressive supraglottic edema (singed nares, soot below cords, stridor, hoarseness)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Airway. The airway is closing. EARLY ELECTIVE INTUBATION before swelling makes intubation impossible. Awake fiberoptic by anesthesia for anticipated difficult airway. Once intubated, secure the tube carefully — re-intubation after dislodgement may be impossible.

opts.text: Risk for Cellular Hypoxia r/t carboxyhemoglobin 32% AND suspected cyanide toxicity (lactate 11.4 + AG acidosis)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Oxygen delivery and utilization both impaired. 100% FiO2 to clear CO; hydroxocobalamin 5 g IV for cyanide; hyperbaric O2 if available within 6 hr. CO-Hb is measured by co-oximetry (NOT standard pulse ox); cyanide is treated empirically when lactate and acidosis are severe (no immediately available bedside cyanide level).

opts.text: Risk for Hypovolemic Shock r/t massive capillary leak from 45% TBSA burns (hemoconcentration, BP 96/58, HR 138)
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Circulation. Parkland fluid resuscitation: 14,400 mL LR over 24 hr from time of burn; ~900 mL/hr in the first 8 hr; titrate to UOP 30–50 (75–100 with rhabdo). Two large-bore IVs through unburned skin if possible; central line if needed. AVOID over-resuscitation (fluid creep).

opts.text: Risk for Limb Loss r/t circumferential full-thickness burn of right arm with loss of distal pulse
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. ESCHAROTOMY at the bedside or OR — vertical incisions along medial and lateral midline through the eschar to allow expansion. Time-critical to preserve the limb. Burn surgery performing this concurrently with the resuscitation.

opts.text: Risk for AKI r/t rhabdomyolysis (CK 12,400, dark urine) + intravascular volume depletion
opts.rat: #5 — Real but actively addressed by the resuscitation already underway. ESCALATE UOP target to 75–100 mL/hr until urine clears; monitor CK q4h; CRRT escalation if AKI develops despite hydration. Avoid contrast and nephrotoxins. The same fluids that resuscitate the burn also flush the kidneys.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: EARLY ELECTIVE INTUBATION by anesthesia using awake fiberoptic technique (anticipated difficult airway from inhalation injury and facial burns)
opts.rat: Time-critical. Intubate BEFORE the airway closes, not when it has. Awake fiberoptic preserves spontaneous ventilation during a difficult airway scenario. Once intubated, secure the tube CAREFULLY — re-intubation after dislodgement may be impossible in severely edematous airway.

opts.text: 100% FiO2 mechanical ventilation; titrate FiO2 down only after CO-Hb <5%
opts.rat: Reduces CO half-life from 4–6 hr (room air) to ~60–80 min. Lung-protective ventilation (TV 6 mL/kg IBW, PEEP 5, plateau pressures <30) anticipating ARDS development over 24–72 hr.

opts.text: HYDROXOCOBALAMIN 5 g IV over 15 min for empiric cyanide toxicity (lactate 11.4 + smoke inhalation + acidosis + hemodynamic instability)
opts.rat: Standard cyanide antidote in burn/smoke patients. Empiric administration based on clinical picture; no need to wait for cyanide levels (which take hours). Expected side effects: red skin/urine (cyanocobalamin = vitamin B12 derivative), transient HTN, lab interference with co-oximetry and urine catecholamines.

opts.text: Parkland resuscitation: Lactated Ringer's 4 mL × 80 kg × 45 = 14,400 mL over 24 hr from time of burn; first 7,200 mL by 1245 (~900 mL/hr); titrate to UOP 30–50 mL/hr (75–100 with rhabdo)
opts.rat: Core burn resuscitation. LR is preferred (balanced crystalloid; avoids hyperchloremic acidosis of large-volume NS). Titrate to UOP, not to BP — under- AND over-resuscitation both cause harm.

opts.text: ESCHAROTOMY of right arm at bedside (loss of radial pulse, circumferential FT burn)
opts.rat: Limb-saving procedure. Vertical incisions along the medial and lateral midline through the eschar to allow swelling. No anesthesia needed for the eschar itself (insensate); cover with sterile dressings post-procedure; reassess perfusion.

opts.text: Foley catheter for strict hourly UOP monitoring; escalate UOP target to 75–100 mL/hr due to myoglobinuria/rhabdo
opts.rat: UOP is the bedside resuscitation gauge AND the renal protection metric in rhabdo. Higher target flushes myoglobin from the tubules. Avoid the false reassurance of 'normal' BP — UOP is more sensitive to end-organ perfusion.

opts.text: NG/OG tube for gastric decompression; NPO initially; advance enteral nutrition within 24–48 hr (early enteral feeding reduces gut translocation and improves outcomes in major burns)
opts.rat: Gastric ileus is common in burns >20% TBSA. Early enteral nutrition (typically by Day 2) is associated with lower infection rates and better outcomes than delayed feeding. Protein needs are very high in burn patients (1.5–2 g/kg/day).

opts.text: Tdap booster IM (last dose ~9 yr ago — burns are tetanus-prone wounds)
opts.rat: Routine but easy to overlook. Tdap if last booster >5 yr for tetanus-prone wounds (vs the general 10-yr rule). TIG only if vaccine history unknown or incomplete. Add to the resuscitation checklist.

opts.text: Prophylactic systemic antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam) to prevent burn wound infection
opts.rat: ⚠️ Burns are STERILE from heat initially. Prophylactic systemic antibiotics select for resistant organisms and DELAY accurate diagnosis when infection develops. Reactive treatment based on cultures and clinical signs is the standard. Topical antimicrobials (silver sulfadiazine, mafenide) ARE applied to the wounds.

opts.text: Sodium nitrite + sodium thiosulfate (older cyanide kit) for the suspected cyanide toxicity
opts.rat: ⚠️ AVOID in burn/smoke patients. Nitrite-induced methemoglobinemia compounds the CO impairment of oxygen delivery — double impairment of oxygen carrying. Hydroxocobalamin is preferred in this context. The older kit is still used for non-smoke cyanide exposures.

opts.text: Ice packs over the burned areas for pain relief and to limit ongoing tissue damage
opts.rat: ⚠️ ICE causes frostbite injury, worsens tissue loss in burn beds, and produces excessive heat loss in major burns. Brief room-temperature water cooling (1–2 min) is acceptable for small burns; major burns should be covered with clean dry sheets and the patient kept WARM (warmed IV fluids, Bair Hugger) to prevent hypothermia which worsens outcomes.

opts.text: Pain control with IV fentanyl drip 50–100 mcg/hr; AVOID IM injections during the first 24–48 hr (variable absorption with fluid shifts and tissue edema)
opts.rat: IV continuous opioid is the gold standard for major burn pain. IM injections have unpredictable absorption with the massive fluid shifts and tissue edema of the early phase — historically, IM doses 'released' all at once when perfusion restored, with overdose risk. Ketamine adjunct is increasingly used (analgesia, dissociation, bronchodilation, minimal respiratory depression).

opts.text: DVT prophylaxis: bilateral SCDs immediately; pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SubQ daily) starting at 24+ hr if bleeding risk acceptable
opts.rat: Burn patients are at high VTE risk from immobility, inflammatory state, and central venous catheters. Mechanical prophylaxis starts on admission; pharmacologic in 24–48 hr after escharotomy and acceptance of bleeding risk.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: STOP the burning process if ongoing; PROTECT the AIRWAY — early elective intubation by anesthesia (awake fiberoptic for anticipated difficult airway); 100% FiO2; remove all clothing and jewelry from burned areas
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. ABC. The airway IS the priority — singed nares + soot + hoarseness + stridor + 45% TBSA = intubate NOW, not after collapse. 100% FiO2 begins clearing CO immediately. Clothing/jewelry continues to burn or constricts as edema develops — remove early.

opts.text: Two large-bore IVs through unburned skin (forearms preferred); start Lactated Ringer's per Parkland formula 14,400 mL over 24 hr from TIME OF BURN (~900 mL/hr first 8 hr); Foley for hourly UOP
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. Fluid resuscitation must run concurrently with airway management. The Parkland clock started at 0445 (burn time), not at arrival — this patient is already behind. Two IVs allow simultaneous resuscitation, opioid drip, and rescue medications.

opts.text: Send carboxyhemoglobin, lactate, ABG, electrolytes, CK, urine myoglobin, CBC, type and screen; HYDROXOCOBALAMIN 5 g IV for empiric cyanide treatment (lactate >10 + acidosis + smoke inhalation + instability)
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical. The labs guide ongoing management; hydroxocobalamin is given empirically because cyanide levels are not available at the bedside. Documenting the rationale (lactate >10 + clinical picture) in the chart supports the empiric use.

opts.text: Burn surgery for ESCHAROTOMY of right arm (loss of radial pulse, circumferential FT burn); cover wounds with clean dry sheets; rule of nines TBSA documentation; Tdap booster; NG/OG; pain control with IV fentanyl drip
opts.rat: #4 — Procedural and supportive. Escharotomy preserves the limb. NG/OG manages gastric ileus. Pain control is mandatory and humane. Tdap booster is a routine but easily forgotten step. Each of these is essential to complete burn care.

opts.text: Burn ICU admission; PT/OT consult Day 1; family meeting with social work and chaplaincy; ophthalmology for face burns; psychiatry for acute stress disorder/PTSD risk; long-term burn rehab planning
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management. Once the acute hour is managed, multidisciplinary care defines the long arc of recovery. Early PT/OT positioning and splinting prevents contractures; psychiatric support reduces long-term PTSD; family support and education is a months-to-years process.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Hour 4: CO-Hb down from 32% to 6% on 100% FiO2; lactate 4.2 (from 11.4) after hydroxocobalamin; UOP 60 mL/hr; right arm post-escharotomy with restored radial pulse and Doppler signal
opts.rat: Excellent multi-front response: CO clearing, cyanide reversing, perfusion restored, escharotomy successful. Continue current plan; transition FiO2 down once CO-Hb <5% confirmed; continue Parkland resuscitation; serial neuro checks for delayed CO sequelae.

opts.text: Day 7: extubated, tolerating soft diet, ambulating with PT, donor sites and grafts healing, no infection on surveillance cultures, ongoing IV opioids transitioning to PO, family meeting confirms transfer to acute rehab in 1 week
opts.rat: Successful trajectory. Most major-burn deaths occur in the first 48–72 hr from shock and inhalation injury or weeks later from sepsis. This patient is past the highest-risk window and entering the long-arc rehab phase. Months to a year of skin and rehabilitation care expected; psychiatric follow-up for PTSD risk.

opts.text: Day 30 in burn rehab: split-thickness skin grafts have taken well; physical therapy 2x daily; pain managed with multimodal regimen; psychiatry follow-up for adjustment disorder; family attending weekly support group at the burn center
opts.rat: Realistic recovery profile. Major burn survivors have months of rehabilitation, scar maturation, contracture management, and psychological recovery ahead. The family support and psychiatric attention are not optional — they predict long-term function and quality of life as much as the wound healing does.

opts.text: Hour 12: ventilator peak airway pressures climbing from 28 to 45 cm H2O; abdomen tense, distended; bladder pressure 28 mmHg (abdominal compartment syndrome); UOP falling despite IV fluids
opts.rat: FLUID CREEP / abdominal compartment syndrome — a feared complication of over-resuscitation. Decompress via NG; consider trunk escharotomy if circumferential; if bladder pressure stays elevated and end-organ dysfunction persists, may need surgical decompression (rare). Cut back on fluids — titrate DOWN to target UOP.

opts.text: Hour 36: temperature 102.4°F, leukocytosis 22 K with bands, hypotension, lactate climbing to 6.8, blood cultures growing GNR — early burn-wound sepsis
opts.rat: Burn-wound sepsis — major cause of late mortality. Empiric broad-spectrum coverage (piperacillin-tazobactam + vancomycin); wound cultures from each burn area; consider operative debridement and grafting earlier. Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter, MRSA, and Candida are common burn-wound pathogens; choose empirics based on local antibiogram and patient acuity.

opts.text: Hour 48: hypoxic respiratory failure with new bilateral infiltrates on CXR; P/F ratio 140; lung-protective ventilation maintained; sedation deepened; prone positioning initiated — ARDS from inhalation injury evolving
opts.rat: ARDS from inhalation injury — typical timing (24–72 hr after smoke exposure). Continue lung-protective ventilation (6 mL/kg IBW, PEEP titrated), consider prone positioning for P/F <150, conservative fluid strategy (within the constraints of burn resuscitation), neuromuscular blockade if needed. Mortality is significant.

opts.text: Day 10: stress ulcer with hematemesis 600 mL despite PPI; Hgb 7.4; melena overnight; endoscopy shows large gastric ulcer (Curling ulcer)
opts.rat: Curling ulcer is the burn-specific stress ulcer (Cushing ulcer is the head-injury equivalent). Hemodynamic stabilization with blood transfusion; endoscopic hemostasis; aggressive PPI infusion; H. pylori testing once stable. Prevention: PPI prophylaxis + early enteral feeding (which protects the gastric mucosa).

opts.text: Day 14: hypertonic 3% saline initiated for new hyponatremia of Na 124 with new fatigue and lethargy; recheck Na q4h
opts.rat: Burn-associated hyponatremia is common (from inflammation, SIADH-like physiology, dilutional from fluid resuscitation). Symptomatic hyponatremia (lethargy, mental status changes) at Na 124 warrants treatment. Treat slowly — overcorrection causes osmotic demyelination (case-041, case-042 cross-reference). Goal correction ≤8–10 mEq/L per 24 hr.

opts.text: Discharge home at Day 90: scar therapy ongoing, returned to part-time work, splints and pressure garments worn, follow-up at burn clinic weekly for scar maturation; psychiatric support for adjustment + PTSD; family integrated into the long-term plan
opts.rat: Realistic disposition profile. Major-burn rehabilitation is 1–2 years for full scar maturation and functional recovery. Pressure garments + scar massage + ongoing PT/OT preserve range of motion and reduce hypertrophic scarring. Psychiatric support is essential — burn survivors have very high rates of PTSD, depression, body image concerns, and substance use disorders.

opts.text: Day 21 follow-up neuropsych testing: mild memory and executive function deficits — likely delayed neurologic sequelae of CO poisoning + critical illness
opts.rat: Delayed neuropsychiatric sequelae of CO poisoning occur 2–40 days after exposure in ~10–40% of survivors of severe CO toxicity, especially those who had LOC at the scene. Cognitive rehabilitation, ongoing neuropsych testing, hyperbaric O2 (if late HBO available — controversial), and patient/family education are the mainstays. May resolve over months but some have permanent deficits.

title: Sickle Cell Vaso-Occlusive Crisis with Acute Chest Syndrome
subtitle: Conservative Fluids, Around-the-Clock Multimodal Analgesia, and the Bias Trap

patient.name: D. Washington
patient.room: Pediatric Hematology Unit Bed 6 → PICU pending transfer

vitals.time: Hematology clinic — 3 weeks ago (well visit, baseline)
0210 (ED arrival — Day 3 of pain crisis at home)
0540 (CURRENT — 3.5 hr in ED, on 4 L NC + opioid infusion + 1st antibiotic dose)

labs.r: Sickle cell baseline 7–9 typical (chronic hemolytic anemia); ≥2 g/dL drop from baseline = significant acute hemolysis
Healthy bone marrow response to anemia; 1–2% normal; >4% appropriate in chronic hemolytic anemia; LOW retic with worsening Hgb suggests aplastic crisis (parvovirus B19)
Sickle cell baseline mildly elevated due to chronic inflammation; >15 K with bandemia in fever raises bacterial infection concern
Often elevated at baseline (functional asplenia from auto-infarction); 150–400 K
<280 U/L; markedly elevated reflects active hemolysis

labs.n: Hemoglobin S percentage on hemoglobin electrophoresis
labs.v: HbS 88%, HbF 8%, HbA2 3% (HbSS phenotype confirmed at prior visit; HbA 0%)
labs.r: Healthy: HbA >95%, HbA2 <3%, HbF <2%; this patient has homozygous sickle cell disease

labs.v: NEW right lower lobe consolidation/infiltrate; clear left lung; no pneumothorax; cardiac silhouette normal
labs.r: Clear lung fields — the NEW pulmonary infiltrate is the diagnostic criterion for Acute Chest Syndrome

labs.v: Right lower lobe B-lines and small consolidation; left lung A-lines (clear)
labs.r: Bilateral A-lines; no B-lines or consolidation

labs.v: MEETS all 5: (1) NEW radiographic pulmonary density; (2) fever ≥38.5°C (102.4°F here); (3) chest pain; (4) cough/wheeze/dyspnea (tachypnea + cough + splinting); (5) hypoxia (SpO2 88% RA from baseline 96%, ≥4% decrease)
labs.r: Any new pulmonary infiltrate + respiratory symptoms in a sickle cell patient = treat AS Acute Chest Syndrome until proven otherwise

labs.v: Blood cultures x2 drawn before first antibiotic; respiratory viral panel + COVID-19 + influenza pending
labs.r: Both viral and bacterial infections can trigger ACS; common bacterial: S. pneumoniae, Mycoplasma, Chlamydia; viral: RSV, influenza, COVID

labs.v: pH 7.40 / PaCO2 36 / PaO2 78 / HCO3 23 — adequate ventilation but borderline oxygenation despite supplemental O2
Self-reported pain 9/10 in bilateral chest/back/upper extremities; described as 'crushing, stabbing, all over,' worsened by deep breath; splinting on exam
labs.r: Trust the patient's self-report; pain in sickle cell is chronically undertreated and the actual experience is severe

labs.v: Patient attended a high-school football game 2 days ago in cold rainy weather (~50°F, sat outside for 3 hr in wet clothes); did not drink water that day; runny nose started the day after
labs.r: Triggers for sickling include: cold, dehydration, infection (often viral URI), hypoxia, stress, acidosis

labs.v: Hydroxyurea 30 mg/kg/day PO (last dose 36 hr ago — 'I forgot to bring my pill bottle to the game'); penicillin V 250 mg PO BID prophylaxis discontinued at age 5 per current guidelines; folic acid 1 mg PO daily
labs.r: Hydroxyurea adherence is the single most modifiable factor in sickle cell outcomes — gap in adherence often precedes crisis

labs.v: No prior splenic sequestration history; no signs of stroke (neuro exam intact); no priapism; no pulmonary embolism signs other than the dyspnea (no unilateral leg swelling); no signs of meningitis (no neck stiffness, no rash); no signs of cholecystitis (no RUQ tenderness — important in sickle cell with pigment gallstones)
labs.r: Sickle cell crisis can present similarly to other emergencies — careful exclusion

nursesNote: 0540 — 16 y/o male with homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS, confirmed by electrophoresis at diagnosis in infancy) followed in our pediatric hematology clinic, baseline Hgb 8.4. Brought in by mother at 0210 after 3 days of escalating bilateral chest, back, and upper extremity pain at home that initially seemed like his 'usual' pain crisis but worsened today with new fever, cough, and dyspnea. The trigger this time was clear in retrospect: he attended a high-school football game 2 days ago in cold rainy weather (~50°F), sat outside for 3 hr in wet clothes, did not drink any water all day, and developed a runny nose the next day. He had NOT taken his hydroxyurea (30 mg/kg/day) for the previous 36 hr because he 'forgot the pill bottle' for the trip. On arrival: lethargic but arousable, splinting on chest examination, RR 28 shallow, audible cough, mother reports his lips were blue when he got out of the car. SpO2 88% on RA (baseline 96%) — a 4-point drop meeting hypoxia criterion for ACS. Temperature 102.4°F. CXR at 0240 shows NEW right lower lobe consolidation/infiltrate (left lung clear). Hgb 6.8 (baseline 8.4 — 1.6 g/dL drop), retic appropriately elevated at 12%, LDH 850, indirect bilirubin 3.8. ACS diagnostic criteria MET (new infiltrate + fever + chest pain + cough/dyspnea + hypoxia). BLOOD AND RESPIRATORY CULTURES drawn before antibiotics; empiric ceftriaxone 75 mg/kg IV q24h + azithromycin 10 mg/kg PO/IV daily started by 0320 for community-acquired pneumonia coverage (S. pneumoniae, Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, atypicals all common ACS triggers in this age group). 4 L NC oxygen titrating SpO2 ≥95%. PAIN management is the critical priority: morphine 0.15 mg/kg = 9 mg IV given at 0245 with hydromorphone 0.02 mg/kg = 1.2 mg IV at 0335 for breakthrough; ketorolac 30 mg IV at 0245 for adjunctive non-opioid effect; transitioned to morphine PCA at 0400 with continuous basal 1 mg/hr + 0.5 mg q10min demand (per pediatric pain protocol). MAINTENANCE IV fluids: D5 ½NS at 1.25x maintenance (~125 mL/hr) — NO boluses (fluid overload can worsen acute chest syndrome by increasing pulmonary capillary pressure and exacerbating pulmonary edema). Bedside incentive spirometry q1h while awake. Bedside warming with blankets (cold worsens vaso-occlusion). Hematology consultation: simple TRANSFUSION 1 unit PRBC (leukoreduced, sickle-negative, antigen-matched for C, E, K to reduce alloimmunization) being prepared with goal post-transfusion Hgb 9–10 g/dL (NOT >11 — hyperviscosity from rapid Hgb elevation can worsen ACS); EXCHANGE TRANSFUSION via apheresis is on standby if Hgb drops further, ACS worsens, or neurologic symptoms develop. Continuous cardiac monitoring, continuous pulse oximetry, EtCO2; PICU bed being prepared. Mother is exhausted but appropriately anxious; younger brother (age 8, also HbSS but well) being cared for by aunt. The bedside nurse has been EXPLICITLY ADVOCATING for adequate analgesia — patient/family report multiple ED visits in the past where pain was undertreated and they were perceived as 'drug-seeking,' a documented and shameful pattern in sickle cell care that the team is committed to correcting.
providerOrders: Acute Chest Syndrome protocol activated; PICU admission for monitoring

Oxygen titrated to SpO2 ≥95% (4 L NC currently; escalate to NRB or HFNC if needed); AVOID supplemental O2 once SpO2 stable (excessive supplemental O2 suppresses erythropoietin and reticulocyte response in chronic anemia)

EMPIRIC ANTIBIOTICS within 1 hr: ceftriaxone 75 mg/kg IV q24h (max 2 g/dose) + azithromycin 10 mg/kg PO/IV daily for community-acquired pneumonia coverage including atypicals (S. pneumoniae, Mycoplasma, Chlamydia); add vancomycin if MRSA suspected
Blood cultures x2 BEFORE antibiotics (already done); respiratory viral panel + COVID-19 + influenza testing

AROUND-THE-CLOCK MULTIMODAL ANALGESIA — start scheduled, NOT PRN: (1) Morphine PCA basal 1 mg/hr + demand 0.5 mg q10 min, escalate per pain response; (2) Ketorolac 0.5 mg/kg IV q6h x 5 days max (renal function monitoring); (3) Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO/IV q6h scheduled; (4) Heat packs to non-chest areas; (5) Distraction, child life

Document pain assessments q1h initially using age-appropriate scale; TRUST the patient's self-report; SICKLE CELL PAIN IS DOCUMENTED TO BE UNDERTREATED due to clinician bias and concerns about opioid use — the goal is adequate pain control, NOT minimum opioid exposure

MAINTENANCE IV FLUIDS only: D5 ½NS at 1.0–1.5x maintenance (this patient ~125 mL/hr); DO NOT BOLUS — fluid overload can WORSEN ACS by increasing pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure and exacerbating pulmonary edema in already-vulnerable pulmonary microcirculation
Incentive spirometry q1h while awake (10 deep breaths × 4 cycles) — reduces atelectasis and prevents progression of pulmonary involvement
Bedside warming with blankets and warm IV fluids; AVOID cold exposure (cold worsens vaso-occlusion); avoid ice packs to painful areas

Hematology consultation: SIMPLE TRANSFUSION 1 unit leukoreduced, sickle-negative, antigen-matched (C, E, K) PRBC over 3 hr — goal post-transfusion Hgb 9–10 g/dL; AVOID rapid Hgb elevation >11 (hyperviscosity worsens ACS)

EXCHANGE TRANSFUSION (via apheresis) on standby — indicated if: Hgb continues falling, ACS progresses (worsening hypoxia, new infiltrates), neurologic symptoms develop, or simple transfusion fails to improve oxygenation
Continuous cardiac monitoring, continuous pulse oximetry, EtCO2; arterial line if vasopressor-dependent or worsening respiratory failure

DVT prophylaxis: bilateral SCDs immediately; pharmacologic (enoxaparin 40 mg SubQ daily) starting at 24 hr if bleeding risk acceptable (sickle cell is procoagulant; especially with immobility and central lines)
Bronchodilator (albuterol 2.5 mg neb q4h) if wheezing is present; some ACS patients have a wheezing component that responds
NPO until acute respiratory distress improves and pain controlled enough to safely swallow — then advance diet as tolerated
Strict I&O hourly; daily weights; recheck CBC q12h initially, electrolytes q6h, lactate, retic count, LDH, bilirubin
RESUME hydroxyurea once oral intake tolerated (do NOT discontinue chronic disease-modifying therapy unless severe BMS); home dose 30 mg/kg/day; counsel on adherence
Pneumococcal and meningococcal vaccine status verified up-to-date (functional asplenia in sickle cell mandates expanded vaccine schedule); influenza vaccine annually

Discharge planning: hematology follow-up in 1 week; case management for HU adherence support, possible smartphone reminders, structured medication refill; school nurse coordination for hydration support during sports/cold weather

Long-term planning: chronic transfusion vs hydroxyurea optimization vs evaluation for hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT — only curative therapy, increasingly considered for severe disease); gene therapy options (lovotibeglogene autotemcel, exagamglogene autotemcel) for highly selected patients

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Full ACS diagnostic criteria MET: new RLL infiltrate on CXR + fever 102.4°F + chest pain + cough/dyspnea + hypoxia (SpO2 88% from baseline 96%)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Acute Chest Syndrome is the LEADING CAUSE OF DEATH in sickle cell disease. The 5-criterion clinical definition (new infiltrate + fever + chest pain + respiratory symptoms + hypoxia) is the bedside diagnosis. Treat AS ACS even if only some criteria are met — early aggressive treatment improves outcomes.

opts.text: Hgb 6.8 (baseline 8.4) with reticulocyte count appropriately elevated at 12% and LDH 850 + indirect bilirubin 3.8 — acute hemolysis from active sickling
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — significant acute hemolysis (≥2 g/dL drop from baseline is a marker of severe crisis). The appropriately elevated reticulocyte count is reassuring — it means the bone marrow is responding. A LOW retic with worsening anemia would raise concern for APLASTIC CRISIS (often parvovirus B19) — a different emergency.

opts.text: Pain 9/10 in bilateral chest, back, and upper extremities described as 'crushing, stabbing'
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — vaso-occlusive crisis pain is severe and is one of the defining features of sickle cell disease. TRUST the patient's self-report; pain in sickle cell is DOCUMENTED to be undertreated due to clinician bias. Aggressive multimodal around-the-clock analgesia is the standard of care.

opts.text: Pain has been present 3 days and his hydroxyurea has been MISSED for the previous 36 hr (forgot pill bottle on a trip)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — identifies the PRECIPITATING factor (in part). Hydroxyurea is the most effective disease-modifying therapy for HbSS — it raises fetal hemoglobin (HbF), reduces sickling, and reduces crisis frequency by ~50%. Missed doses are the most common modifiable risk factor for crisis. Resume as soon as oral intake tolerated.

opts.text: Cold/wet exposure at football game 2 days ago + dehydration (no water all day) + new viral URI = a multi-trigger setup for sickling
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — IDENTIFIES the trigger constellation. Cold causes vasoconstriction → sickling; dehydration concentrates HbS; infection adds inflammation and acidosis. Each trigger alone can precipitate a crisis; together they are nearly guaranteed to. Patient education to avoid this combination is the highest-leverage prevention intervention.

opts.text: Tachypnea 28/min with shallow splinted breathing pattern and audible cough
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pulmonary involvement. The splinting from chest pain produces atelectasis; atelectasis worsens V/Q matching and oxygenation; hypoxia drives more sickling in the pulmonary microcirculation; the cycle compounds. Incentive spirometry q1h is one of the strongest evidence-based interventions to interrupt this cycle.

opts.text: Temperature 102.4°F with WBC 18 K and 12% bands (left shift)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — fever in sickle cell is always concerning. Functional asplenia (auto-infarction of spleen by ~age 4 in HbSS) leaves these patients vulnerable to encapsulated organisms (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, N. meningitidis, Salmonella). Empiric antibiotics within 1 hour for any sickle cell patient with fever — full sepsis workup as well.

opts.text: Hgb electrophoresis HbS 88%, HbF 8%, HbA 0% — homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — establishes the diagnosis and severity. HbSS is the most severe sickle cell genotype. Higher HbF percentages are protective (hydroxyurea raises HbF). Patients with HbSC or sickle-beta+ thalassemia have milder disease courses. The percent HbS is the variable that determines exchange-transfusion targets.

opts.text: ABG: pH 7.40 / PaCO2 36 / PaO2 78 on 4 L NC — borderline oxygenation despite supplemental O2
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms inadequate gas exchange. A PaO2 78 on 4 L NC means the alveolar-arterial gradient is widening; ACS can progress to ARDS-like physiology. Escalation to NRB, HFNC, BiPAP, or intubation may be needed. Exchange transfusion is increasingly considered as oxygenation worsens.

opts.text: Mother reports patient/family have experienced undertreatment of pain in past ED visits and being perceived as 'drug-seeking'
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a SYSTEMS-LEVEL concern, not a clinical lab value, but central to good sickle cell care. Multiple studies document that sickle cell patients (predominantly Black in the US) are undertreated for pain compared to other groups. Nursing leadership to advocate for adequate around-the-clock multimodal analgesia is part of the standard of care.

opts.text: Platelets 385 K/µL
opts.rat: Mildly elevated but expected — functional asplenia from chronic auto-infarction means the patient doesn't 'sequester' platelets normally, so baseline platelet counts run mildly high. Not actionable.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring (no medication restrictions) but not a 'priority cue' for the acute management. Document and proceed.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: Under stressors (cold, dehydration, hypoxia, infection, acidosis), HbS polymerizes into rigid fibers within red cells; the sickled RBCs are inflexible, occlude small vessels, lyse prematurely, and trigger inflammation — producing pain (microvascular ischemia), hemolytic anemia (premature lysis), and end-organ damage
opts.rat: CORRECT — the central mechanism. The 'vaso-occlusive crisis' is a downstream consequence of HbS polymerization. Hydroxyurea raises fetal hemoglobin (HbF) which does NOT participate in the polymerization, thereby reducing sickling. Newer therapies (voxelotor, crizanlizumab, gene therapies, allogeneic HSCT) target additional steps in this pathway.

opts.text: ACUTE CHEST SYNDROME (ACS) is the leading cause of mortality in sickle cell disease. Triggers include pulmonary infection (S. pneumoniae, Mycoplasma, viruses), fat embolism from bone marrow infarction during VOC, pulmonary infarction from sickled cells, hypoventilation from pain-related splinting. Treatment: oxygen, antibiotics, multimodal pain control, INCENTIVE SPIROMETRY, conservative IV fluids, transfusion as needed (simple vs exchange).
opts.rat: CORRECT — comprehensive framework. The multiple possible triggers explain why empiric broad treatment (antibiotics + pain control + spirometry + cautious fluids ± transfusion) is the standard. About 50% of ACS cases follow a VOC by a few days (as bone marrow fat embolism settles in the pulmonary microcirculation) — exactly this patient's timeline.

opts.text: FLUID MANAGEMENT IS COUNTERINTUITIVE: maintenance ONLY (1.0–1.5x), NOT boluses. Aggressive fluid resuscitation can WORSEN ACS by increasing pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure and overwhelming already-compromised pulmonary microcirculation, producing pulmonary edema.
opts.rat: CORRECT and HIGH-YIELD. The reflex to give a fluid bolus for tachycardia or borderline BP in sickle cell is WRONG. Dehydration treatment uses MAINTENANCE rate, not boluses. Watch for pulmonary edema; consider gentle diuresis with furosemide if fluid overload develops. Vasopressor support for refractory hypotension is preferred over bolus fluid.

opts.text: SUPPLEMENTAL OXYGEN should be titrated to SpO2 ≥95%; EXCESSIVE supplemental O2 in chronic anemia suppresses erythropoietin and the reticulocyte response, potentially slowing recovery; once SpO2 stable on RA, wean the supplemental O2
opts.rat: CORRECT — a nuance many nurses miss. Hypoxia triggers sickling, so adequate oxygenation matters; but excessive supplemental O2 over the long term blunts the marrow response in a population dependent on robust reticulocytosis. The 'as much O2 as it takes' instinct is wrong — target normoxia, not hyperoxia.

opts.text: Pain in sickle cell is documented to be undertreated due to clinician bias and unwarranted concerns about opioid-seeking behavior; patients (predominantly Black in the US) report being perceived as drug-seeking. NURSING LEADERSHIP responsibility includes advocating for adequate around-the-clock multimodal analgesia — start scheduled, not PRN; use PCAs liberally; use multimodal adjuncts (ketorolac, acetaminophen, ketamine, heat); document pain response.
opts.rat: CORRECT — a critical systems-level lesson. Multiple peer-reviewed studies show sickle cell patients wait longer in EDs, receive less opioid, and are more likely to be labeled drug-seeking compared to demographically and clinically similar patients with other pain conditions. The undertreatment is the deviation from standard, not the patient's request for analgesia. Speak up.

opts.text: Transfusion strategy: SIMPLE transfusion (1–2 units PRBC) to goal Hgb 9–10 g/dL is used for moderate ACS and significant hemolytic anemia drop. EXCHANGE TRANSFUSION (via apheresis) is used for severe/progressive ACS, neurologic symptoms, multi-organ involvement, or failed simple transfusion. AVOID overshooting Hgb >11 (hyperviscosity worsens sickling and ACS).
opts.rat: CORRECT — transfusion decision-tree. Simple transfusion raises Hgb but does NOT lower HbS percentage; exchange transfusion reduces HbS to ~30% by replacing sickled cells with normal donor cells. Antigen matching (C, E, K) reduces alloimmunization in a chronically transfused population. Sickle-negative, leukoreduced units.

opts.text: Sickle cell pain should be managed with limited PRN doses to avoid opioid tolerance and addiction risk; if the patient requests medications frequently, consider that drug-seeking is more likely than true pain
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT AND HARMFUL — and exactly the bias that produces undertreatment. Frequent opioid requests in vaso-occlusive crisis reflect SEVERE PAIN, not drug-seeking. Chronic exposure does build tolerance, which means higher doses are NEEDED for adequate control. Around-the-clock dosing with PCA is the standard. Addiction risk in sickle cell patients receiving acute crisis treatment is NOT elevated.

opts.text: Aggressive 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus is the appropriate fluid resuscitation for hypotension or tachycardia in sickle cell crisis
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT and DANGEROUS in sickle cell. The 30 mL/kg bolus is sepsis dogma; in sickle cell it can WORSEN ACS by overwhelming pulmonary microcirculation. Treat tachycardia and pain together; treat hypotension with cautious smaller fluid challenges and consider early vasopressor support. Maintenance rate of 1.0–1.5x is the standard.

opts.text: Continuous high-flow supplemental oxygen at 100% should be maintained for the duration of admission to prevent further sickling
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT. Oxygen titrate to SpO2 ≥95%; excessive supplemental O2 in chronic anemia suppresses reticulocyte response. The goal is normoxia, not hyperoxia. Wean to room air as the ACS resolves.

opts.text: Hydroxyurea is a disease-modifying therapy that raises fetal hemoglobin (HbF) and reduces sickling; missed doses are a common precipitant of crisis; HU should be resumed during admission as soon as oral intake tolerated
opts.rat: CORRECT. Adherence is the single most modifiable factor in sickle cell outcomes. Hydroxyurea's effects build over weeks-months; the protective effect comes from sustained therapy. Don't discontinue HU during admission unless severe bone marrow suppression — resume as the patient recovers.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Impaired Gas Exchange r/t Acute Chest Syndrome (new infiltrate, SpO2 88% on RA, PaO2 78 on 4 L NC) — leading cause of mortality in sickle cell disease
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Breathing. ACS can progress rapidly from bilateral pneumonia-like physiology to ARDS-like respiratory failure. Aggressive bundled treatment (O2, empiric antibiotics, transfusion, incentive spirometry, pain control to enable deep breathing, conservative fluids) reduces mortality. Exchange transfusion if progression continues.

opts.text: Acute Pain r/t vaso-occlusive crisis (chest, back, upper extremities 9/10) — the most-undertreated pain in modern medicine
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Pain. Severe pain causes splinting → atelectasis → worsening ACS. Effective pain control IS part of the ACS treatment (enables deep breathing, incentive spirometry, mobilization). Around-the-clock multimodal analgesia with PCA, ketorolac, acetaminophen, ketamine adjuncts. Document pain response; advocate against undertreatment.

opts.text: Risk for Sepsis r/t fever 102.4°F with functional asplenia (encapsulated organism vulnerability) + community-acquired pneumonia possibility
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Empiric antibiotics within 1 hr for any sickle cell patient with fever — functional asplenia leaves them vulnerable to encapsulated organisms (S. pneumoniae especially). Blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics. Watch for septic-shock physiology; vasopressor support if MAP <65 after MAINTENANCE fluids (NOT boluses).

opts.text: Risk for Worsening Hemolytic Anemia / Need for Transfusion r/t acute hemolysis (Hgb 6.8 from baseline 8.4)
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Simple transfusion 1 unit PRBC to goal Hgb 9–10 (NOT >11 — hyperviscosity worsens ACS). Antigen-matched (C, E, K), leukoreduced, sickle-negative units. Exchange transfusion if progression continues. Watch for delayed hemolytic transfusion reactions in this chronically transfused population.

opts.text: Knowledge Deficit r/t hydroxyurea adherence and crisis triggers (cold/dehydration/infection clustering) — modifiable factors for future crisis prevention
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in acute ranking but THE preventive factor for the next crisis. Hydroxyurea adherence support: pill organizer, smartphone reminder, structured medication refill, school nurse coordination for sports/cold weather hydration. Patient and mother understand triggers but need actionable plans. Case management referral, hematology clinic Q3 month routine.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Supplemental O2 titrated to SpO2 ≥95% (currently 4 L NC, escalate to NRB or HFNC if needed); wean once stable
opts.rat: Targeted oxygenation to prevent further sickling without overshooting. Reassess frequently. NRB if 4 L NC insufficient; HFNC for higher FiO2 needs; BiPAP or intubation if respiratory failure progresses. Avoid supraphysiologic hyperoxia — it blunts the marrow response in chronic anemia.

opts.text: Empiric antibiotics within 1 hr: ceftriaxone 75 mg/kg IV q24h + azithromycin 10 mg/kg IV daily (CAP coverage including atypicals)
opts.rat: Both ACS protocol (pulmonary infection is a common trigger) AND sepsis-in-fever-with-functional-asplenia. Blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics. Add vancomycin if MRSA concern; broaden if nosocomial exposure or severe immunocompromise.

opts.text: AROUND-THE-CLOCK multimodal analgesia: morphine PCA basal 1 mg/hr + demand 0.5 mg q10min; ketorolac 0.5 mg/kg IV q6h; acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO/IV q6h scheduled; ketamine 0.1–0.3 mg/kg/hr IV as adjunct if pain refractory
opts.rat: Standard sickle cell crisis pain management. PCA gives patient control and reduces dosing delays. Ketorolac adds NSAID effect (monitor renal function with sickle nephropathy in mind). Acetaminophen scheduled, not PRN. Ketamine for refractory pain (analgesia + dissociation, opioid-sparing).

opts.text: Maintenance IVF: D5 ½NS at 1.25x maintenance (~125 mL/hr) — NO boluses
opts.rat: Conservative fluid strategy. The dextrose helps prevent ketosis in NPO state. The 1.25x maintenance rate addresses ongoing dehydration without overwhelming the pulmonary circulation. Reassess frequently; reduce to 1x or below if any signs of pulmonary edema.

opts.text: Incentive spirometry q1h while awake (10 deep breaths x 4 cycles); bedside warming with blankets
opts.rat: Strong evidence-based intervention. IS reduces atelectasis and the cycle of pain-splinting → atelectasis → V/Q mismatch → hypoxia → more sickling. Warm environment prevents cold-induced vasoconstriction. Simple, non-medication interventions with large impact.

opts.text: Simple transfusion 1 unit PRBC (leukoreduced, sickle-negative, antigen-matched C, E, K) over 3 hr to goal Hgb 9–10 (NOT >11)
opts.rat: Indicated for significant hemolytic anemia (Hgb drop ≥2 g/dL from baseline) with ACS. The Hgb ceiling 9–10 prevents hyperviscosity from rapid elevation. Antigen matching (C, E, K) reduces alloimmunization in this chronically transfused population. Have exchange transfusion ready if ACS progresses.

opts.text: Exchange transfusion via apheresis if Hgb continues falling, ACS progresses, or neurologic symptoms develop
opts.rat: Standby intervention. Exchange transfusion reduces HbS percentage to ~30% by physically replacing sickled cells; reserved for severe disease. Requires apheresis catheter and team; coordinate with blood bank for adequate units (typically 8–10 units).

opts.text: 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus IV stat for the tachycardia and borderline BP
opts.rat: ⚠️ AVOID in sickle cell with ACS. The aggressive bolus can WORSEN ACS by increasing pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure and exacerbating pulmonary edema. Maintenance rate (1.0–1.5x) is the correct fluid approach. For refractory hypotension, consider vasopressor over additional bolus.

opts.text: PRN morphine 2 mg IV q3-4h with no PCA — minimize opioid exposure due to drug-seeking concerns
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT AND HARMFUL — the documented undertreatment pattern. PRN dosing requires the patient to call for each dose and creates delays; AROUND-THE-CLOCK scheduled dosing or PCA is standard. The 'minimize opioid' instinct in sickle cell crisis is the bias that produces poor outcomes. The right call is adequate around-the-clock multimodal analgesia.

opts.text: Ice packs to the chest for pain relief
opts.rat: ⚠️ CONTRAINDICATED. Cold causes vasoconstriction, which WORSENS sickling. Use warm packs (carefully, to avoid burns), keep the patient warm with blankets, and use heated IVF if needed. Cold environments and ice packs are documented triggers for crisis.

opts.text: Discontinue hydroxyurea during the admission to avoid bone marrow suppression
opts.rat: Inappropriate routine discontinuation. HU is disease-modifying and should be continued unless there is severe bone marrow suppression (severely low ANC or platelets). Resume oral HU as soon as the patient tolerates PO; arrange for adherence support post-discharge. Discontinuing chronic HU contributes to crisis recurrence.

opts.text: Verify pneumococcal and meningococcal vaccine status; arrange hematology follow-up in 1 week; case management for HU adherence support; school nurse coordination
opts.rat: Comprehensive prevention bundle. Functional asplenia mandates expanded vaccine schedule (PPSV23, PCV20, MenACWY, MenB, influenza annually). Adherence support is the highest-leverage intervention to reduce future crisis frequency. School nurse coordination addresses the cold-weather sports trigger that produced this admission.

opts.text: Document and respond to family report of past undertreatment: clear pain plan in chart, scheduled multimodal analgesia, no PRN-only orders, advocate for the patient when shift change happens
opts.rat: Nursing leadership essential. The family's experience of past undertreatment is real and shapes their trust in the team. Documenting the current adequate plan, communicating it across shifts, and addressing any deviations is part of the standard of care. This is a leadership-level intervention that improves outcomes.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Apply O2 to SpO2 ≥95% (start 4 L NC); blood cultures x2 BEFORE antibiotics; administer empiric ceftriaxone + azithromycin within 1 hr; obtain stat CXR
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Oxygenation + early diagnostics + time-critical antibiotics. The 1-hr antibiotic rule applies to ANY sickle cell patient with fever (functional asplenia vulnerability). CXR confirms ACS criteria. None of these require waiting — bedside-nursing actions plus standing orders.

opts.text: Start around-the-clock multimodal analgesia: morphine PCA basal 1 mg/hr + demand 0.5 mg q10min; ketorolac 30 mg IV; acetaminophen 1 g IV; pain reassessment q1h
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. Adequate pain control isn't optional — it's part of the ACS treatment (enables deep breathing, IS, mobilization). Scheduled and PCA, not PRN. Multimodal reduces total opioid exposure while increasing analgesia.

opts.text: Maintenance IVF D5 ½NS at 1.25x (~125 mL/hr) — NO boluses; bedside warming; incentive spirometry q1h while awake
opts.rat: #3 — Time-critical. Conservative fluid strategy + warming + IS are the bedside nursing interventions that interrupt the vaso-occlusive cycle. No fluid boluses even with tachycardia. Cold avoidance. IS reduces atelectasis.

opts.text: Hematology consultation; order simple transfusion 1 unit PRBC (antigen-matched, leukoreduced, sickle-negative) targeting Hgb 9–10; have exchange transfusion ready on standby
opts.rat: #4 — Definitive intervention. Transfusion decision is hematology-driven; the goal Hgb ceiling 9–10 avoids hyperviscosity. Exchange transfusion (via apheresis) on standby if ACS progresses. Coordinate with blood bank for antigen-matched units.

opts.text: Patient/family teaching on triggers (cold + dehydration + infection clustering) and hydroxyurea adherence; case management for HU support; school nurse coordination; hematology follow-up in 1 week; document and address past undertreatment concerns
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management and prevention. Once acute pathway is in motion, the long-term levers (HU adherence, trigger avoidance, communication, advocacy against bias) determine the trajectory of the disease. Nursing leadership essential.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Hour 12: SpO2 96% on 2 L NC (weaning), temp 100.0°F, pain 4/10 with PCA, post-transfusion Hgb 9.6 g/dL, repeat CXR shows resolving RLL infiltrate
opts.rat: Excellent trajectory. The bundle is working — oxygenation improving, pain controlled, infection resolving, transfusion successful. Continue current plan; wean O2 to RA; transition PCA to PO opioids; complete antibiotic course; plan discharge in 3–5 days.

opts.text: Day 5: discharged home on oral opioids (tapering plan), hydroxyurea resumed at home dose, school nurse coordination meeting scheduled, hematology follow-up in 1 week, case management has set up pill organizer + smartphone reminders + structured medication refill
opts.rat: Successful disposition with strong prevention bundle. The school nurse coordination addresses the trigger that produced this admission (cold weather sports without hydration). HU adherence support reduces future crisis frequency. Family report having felt heard about past undertreatment concerns.

opts.text: 3-month outpatient follow-up: no crises since discharge, HU adherence >95% by pill count, HbF level 18% (up from 8%), reports feeling 'best in years,' returning to sports with hydration plan
opts.rat: Successful trajectory. Sustained HU adherence produces measurable disease modification (rising HbF, fewer crises). The bedside admission has translated into long-term gains because the system (school, family, hematology, case management) is supporting the patient. Continue current plan; routine clinic visits.

opts.text: Hour 4: SpO2 dropping to 82% on 4 L NC, new LEFT lung infiltrate on CXR, RR 38, lethargic, lactate climbing to 4.8 — ACS progressing despite simple transfusion
opts.rat: ACS progression — escalation indication. EXCHANGE TRANSFUSION via apheresis NOW (goal HbS <30%); transfer to PICU for HFNC or BiPAP; consider intubation if respiratory failure continues; reassess infection coverage (broaden antibiotics if MRSA/Pseudomonas concern); call hematology and apheresis team. Mortality of severe ACS is significant — aggressive escalation.

opts.text: Hour 8: new focal weakness right side, expressive aphasia, GCS drops from 14 to 12 — concern for sickle cell stroke
opts.rat: STROKE in sickle cell — neurologic emergency. Stat MRI/MRA; emergency EXCHANGE TRANSFUSION (goal HbS <30%); avoid tPA (acute sickling lesions are not tPA targets); neurology consultation; ICU admission. Stroke is a known complication of sickle cell disease; ~10% of HbSS children have stroke. Chronic transfusion or HU intensification for secondary prevention.

opts.text: Hour 12: BP drops to 78/40 with mottled extremities, lactate 5.8, despite maintenance IVF; ICU intensivist considers vasopressor over additional fluid boluses
opts.rat: Septic-shock physiology developing — escalation. Norepinephrine drip 0.05 mcg/kg/min titrated to MAP ≥65; CAUTIOUS additional fluid (small 250 mL challenges rather than 30 mL/kg bolus given ACS risk); broaden antibiotics; central line; ICU intensivist at bedside. The conservative fluid strategy is harder when shock develops but remains important — supplement with vasopressor.

opts.text: Day 7: develops large gallstone-related cholecystitis from pigment stones (chronic hemolysis); needs cholecystectomy
opts.rat: Pigment gallstones are a known complication of chronic hemolysis (~50% of HbSS children by adolescence). Cholecystectomy when symptomatic. Perioperative management: pre-op simple transfusion to goal Hgb 9–10, careful temperature/hydration/oxygenation management, ACS prophylaxis with IS, post-op pain control without inducing splinting.

opts.text: Day 2: bedside nurse on the night shift writes order for 'morphine 2 mg IV q4h PRN' without scheduled dosing; patient pain rises to 8/10 by morning
opts.rat: Bias-driven undertreatment. The night shift order is the documented pattern. ADVOCATE for the scheduled around-the-clock plan; communicate the patient's history of past undertreatment and the current adequate plan; document the pain response and the lapse in care; if appropriate, raise to charge nurse / clinical educator / sickle cell program leadership. Systems-level improvement is part of the standard of care.

opts.text: Mildly tender lower extremities for 1 week post-discharge despite resolved primary pain crisis
opts.rat: Mild residual post-VOC pain is expected for days to weeks after a major crisis. Acetaminophen and NSAID for breakthrough; gradual return to activity; avoid the triggers that precipitated the original event. Most patients have intermittent baseline pain between crises — chronic pain syndromes are common in sickle cell.

opts.text: 5-year follow-up: enrolled in clinical trial for gene therapy (lovotibeglogene autotemcel); successful transplant; HbS now <30% with HbF significantly elevated; pain crisis frequency reduced by >90%
opts.rat: Realistic long-term outcome with cutting-edge therapy. FDA-approved gene therapies (lovotibeglogene autotemcel — exa-cel; exagamglogene autotemcel — exa-cel; etc.) and hematopoietic stem cell transplantation are increasingly available for severe sickle cell disease. They are not curative for all but produce substantial disease modification. The patient's strong adherence and family support make him an excellent candidate.

title: Severe Alcohol Withdrawal with Delirium Tremens
subtitle: CIWA-Ar Protocol, Thiamine-Before-Glucose, and the Benzo Front-Load

patient.name: T. Jameson
patient.admitDate: Hospital Day 2 (admitted from PACU after elective inguinal hernia repair)
patient.room: Med-Surg 4B → step-down for safety / sitter pending

vitals.time: Hospital Day 1, 1800 (24 hr post-op — early withdrawal noted)
Hospital Day 2, 0600 (CURRENT — 48 hr since last drink — florid DTs)

labs.n: CIWA-Ar score (Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol, revised)
labs.v: 32 — SEVERE withdrawal (nausea 5, tremor 6, sweats 7, anxiety 7, agitation 7, tactile 4, auditory 4, visual 5, headache 4, orientation/sensorium 3)
labs.r: 0–67 scale; <10 mild, 10–18 moderate, ≥19 severe; >15 needs benzodiazepine; reassess q1–4h
1.8–2.4 mg/dL; chronic alcohol use depletes Mg; low Mg WORSENS withdrawal and prevents K+ replacement
3.5–5.0 mEq/L; will NOT replete effectively until Mg corrected
2.5–4.5 mg/dL; chronic alcohol use depletes; refeeding-syndrome risk during nutritional repletion
136–145; mild hyponatremia from chronic alcohol effects on ADH and volume status
70–140; trending toward hypoglycemia — do NOT give dextrose until thiamine is on board (Wernicke prevention)
AST 10–35, ALT 7–35; AST > ALT with ratio >1.5 is the classic alcoholic-liver pattern
Albumin 3.5–5.0; INR <1.1; total bili <1.2 — synthetic and excretory liver dysfunction
<200 U/L; mild rhabdomyolysis from prolonged agitation, restraints, or seizure-like activity
<60 µg/dL; mildly elevated — concurrent hepatic encephalopathy could contribute to confusion in the differential
0.5–2.2 mmol/L; mildly elevated from agitation and increased muscle work
<60 U/L; rules out concurrent acute pancreatitis (alcoholic patients often have it)

labs.v: Ethanol level 28 mg/dL (residual, declining); cannabinoids positive; otherwise negative for opiates, cocaine, amphetamines, benzodiazepines
labs.r: Important to RULE OUT concurrent intoxication or polysubstance withdrawal

labs.v: Sinus tachycardia 142; QTc 480 ms (mildly prolonged — caution with QT-prolonging drugs); no ischemic ST changes; nl axis

labs.n: Alcohol use history (from family on Hospital Day 2)
labs.v: Family reports patient drinks ~1 liter vodka daily x 20+ years; patient previously reported only 'a few beers on weekends' to the surgeon at pre-op (typical minimization)
labs.r: True alcohol use history is essential for predicting withdrawal severity and pre-treatment planning

labs.v: Last drink ~36 hr before admission + 48 hr in hospital = ~84 hr (3.5 days) since last drink at this moment of presentation — squarely in the DT window (48–96 hr after last drink)
labs.r: Withdrawal timeline: 6–12 hr early autonomic + tremor; 12–24 hr hallucinations (mostly visual); 12–48 hr seizures (usually generalized tonic-clonic); 48–96 hr DELIRIUM TREMENS

labs.v: Disoriented to PLACE and TIME (knows name only); CAM-positive (acute fluctuating attention deficit + disorganized thinking); visual hallucinations (sees 'snakes on the bed' and 'bugs on the walls'); profound psychomotor agitation with attempts to climb out of bed; coarse tremor of both upper extremities; gait not tested (unsafe); no focal neurologic deficits; pupils 4 mm, reactive
labs.r: Oriented x3; calm and cooperative; no hallucinations; no tremor

nursesNote: 0600 — 54 y/o male admitted to Med-Surg 2 days ago after uneventful elective laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair. Pre-op assessment documented 'mild social alcohol use, a few beers on weekends' — patient minimized his use to the surgeon (a common and clinically important pattern). On Day 1 at 1800 the night-shift nurse noted mild tremor, anxiety, mild diaphoresis, BP up to 158/96, HR 108; CIWA-Ar score was 12 (mild–moderate withdrawal) — diazepam 10 mg PO given per the symptom-triggered protocol. The team called the patient's wife at home to get a fuller history; she disclosed that her husband has been drinking 'a liter of vodka, sometimes more' daily for the last 20+ years, has had two prior episodes of 'the shakes' in past hospitalizations, and one prior seizure he attributed to 'low blood sugar.' Last drink confirmed ~36 hr before admission. The team upgraded the level of withdrawal-risk monitoring but the case progressed overnight: at 0200 the patient began experiencing visual hallucinations (snakes on the bed, bugs on the walls), HR climbed to 132, BP to 178/104, temperature rose to 101.4°F, tremor became coarse and continuous. At 0500 he attempted to climb out of bed and pulled out his IV; rapid response was called; new peripheral IV established; lorazepam 4 mg IV given but agitation continued. At the 0600 reassessment NOW: CIWA-Ar is 32 (severe), patient is markedly agitated, attempting to disrobe and 'fight the bugs,' disoriented to place and time, HR 142, BP 184/108, T 102.3°F, RR 28, profusely diaphoretic, coarse tremor of all extremities, multiple superficial scratches from picking at imagined skin pests. NPO confirmed. SAFETY FIRST: 1:1 sitter at bedside; bed alarm activated; quiet room with dim lighting (overstimulation worsens DTs); restraints AVOIDED if at all possible (worsens agitation; only as last resort with strict orders and reassessment). THIAMINE 500 mg IV (high-dose for active Wernicke risk, NOT the standard 100 mg) just given BEFORE any glucose-containing IVF — Wernicke prevention is non-negotiable in any alcohol-use disorder patient. FOLATE 1 mg IV, multivitamin IV. MAGNESIUM sulfate 2 g IV running (low Mg 1.4 — must be corrected before potassium replacement). LORAZEPAM 4 mg IV q15 min — front-loading per the severe-withdrawal protocol (rather than waiting for CIWA-driven dosing); first 4 doses (16 mg total over 60 min) given so far with only modest improvement in CIWA score (28 → 25). The hospitalist and ICU consultant are at bedside. Plan being made now: (1) escalate to PHENOBARBITAL load 5–10 mg/kg IV for benzodiazepine-resistant DTs and/or (2) transfer to ICU for DEXMEDETOMIDINE drip for autonomic and agitation control (alpha-2 agonist; does NOT suppress respiratory drive; does NOT prevent seizures so benzo MUST continue). Cardiology aware (QTc 480, baseline tachycardia, AST/ALT elevation, electrolyte derangements). Wife at bedside, exhausted and worried; she had not appreciated the severity of his drinking.
